Scenario: You and your cousin have one hour before dinner to play a board game together, and you both want to finish a complete game so you can tell your grandmother about it at dinner like she asked. You want to play chess where you can think through your own moves carefully and make your own decisions, but your cousin wants to play a wild dice game full of surprises and excitement. You must pick one game to play right now.
Scenario_rev: You and your cousin have one hour before dinner to play a board game together, and you both want to finish a complete game so you can tell your grandmother about it at dinner like she asked. You want to play a wild dice game full of surprises and excitement, but your cousin wants to play chess where they can think through their own moves carefully and make their own decisions. You must pick one game to play right now.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values independent thinking and autonomous decision-making (choosing your own moves in chess), while Stimulation values novelty, excitement, and unpredictable experiences (random dice outcomes and surprises).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both you and your cousin directly benefit equally from completing a game together—you both get to enjoy the activity and fulfill your grandmother's request, with no competition or personal sacrifice involved.

Scenario: You and your friend are making a 3-minute video for your school's film contest where first place wins a $50 gift card to split between you. You must pick one filming method today because the contest deadline is Monday.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating and following your own plan with independent control over decisions, while Stimulation values spontaneous excitement and unpredictable experiences that create novelty.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both you and your friend share the same goal of winning the contest and would split the $50 prize equally, with both directly benefiting from success without competing against each other.

Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both you and your classmate share the goal of winning the science fair and would both equally enjoy having your project displayed, with no competition between you or personal sacrifice required.

Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating an organized system with clear guidance that you independently design, allowing scouts to cross safely following your planned instructions. Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you volunteer your time after school to create a practice route that helps 20 fellow scouts develop orienteering skills. Your effort serves the troop's educational goals rather than providing direct personal benefits.

Scenario: You and your thrill-seeking neighbor are the two teen volunteers managing the community center's new skateboard ramp during after-school hours (3-5 PM weekdays), and the director asked you to create the usage schedule for the 25 kids who signed up. You must decide whether to create a structured schedule with clear 15-minute time slots where each skater knows exactly when they can practice specific areas, or allow open skating where anyone can use any section spontaneously with no set turns. You need to post one finalized schedule on the bulletin board by tomorrow morning so families can plan their week.
Scenario_rev: You and your structured neighbor are the two teen volunteers managing the community center's new skateboard ramp during after-school hours (3-5 PM weekdays), and the director asked you to create the usage schedule for the 25 kids who signed up. You must decide whether to create a structured schedule with clear 15-minute time slots where each skater knows exactly when they can practice specific areas, or allow open skating where anyone can use any section spontaneously with no set turns. You need to post one finalized schedule on the bulletin board by tomorrow morning so families can plan their week.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating an organized system with predictable time slots that you control and design, giving each skater clear independent access. Stimulation values spontaneous, unpredictable open skating where exciting interactions and varied combinations happen naturally without rigid structure.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you volunteer your after-school time to supervise and organize the ramp for 25 younger kids in the community. Your effort and time commitment serves families who need after-school activities, not your personal interests.

Scenario: You and your excitement-loving friend must create a 5-minute social media safety presentation for 60 fifth-graders at Tuesday's assembly, and you need to choose between two formats. You can either produce a carefully edited pre-recorded video lesson that you script and control completely, or perform a live, unpredictable skit where students act out risky online situations and you both must respond in real time. You must agree on one format tonight and have everything ready by tomorrow morning.
Scenario_rev: You and your careful-planning friend must create a 5-minute social media safety presentation for 60 fifth-graders at Tuesday's assembly, and you need to choose between two formats. You can either produce a carefully edited pre-recorded video lesson that your friend scripts and controls completely, or perform a live, unpredictable skit where students act out risky online situations and you both must respond in real time. You must agree on one format tonight and have everything ready by tomorrow morning.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating a controlled, pre-recorded presentation that you independently script and edit to deliver your exact message. Stimulation values live, spontaneous performance with unpredictable student interactions that create excitement and novelty through real-time responses.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you volunteer your time to educate 60 younger students about online safety, serving their wellbeing. Your effort benefits others' digital literacy and protection rather than providing personal rewards.

Scenario: You and your thrill-loving cousin inherited your grandmother's backyard garden plot (20 feet by 20 feet) and agreed to donate all the produce to the community food bank this summer, but first you must decide on a planting plan by this Saturday when the garden center's seed donation expires. You can either plant labeled rows of reliable vegetables (tomatoes, carrots, lettuce) with a clear map and watering schedule that you organize, or create a mixed surprise garden where you plant mystery seed packets and experimental combinations without planning what grows where. You have only Saturday morning to plant everything before the weather turns too hot for seeds to sprout.
Scenario_rev: You and your methodical cousin inherited your grandmother's backyard garden plot (20 feet by 20 feet) and agreed to donate all the produce to the community food bank this summer, but first you must decide on a planting plan by this Saturday when the garden center's seed donation expires. You have only Saturday morning to plant everything before the weather turns too hot for seeds to sprout.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating an organized garden system with clear rows and a watering schedule that you independently plan and control for predictable results. Stimulation values an experimental, unpredictable planting approach with mystery seeds that create surprise and novelty as unknown plants emerge.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you commit your time and inherited garden space to grow food for the community food bank, helping families in need. Your effort and sacrifice of the garden's potential benefits others rather than yourselves.

Scenario: You and your adventure-seeking friend are student safety ambassadors, and the building manager asked you both to create a poster explaining the fire evacuation route for the 30 families in your apartment building before next month's fire drill. You must decide whether to design a clear map with numbered steps, exit arrows, and the meeting point clearly labeled, or create an exciting comic-style poster showing a dramatic evacuation story with surprising twists that demonstrates the route. You have only this weekend to design, print, and post one poster type on each floor's bulletin board.
Scenario_rev: You and your safety-focused friend are student safety ambassadors, and the building manager asked you both to create a poster explaining the fire evacuation route for the 30 families in your apartment building before next month's fire drill. You have only this weekend to design, print, and post one poster type on each floor's bulletin board.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values designing a clear, organized safety map that you independently create with precise instructions for predictable understanding. Stimulation values a dramatic, story-based poster with exciting plot twists and unexpected elements that capture attention through novelty.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you volunteer your weekend time to improve safety for 30 families in your building. Your effort serves the community's wellbeing and emergency preparedness rather than providing personal benefits.

Scenario: You and your fun-loving classmate are junior counselors at the five-day summer art camp that raises money for the children's hospital, and the director asked you both to plan Monday's schedule for the 20 elementary-age campers. You must decide whether to create a structured timetable with four specific art projects at set times (painting 9-10 AM, clay 10-11 AM, etc.) that you post on the wall, or design a flexible day where you announce surprise activities every hour and kids move between stations spontaneously. You need to email the director your one finalized schedule tonight so she can prepare materials and supplies.
Scenario_rev: You and your organized classmate are junior counselors at the five-day summer art camp that raises money for the children's hospital, and the director asked you both to plan Monday's schedule for the 20 elementary-age campers. You need to email the director your one finalized schedule tonight so she can prepare materials and supplies.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating a clear, organized schedule that you independently plan and control, giving campers predictable structure. Stimulation values spontaneous, unpredictable activity announcements that create excitement and novelty throughout the day without rigid timing.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you volunteer as junior counselors to run a camp that raises funds for sick children at the hospital. Your time and effort serve the fundraising cause and provide enrichment for 20 younger campers rather than benefiting yourselves.

Scenario: You and your excitement-seeking teammate are the two eighth-grade captains of the school robotics club, preparing 12 younger members for the regional rescue robot competition in six weeks. You must decide on the practice plan for your three weekly after-school sessions: either repeat the same official competition course layout each time so members master the specific challenges through your organized drills, or create different surprise obstacle courses each week to build adaptability with unpredictable problems. You need to tell your faculty advisor your training plan tomorrow so he can reserve the right gym space and equipment.
Scenario_rev: You and your systematic teammate are the two eighth-grade captains of the school robotics club, preparing 12 younger members for the regional rescue robot competition in six weeks. You need to tell your faculty advisor your training plan tomorrow so he can reserve the right gym space and equipment.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating a consistent, structured practice plan where you independently organize repeated drills on the known course for mastery. Stimulation values varied, unpredictable obstacle courses that create novelty and excitement while building general problem-solving through surprising challenges.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you volunteer extra time as captains to train 12 younger club members for competition success. Your coaching effort serves the team's development and the school's robotics program rather than providing personal competitive advantages.

Scenario: You and your risk-loving older sibling are setting up your family's three old tablets for the neighborhood Tech Lending Library at the community center, which will loan devices to five younger kids (ages 6-8) whose families cannot afford them. Your mom said you have this Sunday afternoon to choose one setup approach: either pre-install specific educational apps (math games, reading programs, drawing tools) with written guides you create explaining each one, or install the app store with light parental controls so kids can freely explore and download apps themselves. The tablets will be distributed Monday morning.
Scenario_rev: You and your cautious older sibling are setting up your family's three old tablets for the neighborhood Tech Lending Library at the community center, which will loan devices to five younger kids (ages 6-8) whose families cannot afford them. Your mom said you have this Sunday afternoon to choose one setup approach: either pre-install specific educational apps (math games, reading programs, drawing tools) with written guides your sibling creates explaining each one, or install the app store with light parental controls so kids can freely explore and download apps themselves. The tablets will be distributed Monday morning.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values pre-selecting and organizing specific apps with clear guides that you independently create, giving kids structured choices you control. Stimulation values open access to the app store where kids can freely discover and download new apps, creating unpredictable exploration and surprise.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you donate your family's tablets and volunteer your Sunday to help five kids whose families lack resources. Your effort serves these children's educational access and opportunity rather than providing personal benefits.

Scenario: You and your thrill-loving friend are organizing this Saturday's park cleanup volunteer event for the town Earth Day celebration, expecting 30 community volunteers to help clean the 5-acre Riverside Park in three hours. You must decide on the approach: either divide the park into six labeled zones on a map you create, assigning teams to specific areas with clear trash targets (Zone 1: playground - bottles and wrappers, etc.), or use a flexible roaming approach where volunteers pick up trash anywhere they find it with surprise announcements about new focus areas every 30 minutes. You need to submit your one event plan to the town volunteer coordinator by Thursday so she can prepare materials.
Scenario_rev: You and your structured friend are organizing this Saturday's park cleanup volunteer event for the town Earth Day celebration, expecting 30 community volunteers to help clean the 5-acre Riverside Park in three hours. You must decide on the approach: either divide the park into six labeled zones on a map your friend creates, assigning teams to specific areas with clear trash targets (Zone 1: playground - bottles and wrappers, etc.), or use a flexible roaming approach where volunteers pick up trash anywhere they find it with surprise announcements about new focus areas every 30 minutes. You need to submit your one event plan to the town volunteer coordinator by Thursday so she can prepare materials.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating an organized zone system with a clear map and assignments that you independently plan for systematic coverage. Stimulation values a flexible, spontaneous approach with surprise announcements about new focus areas that create unpredictability and variety throughout the event.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you volunteer your time to organize a cleanup that benefits the entire community's environment and Earth Day celebration. Your planning effort serves the town and 30 volunteers rather than providing personal rewards.

Scenario: You and Marie volunteer to manage the animal shelter's adoption event for two weeks. You want to stick with your detailed plan that carefully lists which animals to feature each day, but Marie wants to keep changing the schedule to try exciting new activities like surprise celebrity visits or flash adoption sales. If you don't get 50 animals adopted, the shelter will have to close its doors permanently and the animals will be moved to a crowded city facility.
Scenario_rev: You and your organized co-volunteer volunteer to manage the animal shelter's adoption event for two weeks. Your co-volunteer wants to stick with their detailed plan that carefully lists which animals to feature each day, but you want to keep changing the schedule to try exciting new activities like surprise celebrity visits or flash adoption sales. If you don't get 50 animals adopted, the shelter will have to close its doors permanently and the animals will be moved to a crowded city facility.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values following your own carefully thought-out independent plan and maintaining control over the approach. Stimulation values exciting, varied, and novel experiences that bring unpredictability and change to the event.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both you and Marie are volunteering your time and effort without personal gain to help animals in need. The personal cost is the substantial time commitment and potential stress, while the benefit goes entirely to the shelter animals and community.

Scenario: You and your roommate have $300 in shared household funds and one free weekend to take a trip together before your busy work schedules begin. You must agree on either booking a resort with a spa, pool, and entertainment (relaxing fun) or renting a quiet cabin with art supplies and hiking trails (space for personal creative projects). The shared budget only covers one option, and you both need to go together since neither can afford to travel alone.
Scenario_rev: You and your organizer roommate have $300 in shared household funds and one free weekend to take a trip together before your busy work schedules begin. You must agree on either booking a resort with a spa, pool, and entertainment (relaxing fun) or renting a quiet cabin with art supplies and hiking trails (space for personal creative projects). The shared budget only covers one option, and you both need to go together since neither can afford to travel alone.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values immediate pleasure and relaxation through ready-made entertainment, while Self-direction values independence and creative self-expression through open-ended activities. Choosing the resort prioritizes sensory pleasure over personal growth opportunities.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because they're using shared funds and need each other to afford the trip. If they succeed in choosing together, both get a weekend getaway; if they don't agree, neither can travel.

Scenario: You and your cousin are planning your shared birthday party at a rented hall for 3 hours this Saturday. You must decide together whether to fill the space with bounce houses and party games (maximum fun) or set up maker stations with building blocks, art supplies, and invention corners (kids create their own activities). Your shared $200 birthday money from grandparents only covers one setup style.
Scenario_rev: You and your party-planning cousin are planning your shared birthday party at a rented hall for 3 hours this Saturday. You must decide together whether to fill the space with bounce houses and party games (maximum fun) or set up maker stations with building blocks, art supplies, and invention corners (kids create their own activities). Your shared $200 birthday money from grandparents only covers one setup style.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values immediate enjoyment through ready-made entertainment like bounce houses, while Self-direction values creative freedom and self-designed activities. The choice determines whether guests experience passive fun or active creation.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because it's their shared birthday celebration and shared gift money. If they choose together successfully, both get a party; if not, there's no party for either.

Scenario: You and your teammate won a $150 scholarship that covers club fees for both of you, but only for one club this semester. You must decide together whether to join the casual gaming club (play fun pre-made games twice a week) or the coding club (learn to program and design your own games from scratch). Registration closes Friday, and your choice determines how you both spend after-school time until June.
Scenario_rev: You and your fellow teammate won a $150 scholarship that covers club fees for both of you, but only for one club this semester. You must decide together whether to join the casual gaming club (play fun pre-made games twice a week) or the coding club (learn to program and design your own games from scratch). Registration closes Friday, and your choice determines how you both spend after-school time until June.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values immediate enjoyment from playing finished games, while Self-direction values learning new skills and creating original projects independently. The gaming club offers instant gratification while coding requires effort before reward.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because the scholarship only covers one club for both students together. If they choose successfully, both get free club membership; if they disagree, neither can use the scholarship.

Scenario: You and your sibling are redecorating your shared bedroom with a $200 budget your parents gave you both as a joint gift. You must agree on either creating a cozy entertainment zone (TV, beanbags, snack shelf, game console) or an open creative workspace (desk, art supplies, building area, bookshelf). The room's small size and budget only allow one complete setup.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values comfort and entertainment for relaxation and pleasure, while Self-direction values tools for independent creative work and personal projects. Each setup serves fundamentally different purposes for how the shared space is used.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because they share one bedroom and one budget from their parents. If they choose together, both get a redesigned room; if not, the money can't be spent and the room stays unchanged.

Scenario: You and your classmate share a study table in the library, and the librarian requires you both to agree on one rule for your table this semester: either quiet independent time (for reading, writing, or personal projects) or social collaboration time (for group discussion, sharing ideas, and working together). Scenario_rev: You and your classmate share a study table in the library, and the librarian requires you both to agree on one rule for your table this semester: either quiet independent time (for reading, writing, or personal projects) or social collaboration time (for group discussion, sharing ideas, and working together). Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values social interaction and the pleasure of chatting and sharing, while Self-direction values quiet autonomy for independent work and self-guided projects. The rule determines whether the table supports social enjoyment or solitary focus.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because they share one assigned table and the librarian requires a joint decision. If they agree, both get their preferred table environment; if not, the librarian will assign a random rule.

Scenario: You and your coworker are team representatives who must propose one monthly team event to your manager, who will fund $500 for whichever format you both agree on. Scenario_rev: You and your coworker are team representatives who must propose one monthly team event to your manager, who will fund $500 for whichever format you both agree on. Your joint written proposal is due Friday morning.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values enjoyable social experiences and immediate pleasure from entertainment, while Self-direction values learning opportunities and developing independent capabilities. The night out prioritizes fun while the workshop prioritizes personal growth.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because the manager requires one joint proposal from the two elected representatives. If they submit a unified proposal, the whole team gets monthly funded events; if not, there's no team budget.

Scenario: You and your neighbor are organizing a 2-hour park clean-up with 15 kid volunteers and a $100 budget on Saturday. You must decide together whether to make it a fun party (music, games, pizza prizes for everyone) or a design-your-own event (kids choose which areas to clean, create their own team roles, and invent sorting systems). Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor are organizing a 2-hour park clean-up with 15 kid volunteers and a $100 budget on Saturday. The volunteer sign-up email needs one clear description by tomorrow.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values making the event enjoyable through entertainment and treats, while Self-direction values giving kids freedom to make their own choices and design their own approach. The party format provides ready-made fun while the design format requires kids to create their own structure.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because they're co-organizers using a shared budget and volunteer list. If they send one unified description, they get volunteers; if they can't agree, there's no clean-up event.

Scenario: You and your lab partner must choose one science fair project to work on together for a shared grade due in two weeks. You can either build a colorful baking soda volcano that erupts with foam (fun and easy presentation) or assemble a programmable robot kit that requires problem-solving and lets you design custom movements (challenging self-directed building). You must tell your teacher your choice tomorrow morning.

Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because the teacher assigned one shared project and one shared grade for both students. If they choose together, both get graded; if not, both fail the assignment.

Scenario: You and your co-captain must design tomorrow's 90-minute soccer practice plan that your coach will use. Your joint plan is due to the coach by 6 PM tonight.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values the immediate enjoyment and fun of playing games, while Self-direction values the independence and creative control of designing original strategies. Scrimmages provide instant pleasure while drill design requires thoughtful planning.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because the coach requires one unified plan from both captains. If they submit one plan, the team practices tomorrow; if not, the coach cancels practice for everyone.

Scenario: You and your neighbor are planning a community picnic for 30 families with a $150 budget and 3 hours at the park on Sunday. You must decide together whether to spend everything on delicious catered food and treats (maximum taste and variety) or use $75 for basic food and $75 for a cooking station where kids make their own pizzas and decorate cupcakes (hands-on creating). Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor are planning a community picnic for 30 families with a $150 budget and 3 hours at the park on Sunday. Your shopping list is due tomorrow morning.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values maximizing taste pleasure and food enjoyment through quality catering, while Self-direction values creative independence and self-expression through hands-on cooking. More money for catering means less opportunity for kids to create their own food.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because they're co-organizers sharing one budget and one event. If they submit one shopping list, the picnic happens; if not, there's no organized food plan.

Scenario: You and your brother have 2 hours of shared tablet time before it needs charging, and you both want to use it together. You must agree on either watching funny videos and playing simple games (easy entertainment) or using the drawing app to create your own comic book story with characters you design (creative project). Scenario_rev: You and your brother have 2 hours of shared tablet time before it needs charging, and you both want to use it together. You must agree on either watching funny videos and playing simple games (easy entertainment) or using the drawing app to create your own comic book story with characters you design (creative project). Once you start one activity, there's only enough battery to finish that choice.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values passive entertainment and immediate laughs from ready-made content, while Self-direction values active creation and expressing original ideas. Videos provide instant pleasure while comic-making requires creative effort and planning.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must agree because they share one tablet and want to use it together. If they choose one activity, both get screen time; if they argue, the battery dies during the conflict.

Scenario: You and your fellow class representative must decide on your grade's end-of-year field trip since you both won the planning election. You must choose between a water park with slides and wave pools (maximum fun and excitement) or a science center where students explore exhibits and design their own experiments at interactive stations (self-guided discovery). Scenario_rev: You and your fellow class representative must decide on your grade's end-of-year field trip since you both won the planning election. The school needs your joint decision by Friday to book the $800 trip for 60 students.

Scenario: You and your sibling have one free weekend bus pass to share before it expires Sunday night. You want to visit the amusement park with roller coasters and arcade games for instant fun, but your sibling wants to go to the art workshop center where you each create and design your own pottery project. Scenario_rev: You and your sibling have one free weekend bus pass to share before it expires Sunday night. You must agree on one destination by Friday morning to reserve your spots.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism seeks immediate sensory pleasure and entertainment from rides and games. Self-direction values creative independence and making personal artistic choices without a preset fun structure.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both siblings must agree on one shared destination to use the single bus pass together, and both benefit from the chosen activity. Neither can go alone since they only have one pass between them.

Scenario: You and your roommate must create a joint two-week study schedule before final exams that determines whether you both pass and keep your scholarship dorm room. You want scheduled gaming breaks and music listening time every hour for stress relief and fun, but your roommate wants each person to choose their own study methods and break times independently without a fixed schedule. You must submit one agreed schedule to your resident advisor by Monday.
Scenario_rev: You and your roommate must create a joint two-week study schedule before final exams that determines whether you both pass and keep your scholarship dorm room. You must submit one agreed schedule to your resident advisor by Monday.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism wants structured pleasure breaks for immediate enjoyment and relaxation. Self-direction values freedom to choose personal study approaches and timing without imposed schedules.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both roommates must agree on one shared schedule to satisfy the requirement and both keep their housing if they succeed. Their individual exam performance depends on following their joint plan.

Scenario: You and your lab partner must create one joint science experiment about daily habits and compete for the higher individual grade based on whose data section the teacher rates as more thorough. You want to track fun activities like favorite snacks eaten and video games played with simple yes/no charts, but your partner wants to track self-chosen personal goals where each person designs their own measurement categories. You must submit one agreed experiment tracking sheet by Monday.
Scenario_rev: You and your lab partner must create one joint science experiment about daily habits and compete for the higher individual grade based on whose data section the teacher rates as more thorough. Your partner wants to track fun activities like favorite snacks eaten and video games played with simple yes/no charts, but you want to track self-chosen personal goals where each person designs their own measurement categories. You must submit one agreed experiment tracking sheet by Monday.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism prefers tracking enjoyable, easy activities with simple recording methods. Self-direction wants autonomy in choosing what to measure and creating personalized tracking systems.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both students must agree on one shared experiment format to submit, while competing for individual grades on their separate data analysis sections. The competition is for higher teacher ratings on individual work within the joint project.

Scenario: You and the trip coordinator must design one three-hour museum schedule for your class field trip, competing for whose name gets listed as 'lead planner' on the permission slip parents see. You want fixed time slots for the planetarium show, gift shop, and lunch break so everyone enjoys the best attractions together, but the coordinator wants open exploration periods where each student picks their own exhibits independently. You must give the teacher one agreed schedule by Wednesday.
Scenario_rev: You and your classmate must design one three-hour museum schedule for your class field trip, competing for whose name gets listed as 'lead planner' on the permission slip parents see. Your classmate wants fixed time slots for the planetarium show, gift shop, and lunch break so everyone enjoys the best attractions together, but you want open exploration periods where each student picks their own exhibits independently. You must give the teacher one agreed schedule by Wednesday.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism seeks guaranteed access to enjoyable, popular attractions with minimal effort. Self-direction values personal choice and freedom to explore based on individual curiosity and interests.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both must collaborate to create one schedule the teacher will approve, while competing for the 'lead planner' recognition credit. The competition is for whose name appears as the primary designer on official trip documents.

Scenario: You and your sibling must agree on movie night rules for the next four Fridays, competing for who gets an extra hour of TV time on weekends based on whose rule idea your parents like better. You want to watch the same comedy series every week because it's relaxing and guaranteed funny, but your sibling wants each person to pick different movies weekly so everyone discovers new films independently. You must tell your parents one agreed rule before Friday dinner.
Scenario_rev: You and your sibling must agree on movie night rules for the next four Fridays, competing for who gets an extra hour of TV time on weekends based on whose rule idea your parents like better. Your sibling wants to watch the same comedy series every week because it's relaxing and guaranteed funny, but you want each person to pick different movies weekly so everyone discovers new films independently. You must tell your parents one agreed rule before Friday dinner.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism wants predictable, easy entertainment that guarantees pleasure without risk. Self-direction values variety and personal autonomy in making individual weekly selections.

Conflict Rationale: The flower garden with benches creates a beautiful space for passive enjoyment and relaxation (hedonism), while individual vegetable plots enable neighbors to make independent choices about what to grow and how to garden (self-direction). Limited resources allow only one garden design.
Cooperation Rationale: Both volunteers sacrifice their Saturday free time and physical effort to create a community space instead of relaxing. This is altruistic because they give up their weekend leisure to improve the neighborhood for others' benefit.

Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents must collaborate to create a single shared act, and both directly benefit equally from the pizza party if their class wins the competition.

You must agree on one rule system to print on the 50 flyers for Friday's parent meeting.
Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor volunteer your Saturday to plan a kids' bike race that raises money for the children's hospital, giving up your weekend. You want simpler guidelines that let parent volunteers adapt to different situations, but your neighbor wants detailed rules about helmet checks, course boundaries, and adult supervision at every turn to prevent any injuries. You must agree on one rule system to print on the 50 flyers for Friday's parent meeting.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values creating comprehensive safety procedures that ensure everything runs smoothly without accidents, while Self-direction values flexible guidelines that allow people to make their own decisions.
Cooperation Rationale: Both people sacrifice their Saturday to organize an event that raises money for sick children rather than benefiting themselves.

Scenario: You and a colleague volunteer every weekend for six months to give free business consulting to five struggling small businesses in your town. You want to create a structured program with monthly goals and measurable progress so the businesses improve successfully, but your colleague wants each business owner to explore different solutions at their own pace. You must agree on one consulting approach before meeting all five owners tomorrow morning.
Scenario_rev: You and a colleague volunteer every weekend for six months to give free business consulting to five struggling small businesses in your town. You want each business owner to explore different solutions at their own pace, but your colleague wants to create a structured program with monthly goals and measurable progress so the businesses improve successfully. You must agree on one consulting approach before meeting all five owners tomorrow morning.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values creating clear milestones and proven methods that ensure the businesses actually improve, while Self-direction values letting owners discover their own solutions through independent exploration.
Cooperation Rationale: Both consultants sacrifice their weekends for six months unpaid to help businesses that are struggling, providing expertise that benefits others rather than earning income for themselves.

You want to create radio-friendly songs that will sell more copies and raise more money for the 50 families, but Sam wants to use experimental sounds that express the community's real emotions even if fewer people buy it. You must agree on one production style before recording starts Monday to finish by the fundraiser deadline.
Scenario_rev: You and your commercially-minded collaborator volunteer two months of unpaid work to produce a charity album where all sales go to families who lost homes in the recent flood. You want to use experimental sounds that express the community's real emotions even if fewer people buy it, but your collaborator wants to create radio-friendly songs that will sell more copies and raise more money for the 50 families. You must agree on one production style before recording starts Monday to finish by the fundraiser deadline.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values creating commercially successful songs that maximize fundraising impact, while Self-direction values authentic artistic expression even if it reaches fewer people.
Cooperation Rationale: Both people sacrifice two months of unpaid work to create an album where all money goes to flood victims rather than to themselves.

Scenario: You and your friend run free language tutoring every Saturday morning for 15 younger kids in your neighborhood, giving up your weekend time to help. Cooperation Rationale: Both tutors sacrifice their Saturday mornings every week to teach kids for free, helping others improve their English rather than using the time for themselves.

Scenario: You and a curator volunteer every weekend for three months to create a free community art exhibition that teaches art appreciation to low-income families who cannot afford museum tickets. Scenario: You and a volunteer teacher must create a free 6-lesson coding tutorial series for local middle school students, where you write the lesson scripts and practice exercises while the teacher records and edits the videos. You want to include quizzes after each lesson with score tracking and a completion certificate to show student progress, but the teacher wants students to freely explore coding concepts and build whatever projects interest them without any testing. You both need to donate 20 hours of work over two weeks to finish before the school's summer program starts.
Scenario_rev: You are a volunteer teacher and you must create a free 6-lesson coding tutorial series with another volunteer for local middle school students, where the other volunteer writes the lesson scripts and practice exercises while you record and edit the videos. Conflict Rationale: You value demonstrating measurable learning outcomes through structured assessments and certificates that prove achievement, while the teacher values giving students creative freedom to direct their own learning path without imposed benchmarks. Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both agents volunteer significant unpaid time and effort (20 hours each over two weeks) to create educational content for underserved students without receiving any direct benefit or compensation. The sacrifice is the donated time and work, not just suppressing a value preference.

Scenario: You are the band director and your assistant director are choosing the five songs for the spring concert program that must be printed next week. Conflict Rationale: You prioritize selecting impressive pieces that enhance your status and authority with the school board (Power), while your assistant director wants students to have freedom to play music they personally connect with (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: Both must agree on one song list for printing, and both benefit from a successful concert—you gain recognition from the board and your assistant gains from happy, motivated students. This is reciprocal because the shared goal directly benefits both agents.

Scenario: You are the operations director and the product team lead must decide on a launch approval process for your company's new product line. Conflict Rationale: You want centralized control where your authority governs all decisions (Power), while the team lead wants departments to operate independently without oversight (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: Both must agree and sign one governance document for the board meeting, and both directly benefit from the successful launch through career advancement and company growth. This is reciprocal because the shared goal benefits both agents equally.

Scenario: You and your friend co-manage a shared Instagram account posting art tutorials for your school's 100 art club members. Scenario: You and your friend run a shared gaming stream with 500 subscribers, and a game company offers you $300 to stream their new game three times per week for a month. Scenario: You and a senior volunteer coordinator must plan how your charity will deliver 500 food boxes to families by Friday. Scenario: You and Lucy, your housemate, must decide by Friday whether to convert your shared home office into a tutoring center where students would visit three evenings weekly, splitting $800 monthly income equally. Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both share the same space and would equally benefit from the income if they agree to proceed, or equally benefit from the unchanged quiet workspace if they decline. Both must consent to the same decision since they co-own the space usage.

Conflict Rationale: Security values the established commitment, financial predictability, and stable housing arrangement with guaranteed terms. Self-direction values the autonomy to make spontaneous life choices, explore opportunities, and remain unbounded by long-term commitments.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must sign the same lease or both decline it—the landlord requires joint commitment. Both benefit from shared rent savings if they sign, or both keep housing flexibility if they decline.

Conflict Rationale: Security values the established legal protections, formal support services, and guaranteed rights through regulated agency processes. Self-direction values the autonomy to personally negotiate terms, make individual choices, and maintain relationships without agency constraints.

Both benefit from a successful placement that meets their shared goal of finding appropriate adoptive parents, or both face consequences if the adoption plan falls through.

Scenario: You and your sister Maya must decide by tomorrow whether to go with your friend Lisa on a weekend trip to Barcelona where she plans to meet someone she's only talked to online for three weeks, or convince her to meet this person locally first at a coffee shop with both of you nearby. Scenario_rev: You and the psychiatric nurse must finalize one treatment plan by Friday for your shared patient Tom, who has severe depression and recently tried to harm himself. Conflict Rationale: Security values the structured safety protocols, supervised activities, and monitored routines that protect Tom from immediate self-harm risks. Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must sign the same integrated treatment plan for insurance approval and hospital implementation. Both benefit from Tom's recovery or both face professional consequences if treatment fails due to conflicting approaches in the unified plan.

You must choose between keeping the standard Monday-Friday 9-to-5 shifts that your store has used for five years, or trying your new flexible schedule where employees pick their own hours each week. Both of you must sign off on one schedule system, and the district will review it as part of both your performance evaluations.
Conflict Rationale: You want to keep the standard fixed schedule that the store has always followed (Conformity), while Devon wants to try a new system where employees choose their own hours (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both you and Devon's performance reviews depend on submitting an approved schedule, and you must collaborate by agreeing on one system that satisfies district requirements.

Scenario: You and your co-parent must choose one Saturday activity for your two children starting this weekend and continuing for three months. You must decide between enrolling them in structured soccer lessons that follow the town recreation program used by most neighborhood families, or unstructured park time where the kids create their own games and activities. Both of you will take the children together every Saturday morning.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-parent must choose one Saturday activity for your two children starting this weekend and continuing for three months. You must decide between enrolling them in structured soccer lessons that follow the town recreation program used by most neighborhood families, or unstructured park time where the kids create their own games and activities. Both of you will take the children together every Saturday morning.
Conflict Rationale: You want structured lessons that follow the standard town program (Conformity), while your co-parent wants unstructured time where kids direct their own play (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both parents share the goal of providing quality activities for their children and both must participate together every Saturday, requiring agreement on one approach.

Scenario: You and your co-parent must agree on one set of video game rules for your two teenagers before school starts next Monday. You must choose between the school's recommended schedule of two hours on weeknights and three hours on weekends that the parent handbook suggests, or a flexible system where the teens manage their own gaming time as long as homework is finished. Both of you will enforce whichever rules you choose every day.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-parent must agree on one set of video game rules for your two teenagers before school starts next Monday. You must choose between the school's recommended schedule of two hours on weeknights and three hours on weekends that the parent handbook suggests, or a flexible system where the teens manage their own gaming time as long as homework is finished. Both of you will enforce whichever rules you choose every day.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the school's recommended gaming schedule (Conformity), while your co-parent wants a flexible system where teens self-manage their time (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both parents share the goal of establishing healthy gaming habits for their teenagers and both must consistently enforce the same rules, requiring agreement on one approach.

Scenario: You and Tyler are both admins of your 20-person online chat group, and you must post new rules by Friday to stop the arguments that are splitting the group. You must choose between rules that match the platform's standard community guidelines that similar groups use, or minimal rules that only remove illegal content so members can joke freely about any topic. The group voted that they'll only follow rules if both admins agree and post them together.
Scenario_rev: You and your fellow admin are both admins of your 20-person online chat group, and you must post new rules by Friday to stop the arguments that are splitting the group. You must choose between rules that match the platform's standard community guidelines that similar groups use, or minimal rules that only remove illegal content so members can joke freely about any topic. The group voted that they'll only follow rules if both admins agree and post them together.
Conflict Rationale: You want to adopt the platform's standard community guidelines that established groups follow (Conformity), while Tyler wants minimal rules that let members joke freely without boundaries (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both you and Tyler share responsibility for managing the group and both benefit from stopping the conflicts, requiring collaboration to post one agreed set of rules that members will follow.

Scenario: You and Dr. Patel must finalize the medication schedule for your joint diabetes research study before Monday's review board meeting. You must choose between the hospital's standard twice-daily dosing protocol that all previous studies used, or a flexible patient-choice schedule that Dr. Patel designed where patients take medication when it fits their routine. Both your names are on the study registration, and you both need successful completion for your next grant applications.
Scenario_rev: You and your research partner must finalize the medication schedule for your joint diabetes research study before Monday's review board meeting. You must choose between the hospital's standard twice-daily dosing protocol that all previous studies used, or a flexible patient-choice schedule that you designed where patients take medication when it fits their routine. Both your names are on the study registration, and you both need successful completion for your next grant applications.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the hospital's standard twice-daily protocol used in all previous studies (Conformity), while Dr. Patel wants a flexible patient-choice schedule (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both you and Dr. Patel are co-leads who directly benefit from successful study completion for future grant applications, requiring collaboration on one agreed protocol that the review board will approve.

Scenario: You and your friend Alex bought a single two-movie ticket package that expires today, and you must watch both movies together. Halfway through the first movie, you both find it boring. You must decide whether to stay and finish both movies as planned, or leave now to try a different theater showing movies you'd enjoy more, but you'll lose the $30 package and might not find available seats on this busy Saturday.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend bought a single two-movie ticket package that expires today, and you must watch both movies together. Halfway through the first movie, you both find it boring. You must decide whether to stay and finish both movies as planned, or leave now to try a different theater showing movies you'd enjoy more, but you'll lose the $30 package and might not find available seats on this busy Saturday.
Conflict Rationale: You want to stay and complete the plan you both made (Conformity), while Alex wants to leave and find movies you'd enjoy more even if it means changing plans (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because you and Alex both benefit from an enjoyable movie experience and must make the same choice together since you share a single ticket package.

Scenario: You and your coworker Jordan must write the quarterly team report together that both your managers will review for upcoming promotion decisions. You must choose between using the company's standard five-section template that has been used for ten years, or creating an original format with custom graphs and storytelling that Jordan designed. Both your names appear as co-authors on the report due next Friday.
Scenario_rev: You and your coworker must write the quarterly team report together that both your managers will review for upcoming promotion decisions. You must choose between using the company's standard five-section template that has been used for ten years, or creating an original format with custom graphs and storytelling that you designed. Both your names appear as co-authors on the report due next Friday.
Conflict Rationale: You want to use the company's standard template that has been the accepted format for years (Conformity), while Jordan wants to create an original format with custom design elements (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both you and Jordan directly benefit from a strong report that impresses your managers for promotion decisions, requiring collaboration as co-authors on one agreed format.

Scenario: You and your neighbor are leading Saturday's neighborhood park cleanup with 15 volunteers coming. The town provided official instructions to separate trash into three labeled bins, but your neighbor wants to use a new four-category sorting system they designed. You must agree on one method by Friday so you can print instruction flyers and teach all volunteers the same system Saturday morning.
Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor are leading Saturday's neighborhood park cleanup with 15 volunteers coming. The town provided official instructions to separate trash into three labeled bins, but you want to use a new four-category sorting system you designed. You must agree on one method by Friday so you can print instruction flyers and teach all volunteers the same system Saturday morning.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the town's official three-bin instructions (Conformity), while your neighbor wants to use their own four-category system they created (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both you and your neighbor share responsibility for leading the cleanup successfully and both benefit from volunteers understanding one consistent system, requiring agreement on one approach.

Scenario: You and your five gaming teammates must agree on a practice schedule for the next six weeks to qualify for the Regional Tournament that requires 90% attendance from all members. Your teammate Jake wants everyone to commit to fixed Thursday 8-10pm sessions, but you prefer keeping your current flexible approach where the group plays whenever people are available. All six members must agree on one schedule and sign the tournament registration by Sunday.
Scenario_rev: You and your five gaming teammates must agree on a practice schedule for the next six weeks to qualify for the Regional Tournament that requires 90% attendance from all members. You want everyone to commit to fixed Thursday 8-10pm sessions, but your teammate prefers keeping your current flexible approach where the group plays whenever people are available. All six members must agree on one schedule and sign the tournament registration by Sunday.
Conflict Rationale: You want to keep the flexible spontaneous schedule where people play when available (Self-direction), while Jake wants everyone to commit to fixed Thursday sessions (Conformity).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because all six teammates directly benefit from qualifying for the tournament and must collaborate by agreeing on one schedule that meets the 90% attendance requirement.

Scenario: You and your teammate must choose and mark the official route for tomorrow's school charity race on the event map for all 50 runners. The school has always used the flat, paved loop that the principal approved and parents expect, but your teammate wants to create a new route through the nature trail with hills. You must agree on one route and give the final map to the principal by 3 PM today.
Scenario_rev: You and your organizer teammate must choose and mark the official route for tomorrow's school charity race on the event map for all 50 runners. The school has always used the flat, paved loop that the principal approved and parents expect, but you want to create a new route through the nature trail with hills. You must agree on one route and give the final map to the principal by 3 PM today.
Conflict Rationale: You value following the school's established, approved route that meets everyone's expectations (Conformity), while your teammate values creating an independent, original path (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal cooperation because both of you are equally responsible for creating the race map, and both benefit equally when runners have a good race experience. If the race succeeds, you both receive credit as the planning team.

Scenario: You and your project partner must create one presentation for the school science fair by Friday, and the teacher will write only one student's name as 'lead researcher' on the certificate based on whose approach is used. Your partner wants to design a creative experiment nobody has tried before, but you want to follow the standard project template the school provides. You must agree on one approach to complete the project together.
Scenario_rev: You and your project partner must create one presentation for the school science fair by Friday, and the teacher will write only one student's name as 'lead researcher' on the certificate based on whose approach is used. You want to design a creative experiment nobody has tried before, but your partner wants to follow the standard project template the school provides. You must agree on one approach to complete the project together.
Conflict Rationale: You value using the school's provided template that follows established guidelines (Conformity), while your partner values designing an original, independent experiment (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you must collaborate to create one shared presentation (cooperation), while also competing for the 'lead researcher' recognition that only one of you can receive (competition). The competition is for individual recognition and credit.

Scenario: You and your partner must organize three lunch-time activities together for the school's new 'buddy program' where you help lonely students make friends, and the principal will name only one of you 'buddy leader' based on whose activity ideas are used most. You want to use the three standard games from the program handbook, but your partner wants to invent completely new activities. You must agree on the three activities and submit one activity plan by Monday.
Scenario_rev: You and your partner must organize three lunch-time activities together for the school's new 'buddy program' where you help lonely students make friends, and the principal will name only one of you 'buddy leader' based on whose activity ideas are used most. You must agree on the three activities and submit one activity plan by Monday.
Conflict Rationale: You value following the standard games provided in the official handbook (Conformity), while your partner values inventing original, independent activities (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you must work together to create one shared activity plan for the buddy program (cooperation), while competing for the 'buddy leader' title that depends on whose ideas are selected (competition). The competition is for individual recognition and leadership status.

Scenario: You and your older sibling must create one chore chart together that your parents will hang on the fridge, and whoever's format is used most gets to pick the family movie this weekend. You want to use the sample chart your parents gave you with exact times and steps for each chore, but your sibling wants to make a flexible schedule where you each choose when to do chores. You must agree on one final chart by dinner tonight.
Scenario_rev: You and your younger sibling must create one chore chart together that your parents will hang on the fridge, and whoever's format is used most gets to pick the family movie this weekend. Conflict Rationale: You value using the sample chart your parents provided with established rules and structure (Conformity), while your sibling values creating an independent, flexible system (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you must collaborate to create one shared chore chart that works for both of you (cooperation), while competing for the movie selection privilege that depends on whose format is chosen (competition). The competition is for decision-making power and personal preference.

Scenario: You and your best friend must choose one after-school club to join together as partners for the school year, and the club advisor will select only one student from each pair as 'team captain' based on whose club choice reasoning is better. Conflict Rationale: You value joining the established student council that follows traditional participation patterns (Conformity), while your friend values starting an original, independent club (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you must agree on one club and join together as required partners (cooperation), while competing for the 'team captain' position that only one of you can receive based on whose choice is selected (competition). Scenario: You and your older brother must decide together how to spend the $50 your grandmother gave both of you for this month, and your parents will give an extra $10 to whoever's idea they choose. Scenario_rev: You and your younger sibling must decide together how to spend the $50 your grandmother gave both of you for this month, and your parents will give an extra $10 to whoever's idea they choose. Scenario: You and your cousin must plan one birthday party together for your grandfather next Saturday, and your family will vote on whose ideas they like better to decide who gives the thank-you speech. Scenario: You and your cousin share a vegetable garden and must design one entry together for the neighborhood garden contest with a $50 prize that you'd split equally. Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you must collaborate to create and submit one shared garden entry (cooperation), while the contest judges will evaluate whose design ideas made the garden more successful, creating implicit competition for creative credit (competition). Scenario: You and your lab partner must choose one method to complete your week-long science experiment, and the teacher will write only one student's name as 'project leader' on the display board based on whose method is used. Scenario: You and the class vice president must write one new classroom rule about phone use that the principal will approve, and the principal will name one of you 'student policy leader' in the school announcement based on whose approach is used. Scenario: You and your swim team co-captain must create one training schedule together for the next month before the big meet, and the coach will choose one co-captain as 'training leader' to present the plan to the whole team based on whose approach is used. Scenario: You and your brother must rearrange your shared bedroom before your friends visit this weekend, and your parents will give extra allowance to whoever's friends say the room looks better because of their ideas. You want to arrange the furniture the same way most kids in your neighborhood have their rooms set up, but your brother wants to create a unique layout with separate private corners. You must agree on one final arrangement and finish it by Saturday morning.
Scenario_rev: You and your brother must rearrange your shared bedroom before your friends visit this weekend, and your parents will give extra allowance to whoever's friends say the room looks better because of their ideas. Conflict Rationale: You value arranging the room according to the established neighborhood pattern that follows common standards (Conformity), while your brother values creating an original, independent layout (Self-direction).
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you must work together to rearrange one shared bedroom that both of you live in (cooperation), while competing for extra allowance based on whose arrangement ideas friends prefer (competition). The competition is for financial reward and social validation.

Scenario: You and a senior librarian must create one summer reading list for 200 children by Friday that will be printed in brochures. You need to decide together whether to include only classic books from the library board's approved list or add graphic novels and experimental formats that aren't on the approved list. Whoever's book category gets checked out more by the end of summer gets a $200 bonus from the library director.
Scenario_rev: You are a senior librarian and must create one summer reading list for 200 children by Friday with your junior colleague that will be printed in brochures. You need to decide together whether to include only classic books from the library board's approved list or add graphic novels and experimental formats that aren't on the approved list. Whoever's book category gets checked out more by the end of summer gets a $200 bonus from the library director.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the library board's approved book list to respect established rules. The senior librarian wants to include unapproved experimental formats to give children more creative choices.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to create one unified reading list that serves all the children. You also compete for the bonus by promoting your preferred book category to get more checkouts.

Scenario: You and your robotics partner must build one robot together for the regional tournament in three weeks. You need to decide whether to follow the team's standard building manual that has won twice before or try your partner's new wheel design that has never been tested. Whoever contributes the main design gets named Lead Builder on your school record, which helps with college applications.
Scenario_rev: You are a robotics partner and must build one robot together with your teammate for the regional tournament in three weeks. Whoever contributes the main design gets named Lead Builder on your school record, which helps with college applications.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the proven building manual that follows team rules and has worked before. Your partner wants to try their own creative new design that breaks from the standard approach.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to build one robot that works well enough to compete in the tournament. You also compete for the Lead Builder title that helps with your future college applications.

Scenario: You and another editor must create one book proposal for your publishing house by Friday for a debut author. You need to decide together whether the author should write a traditional mystery novel following genre rules or an experimental format mixing different styles. Whoever's genre approach gets approved by the publisher will be listed as Lead Editor on the project, which counts toward your promotion review.
Scenario_rev: You are an editor and must create one book proposal with another editor for your publishing house by Friday for a debut author.
