Conflict Rationale: You want the author to follow traditional mystery genre rules that publishers expect. The other editor wants the author to experiment with mixing different creative styles.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to create one strong proposal that the publisher will approve. You also compete for the Lead Editor title that helps with your promotion.

Scenario: You and the assistant principal must teach all 300 students one new fire drill route by Friday before the safety inspector visits. You need to decide together whether to use the fire department's official script with specific instructions or let your partner create a simpler version that's easier for young kids to remember. If the inspector sees that students don't follow the proper procedure, the school fails inspection and the end-of-year field trip for all students gets cancelled.
Scenario_rev: You are the assistant principal and must teach all 300 students one new fire drill route by Friday with your teacher colleague before the safety inspector visits. You need to decide together whether to use the fire department's official script with specific instructions or let you create a simpler version that's easier for young kids to remember. If the inspector sees that students don't follow the proper procedure, the school fails inspection and the end-of-year field trip for all students gets cancelled.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the fire department's exact official script to meet safety rules properly. Your partner wants to simplify the instructions creatively so young students can understand and remember them better.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to teach one fire drill method that all students will learn. Neither of you is going on the field trip, but your decision affects whether 300 other students get to go.

Scenario: You and a senior analyst must create one new data review process by Monday that five other departments need for their monthly reports. You need to decide together whether everyone must follow the same standard checklist and timeline or each analyst can create their own review method. If the other departments don't get clean data on time, they miss client deadlines and the company loses three major contracts worth $500,000.
Scenario_rev: You are a senior analyst and must create one new data review process by Monday with your junior colleague that five other departments need for their monthly reports. You need to decide together whether everyone must follow the same standard checklist and timeline or each analyst can create their own review method. If the other departments don't get clean data on time, they miss client deadlines and the company loses three major contracts worth $500,000.
Conflict Rationale: You want everyone to follow the same standard checklist so the process is consistent and follows company rules. The senior analyst wants each person to design their own flexible review method based on their creative judgment.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to create one data process that works for five departments who depend on getting information. Your decision doesn't directly benefit either of you, but it affects whether other teams can serve their clients successfully.

Scenario: You and your co-parent must create one set of discipline rules by Sunday to present at the parent council meeting as a model for 30 families to use. You need to decide together whether the rules require children to obey parent decisions without question or allow children to negotiate and make their own choices. Your own kids are teenagers who don't need these rules anymore, but younger families are having serious behavior problems and asked for help.
Scenario_rev: You are a co-parent and must create one set of discipline rules by Sunday with your parenting partner to present at the parent council meeting as a model for 30 families to use. You need to decide together whether the rules require children to obey parent decisions without question or allow children to negotiate and make their own choices. Your own kids are teenagers who don't need these rules anymore, but younger families are having serious behavior problems and asked for help.
Conflict Rationale: You want rules that require children to obey and respect parent authority. Your co-parent wants rules that let children think independently and make their own creative choices.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to create one set of rules that will help 30 other families. Creating these rules costs you both time and effort, and your own family won't use them since your children are already grown.

Scenario: You and your school counselor must choose one support plan format by Wednesday that will become the new standard for 50 struggling students next year. You need to decide together whether to use brief weekly check-ins following the school's standard schedule or longer flexible sessions where students can skip one class period when needed. The counselor is retiring next month and won't see how well it works, but she needs your input since you've used both types of support before.
Scenario_rev: You are a school counselor and must choose one support plan format by Wednesday with your colleague that will become the new standard for 50 struggling students next year. You need to decide together whether to use brief weekly check-ins following the school's standard schedule or longer flexible sessions where students can skip one class period when needed. You are retiring next month and won't see how well it works, but you need your colleague's input since they've used both types of support before.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the school's standard schedule with brief weekly check-ins that don't disrupt class rules. The counselor wants to create flexible longer sessions that let students choose when they need help most.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to design one support plan that will help 50 other struggling students next year. The counselor is sacrificing time in her last month to create this plan, and you're giving up your input without any personal benefit since the plan is for future students.

Scenario: You and Alice must organize one study session at her apartment this Saturday to help five classmates prepare for Monday's certification exam that they're currently failing. You need to decide together whether to follow the official study guide chapter-by-chapter in order or let each person focus on whatever topics they personally need most. You and Alice already passed this exam last year, so you're giving up your whole Saturday to help others.
Scenario_rev: You are Alice and must organize one study session at your apartment this Saturday with your classmate to help five other classmates prepare for Monday's certification exam that they're currently failing. You need to decide together whether to follow the official study guide chapter-by-chapter in order or let each person focus on whatever topics they personally need most. You and your classmate already passed this exam last year, so you're giving up your whole Saturday to help others.
Conflict Rationale: You want everyone to follow the official study guide in the proper order so they learn the required material correctly. Alice wants each person to study their weak areas freely based on their individual creative judgment.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to run one study session that helps five failing classmates pass their exam. Neither of you needs this study session since you already passed, so you're both sacrificing your Saturday to help others succeed.

Scenario: You and your partner must create one set of babysitting rules by tonight to share at the neighborhood parents' meeting as a guide for 20 families who share babysitters. You need to decide together whether the rules require strict bedtimes and limited screen time or flexible schedules where kids help decide activities. Your own kids are teenagers who don't need babysitters anymore, but younger families asked you to create guidelines they can all use.
Scenario_rev: You are a parent and must create one set of babysitting rules by tonight with your partner to share at the neighborhood parents' meeting as a guide for 20 families who share babysitters. You need to decide together whether the rules require strict bedtimes and limited screen time or flexible schedules where kids help decide activities. Your own kids are teenagers who don't need babysitters anymore, but younger families asked you to create guidelines they can all use.
Conflict Rationale: You want rules that require children to follow strict bedtimes and limits that babysitters can enforce consistently. Your partner wants flexible guidelines that let children make creative choices about their own activities.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to create one set of rules that will help 20 other families coordinate their babysitting. You're spending your evening creating these guidelines even though your own family won't use them since your children are grown.

Scenario: You and a senior manager must create one complaint resolution procedure by Friday that 40 team members will use when they have workplace problems. You need to decide together whether to require formal written complaints submitted through official channels only or allow informal conversations and flexible problem-solving. Neither of you handles complaints directly—the HR department will use whatever system you design for the whole company.
Scenario_rev: You are a senior manager and must create one complaint resolution procedure by Friday with your junior colleague that 40 team members will use when they have workplace problems. You need to decide together whether to require formal written complaints submitted through official channels only or allow informal conversations and flexible problem-solving. Neither of you handles complaints directly—the HR department will use whatever system you design for the whole company.
Conflict Rationale: You want formal written complaints through official channels so the process follows proper company rules and procedures. The senior manager wants informal flexible conversations so people can solve problems creatively without strict requirements.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to create one procedure that 40 team members will rely on for workplace issues. Neither of you personally benefits from this system since HR handles complaints, but your decision affects how other employees get help.

Scenario: You and your boss must finalize one project proposal by Thursday that will secure $2 million in funding to keep 15 junior employees' positions next year. You need to decide together whether the implementation plan follows the company's standard procedures exactly or includes an experimental approach your boss designed. Neither of you will work on this project—a different team will implement whatever plan you submit to the funding committee.
Scenario_rev: You are the boss and must finalize one project proposal by Thursday with your employee that will secure $2 million in funding to keep 15 junior employees' positions next year. You need to decide together whether the implementation plan follows the company's standard procedures exactly or includes an experimental approach you designed. Neither of you will work on this project—a different team will implement whatever plan you submit to the funding committee.
Conflict Rationale: You want the proposal to follow the company's standard procedures exactly so it meets official requirements and expectations. Your boss wants to include their experimental creative approach that breaks from the usual methods.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to create one proposal that will secure funding for 15 other employees' jobs. Neither of you will work on the project itself, so you're spending time and effort purely to help others keep their positions.

Scenario: You and the hospital's compliance officer must submit one recruitment plan by Monday for a clinical trial involving 200 heart patients. You need to decide together whether to follow the standard hospital template with fixed interview scripts and forms or use the officer's flexible method that adapts questions based on each patient's individual responses. Other research teams will use whatever plan you create as the new hospital-wide standard for all future trials.
Scenario_rev: You are the hospital's compliance officer and must submit one recruitment plan by Monday with your colleague for a clinical trial involving 200 heart patients. You need to decide together whether to follow the standard hospital template with fixed interview scripts and forms or use your flexible method that adapts questions based on each patient's individual responses. Other research teams will use whatever plan you create as the new hospital-wide standard for all future trials.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the standard hospital template with fixed scripts so the process follows established medical research rules consistently. The compliance officer wants flexible adaptive methods so researchers can respond creatively to each patient's unique situation.
Cooperation Rationale: You must work together to create one recruitment plan that will become the standard for all hospital research teams. Your decision requires extra time and careful thought because it will affect how other researchers work, not just your own trial.

Scenario: You and another volunteer are creating a 4-week body positivity workshop for the youth center, and must decide whether to include a session about tattoos and piercings as self-expression. The center's director needs both of you to agree on one complete workshop plan by Friday, or the program gets canceled. If you include the tattoo session, you will have to personally defend this choice to concerned parents at next week's community meeting.
Scenario_rev: You and another volunteer are creating a 4-week body positivity workshop for the youth center, and must decide whether to include a session about tattoos and piercings as self-expression. The center's director needs both of you to agree on one complete workshop plan by Friday, or the program gets canceled. If you include the tattoo session, the other volunteer will have to personally defend this choice to concerned parents at next week's community meeting.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the community's traditional values and avoid controversial topics, while the other volunteer wants freedom to explore creative self-expression topics. One of you must compromise your core value to reach agreement.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you both work together to create a workshop that benefits youth in the community, but including the controversial session creates a personal cost (defending the decision publicly) for you. The sacrifice goes beyond simply suppressing a value—it requires facing direct criticism.

Scenario: You and your classmate are planning the school's culture day assembly, and must choose one dress code for all 50 student performers: matching formal outfits that follow school uniform rules, or diverse cultural clothing from each student's heritage. The principal needs both of you to submit one agreed dress code by Monday morning, or she'll cancel the assembly. If you choose cultural clothing, you must help students whose outfits violate school policy fill out exception forms and personally deliver them to the strict vice principal.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-planner are planning the school's culture day assembly, and must choose one dress code for all 50 student performers: matching formal outfits that follow school uniform rules, or diverse cultural clothing from each student's heritage. The principal needs both of you to submit one agreed dress code by Monday morning, or she'll cancel the assembly. If you choose cultural clothing, your co-planner must help students whose outfits violate school policy fill out exception forms and personally deliver them to the strict vice principal.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow school uniform rules and established dress code policies, while your classmate wants students to freely express their cultural identities through clothing. One of you must give up your value to create the assembly.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you both work to create a celebration that benefits the entire school community, but choosing cultural clothing creates real personal cost (extra paperwork, confronting the vice principal) for you. The burden falls unevenly on the agent favoring conformity.

Scenario: You and a coworker volunteer to help a small animal shelter create a new volunteer scheduling system for free, choosing between your company's proven 10-step template or an experimental flexible approach your coworker designed. Your boss needs both of you to agree on one system by Thursday to present to the shelter director. If you choose the experimental system and it fails, you must use your own weekend time for the next month to fix it and retrain all 15 shelter volunteers.
Scenario_rev: You and a coworker volunteer to help a small animal shelter create a new volunteer scheduling system for free, choosing between a proven 10-step template your coworker's company uses or an experimental flexible approach you designed. Your coworker's boss needs both of you to agree on one system by Thursday to present to the shelter director. If you choose the experimental system and it fails, you must use your own weekend time for the next month to fix it and retrain all 15 shelter volunteers.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the company's tested procedures and established workflow standards, while your coworker wants creative freedom to try a new innovative approach. One of you must compromise your value to help the shelter.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you both volunteer your work skills to help a nonprofit that serves the community, but choosing the experimental approach creates concrete personal cost (weekend time commitment) for you if problems arise. The sacrifice is real and measurable.

Scenario: You and another parent volunteer are running free homework help at the community center today, and must pick one tutoring method: the center director's official 5-step checklist, or a flexible approach the other volunteer created. You both must agree on one method before 20 students arrive at 3pm. If you use the flexible method and students don't improve, you must personally call each parent to explain why you didn't follow the director's instructions.
Scenario_rev: You and another parent volunteer are running free homework help at the community center today, and must pick one tutoring method: the center director's official 5-step checklist, or a flexible approach you created. You both must agree on one method before 20 students arrive at 3pm. If you use the flexible method and students don't improve, the other parent volunteer must personally call each parent to explain why you didn't follow the director's instructions.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the official tutoring procedure provided by the center director, while the other volunteer wants freedom to use creative teaching methods. One of you must give up your value to start the session.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you both volunteer your time to help students in the community for free, but choosing the flexible method creates concrete personal cost (making difficult phone calls to parents) for you if results are poor. The potential burden is clear and specific.

Scenario: You're a teacher working with the student council president to create new classroom behavior rules for the whole school, choosing between the district's standard discipline code or a student-designed system based on peer discussion. The principal needs both of you to submit one unified rule system by Friday for the official handbook. If you agree to the student system, you must attend three evening community meetings to personally explain and defend this choice to parents who prefer traditional discipline.
Scenario_rev: You're the student council president working with a teacher to create new classroom behavior rules for the whole school, choosing between the district's standard discipline code or a student-designed system based on peer discussion that you created. The principal needs both of you to submit one unified rule system by Friday for the official handbook. If the teacher agrees to the student system, they must attend three evening community meetings to personally explain and defend this choice to parents who prefer traditional discipline.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the established district discipline code and traditional authority structures, while the student wants creative freedom to design rules through student voice. One of you must compromise your value to create the new system.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you both work to improve the school environment for all 500 students, but choosing the student-designed system creates real personal cost (attending evening meetings, facing parent criticism) for you. The time commitment and social pressure represent concrete sacrifice.

Scenario: You and the motel's operations supervisor must write new guest rules together because the owner put both of you in charge. You want to let each guest family decide for themselves whether to bring pets into the pool area, while the supervisor wants to keep the blanket 'pets welcome everywhere' policy that the motel has followed for 15 years. You must both sign one policy document by Friday or the motel cannot open for summer season.
Scenario_rev: You are the motel's operations supervisor and must write new guest rules together with your policy coordinator because the owner put both of you in charge. You must both sign one policy document by Friday or the motel cannot open for summer season.
Conflict Rationale: You value letting individuals make their own choices about pet access, while the supervisor values maintaining the established practice that has defined the motel's identity for years.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both of you must agree and sign the same policy document to meet the owner's requirement, and both benefit equally when the motel opens successfully for the season.

Scenario: You and your brother must decide together how to display your deceased mother's wedding dress in the family home because you co-own the house. You want to create your own new memorial style by framing just a piece of the dress with modern photos, while your brother wants to display the complete dress exactly as your mother stored it in tissue paper for 40 years. You must finish the memorial display before 20 family members visit in two weeks.
Scenario_rev: You and your sibling must decide together how to display your deceased mother's wedding dress in the family home because you co-own the house. You must finish the memorial display before 20 family members visit in two weeks.
Conflict Rationale: You value creating your own unique way to remember your mother through a personal artistic choice, while your brother values preserving the exact traditional method your mother used to store her most precious possession.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because you both own the house and must agree on one memorial display together, and both of you benefit equally from having a completed memorial that honors your mother when family visits.

Scenario: You and Emma, a veteran family counselor, must create one parenting workshop together for the community center because the director will only fund a co-taught program. Scenario_rev: You are Emma, a veteran family counselor, and must create one parenting workshop together with your co-teacher for the community center because the director will only fund a co-taught program. Scenario: You and John, the church elder, must plan a weekly discussion group together because the church board requires both a tech volunteer and an elder to co-lead any new program. Scenario_rev: You are John, the church elder, and must plan a weekly discussion group together with the tech volunteer because the church board requires both a tech volunteer and an elder to co-lead any new program. Scenario: You and your grandparent must choose your high school together because your grandparent is your legal guardian and controls the education fund your parents left. Scenario_rev: You are the grandparent and legal guardian who must choose a high school together with your grandchild because you control the education fund their parents left. You must both sign the enrollment papers by Friday or your grandchild will have no high school placement for next year.
Conflict Rationale: You value choosing your own educational path that matches your interests and learning style, while your grandparent values continuing the family's long-standing tradition of attending the same respected institution.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because you both must agree and sign the same enrollment papers together due to legal guardianship and fund control, and both benefit when you successfully enroll in a high school.

Scenario: You are 17 years old and received a full scholarship to an art school in another country, but you must convince your father to agree because he must co-sign your visa application as your legal guardian. Scenario: You and Sarah, a veteran math teacher, must create one algebra course together because the principal assigned you to co-teach the only advanced section. Scenario: You are the restaurant manager and must decide the menu with your head chef because the owner requires both of you to approve all menu changes together. You want to create a rotating menu where you can invent new dishes each month based on your own ideas, while your chef wants to keep the same ten signature dishes that the restaurant has served for 15 years. Scenario_rev: You are the head chef and must decide the menu with your restaurant manager because the owner requires both of you to approve all menu changes together. Conflict Rationale: You value having the freedom to independently create new dishes and change direction based on your own culinary inspiration, while the chef values preserving the traditional recipes that built the restaurant's loyal customer base and reputation.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because the owner requires both of you to jointly approve the menu concept, and both of you benefit equally when the restaurant successfully serves customers during the profitable summer season.

Scenario: You and your grandparent are organizing the town's annual talent show together, and the mayor said both of you must agree on the program. Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because the mayor requires both of you to jointly agree on the program, and both of you benefit equally when the show successfully happens and raises money for the community center.

Scenario: You and the music department head must decide together which violin teaching method to use because the school budget only allows one program. Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because the limited budget requires both of you to agree on one single violin program, and both of you benefit equally when the program successfully runs and serves students.

Scenario: You are a professional caregiver who must create a daily schedule with Mr. Chen's daughter because she pays for the care and must approve all routine changes. Scenario: You and your spouse John must decide together how to announce your pregnancy to both families at the holiday dinner you are hosting next week. Scenario: You and a classically-trained artist must design a 20-foot mural for the new community center by Friday, and the arts council will award one of you a $5,000 solo gallery show based on whose style is more visible in the final mural. You want to use bold abstract shapes with unusual color combinations, while the other artist wants realistic paintings of local historical landmarks like the town's other murals. The center opens in two weeks and needs the mural completed.
Scenario_rev: You are a classically-trained artist who must design a 20-foot mural for the new community center by Friday with your abstract-focused peer, and the arts council will award one of you a $5,000 solo gallery show based on whose style is more visible in the final mural. You want realistic paintings of local historical landmarks like the town's other murals, while the other artist wants to use bold abstract shapes with unusual color combinations. The center opens in two weeks and needs the mural completed.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creative independence and experimenting with new artistic expressions, while Tradition values preserving established artistic styles and honoring the town's historical aesthetic standards.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate to create one unified mural (shared goal), while competing for the solo gallery show award based on whose artistic vision dominates the final design (competition for recognition and financial reward).

Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate to create one reception timeline that satisfies the couple and vendors (shared goal), while competing for the promotion based on which planning philosophy receives more positive guest feedback (competition for career advancement).

Scenario: You and your project partner must design the format for tomorrow's quarterly team feedback session for 15 employees, and your manager will choose one of you to facilitate all future monthly sessions based on how smoothly this one runs. You want an open discussion where team members share thoughts freely without a fixed agenda, while your partner wants to use the structured feedback form the company has used for five years. The installation team needs the final plan Monday morning.
Scenario_rev: You are a junior curator who must design the exhibition layout for a newly acquired Renaissance painting by Monday with a senior curator, and the museum director will assign one of you as lead curator for the next major acquisition based on how many visitors this exhibition attracts in its first month. You want a traditional gallery space with the painting as the central focus and only small text labels, while the senior curator wants to create an immersive room with video displays and interactive touchscreens explaining the painting's context. The installation team needs the final plan Monday morning.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating innovative museum experiences and exploring new ways to engage audiences, while Tradition values preserving classical art presentation methods and letting artworks speak for themselves without modern distractions.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must work together to create one exhibition that properly showcases the valuable painting (shared goal), while competing for the lead curator position based on which approach draws more visitors (competition for career advancement and professional authority).

Scenario: You and your sibling must create the gift exchange rules for your family's 20-person holiday gathering, and your parents said whichever system gets more participation this year will be used for all future holidays. You want everyone to make personalized handmade gifts that show creativity, while your sibling wants to continue the traditional system where each person buys a $30 store-bought gift for their assigned family member. You must email the final rules to the family by Sunday so everyone has four weeks to prepare.
Scenario_rev: You and your sibling must create the gift exchange rules for your family's 20-person holiday gathering, and your parents said whichever system gets more participation this year will be used for all future holidays. You want to continue the traditional system where each person buys a $30 store-bought gift for their assigned family member, while your sibling wants everyone to make personalized handmade gifts that show creativity. You must email the final rules to the family by Sunday so everyone has four weeks to prepare.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values personal creative expression and making unique individualized gifts, while Tradition values maintaining the established family customs and predictable gift exchange system used for many years.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must jointly create one gift exchange system that the whole family will follow (shared goal), while competing to establish whose approach becomes the permanent family tradition based on participation levels (competition for long-term influence over family traditions).

Scenario: You and your coworker Sarah must plan the office December celebration for 50 employees by Wednesday, and your manager will choose one of you to lead the new company culture committee based on how many people attend this event. You want to create a winter creativity festival with art stations and activities from different cultures, while Sarah wants a traditional Christmas party with a decorated tree and Secret Santa gift exchange. You must submit one event plan with budget and activities to your manager by Wednesday to book the venue.
Scenario_rev: You are Sarah, and you must plan the office December celebration for 50 employees by Wednesday with your coworker, and your manager will choose one of you to lead the new company culture committee based on how many people attend this event. You must submit one event plan with budget and activities to your manager by Wednesday to book the venue.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values exploring diverse cultural expressions and giving people creative freedom, while Tradition values celebrating established holiday customs and maintaining familiar seasonal ceremonies.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate to plan one successful event that brings the office together (shared goal), while competing for the culture committee leadership position based on attendance numbers (competition for ongoing organizational influence and responsibility).

Scenario: You and a senior music teacher must create one semester curriculum for the school's music program by Friday, and the principal will give next year's $10,000 expanded budget to whichever teaching approach brings in more student enrollments this semester. You want to include modern songwriting and student-created compositions, while the senior teacher wants to focus only on classical pieces and traditional music theory. You must submit one unified curriculum plan by Friday to meet the district deadline.
Scenario_rev: You are a senior music teacher who must create one semester curriculum for the school's music program by Friday with a newer colleague, and the principal will give next year's $10,000 expanded budget to whichever teaching approach brings in more student enrollments this semester. You want to focus only on classical pieces and traditional music theory, while your colleague wants to include modern songwriting and student-created compositions. You must submit one unified curriculum plan by Friday to meet the district deadline.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values encouraging students to create original music and explore their own musical ideas, while Tradition values teaching established classical repertoire and conventional music theory foundations.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must work together to create one approved curriculum that meets district requirements (shared goal), while competing for the expanded budget based on which teaching approach attracts more student enrollment (competition for resources and programmatic influence).

Scenario: You and your spouse must plan your 10th anniversary party that will also serve as this year's family reunion for 30 relatives, and your extended family said they'll be more likely to attend future events in whichever style they enjoy most this time. You want a casual backyard gathering where guests arrive anytime and choose activities freely, while your spouse wants a formal sit-down dinner with traditional toasts and a scheduled program. You must decide on one party format and send invitations by next week so relatives can book travel.
Scenario_rev: You and your spouse must plan your 10th anniversary party that will also serve as this year's family reunion for 30 relatives, and your extended family said they'll be more likely to attend future events in whichever style they enjoy most this time. You want a formal sit-down dinner with traditional toasts and a scheduled program, while your spouse wants a casual backyard gathering where guests arrive anytime and choose activities freely. You must decide on one party format and send invitations by next week so relatives can book travel.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating relaxed environments where people have freedom to socialize as they choose, while Tradition values following formal celebration customs and structured ceremonial events.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must jointly plan one celebration that works as both anniversary and reunion (shared goal), while the family's stated preference creates competition between whose party style becomes the template for future gatherings (competition for influence over family event traditions).

Scenario: You and your older cousin must design a youth program for your church to address declining teen attendance, and the church board will name one of you as the paid youth coordinator based on whose curriculum ideas are more prominent in the final proposal you submit Friday. You want open discussion sessions where teens choose topics and lead conversations themselves, while your cousin wants structured Bible study lessons following the traditional Sunday school format the church has used for 20 years. The board needs the proposal by Friday to start the program next month.
Scenario_rev: You are the older cousin who must design a youth program for your church with your younger cousin to address declining teen attendance, and the church board will name one of you as the paid youth coordinator based on whose curriculum ideas are more prominent in the final proposal you submit Friday. You want structured Bible study lessons following the traditional Sunday school format the church has used for 20 years, while your younger cousin wants open discussion sessions where teens choose topics and lead conversations themselves. Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate to create one youth program proposal that the board approves (shared goal), while competing for the paid coordinator position based on which educational philosophy dominates the program design (competition for employment and programmatic authority).

Scenario: You and Sam, a respected street artist, must create a joint mural for a city wall that will be unveiled at next month's community festival attended by 2,000 residents. You must decide together whether to use your experimental abstract style that expresses individual creative vision, or follow the neighborhood's traditional imagery of historical landmarks that elderly residents and the local cultural preservation society expect. The city will only approve and fund one unified design that both of you sign off on.
Scenario_rev: You are Sam, a respected street artist, and you must create a joint mural with your collaborator for a city wall that will be unveiled at next month's community festival attended by 2,000 residents. You must decide together whether to use your collaborator's experimental abstract style that expresses individual creative vision, or follow the neighborhood's traditional imagery of historical landmarks that elderly residents and the local cultural preservation society expect. The city will only approve and fund one unified design that both of you sign off on.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values independent creative expression and innovative artistic choices, while Tradition values maintaining established visual customs and respecting community heritage. One artist must suppress their core artistic values for the collaborative work to proceed.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because both artists sacrifice personal artistic vision to serve the community's needs and create something for public benefit rather than personal gain. The mural serves the greater good of beautifying a shared public space, requiring artists to compromise their individual preferences.

Scenario: You and Elder Musa, the village farm committee head, must decide today which crops to plant in the communal 10-acre field that feeds 200 village families. You want to try a new four-crop plan with a drought-resistant grain that scientists recommend, while Elder Musa wants to use the traditional three-crop rotation (maize, beans, millet) that has worked for five generations. Both of you must agree and sign the planting order together, or the planting season will be delayed.
Scenario_rev: You are Elder Musa, the village farm committee head, and you must decide today with your fellow committee member which crops to plant in the communal 10-acre field that feeds 200 village families. Your fellow committee member wants to try a new four-crop plan with a drought-resistant grain that scientists recommend, while you want to use the traditional three-crop rotation (maize, beans, millet) that has worked for five generations. Both of you must agree and sign the planting order together, or the planting season will be delayed.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values trying new scientific methods and making independent choices based on current information, while Tradition values preserving proven agricultural practices and respecting ancestral wisdom. The decision requires one person to abandon their core approach to food security.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because both you and Elder Musa are making decisions that primarily serve the village families' food needs rather than your own benefit. You both must put the community's welfare above your personal preferences about farming methods.

Scenario: You and your cousin Maya are planning a surprise 30th birthday party for your close friend Sarah, and you must book the venue today before prices increase tomorrow. You want a casual outdoor picnic where guests choose their own activities and seating, while Maya wants the formal banquet hall with assigned seats and a structured program that your family always uses for milestone birthdays. Sarah's elderly grandparents can only attend if you choose Maya's accessible indoor venue, but Sarah has told you privately she dislikes formal events.
Scenario_rev: You are Maya, and you and your cousin are planning a surprise 30th birthday party for your close friend Sarah, and you must book the venue today before prices increase tomorrow. Your cousin wants a casual outdoor picnic where guests choose their own activities and seating, while you want the formal banquet hall with assigned seats and a structured program that your family always uses for milestone birthdays. Sarah's elderly grandparents can only attend if you choose your accessible indoor venue, but Sarah has told your cousin privately she dislikes formal events.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values personal choice and flexible celebration formats that let individuals express themselves freely, while Tradition values established family customs and formal celebration structures. One organizer must give up their vision of the ideal party format.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because both organizers are sacrificing time and effort to create a celebration for their friend's benefit, not their own. The decision requires prioritizing either the elderly grandparents' needs (accessibility) or the birthday person's stated preferences (casual setting), creating a cost to someone's wishes.

Scenario: You and Dr. Martinez are organizing a three-day research retreat for 15 graduate students, and you must finalize the schedule today for printing. You want to let students skip afternoon sessions to work alone on their projects, while Dr. Martinez wants all students to attend every group session, workshop, and meal together like academic retreats have always done. The university will only fund the retreat if both organizers approve the same schedule, and students are counting on this retreat to finish their projects before the semester deadline.
Scenario_rev: You are Dr. Martinez, and you and your co-organizer are organizing a three-day research retreat for 15 graduate students, and you must finalize the schedule today for printing. Your co-organizer wants to let students skip afternoon sessions to work alone on their projects, while you want all students to attend every group session, workshop, and meal together like academic retreats have always done. The university will only fund the retreat if both organizers approve the same schedule, and students are counting on this retreat to finish their projects before the semester deadline.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values independent work time and letting students make their own choices about how to learn best, while Tradition values structured group learning and mandatory participation that has defined academic retreats. One organizer must compromise their educational philosophy.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because both organizers are volunteering their time and effort to benefit the graduate students' research progress, not for personal gain. The decision requires balancing student needs over the organizers' own preferred teaching approaches.

Scenario: You and Dr. Lisa Chen, a licensed therapist, are creating a free six-week support group for people recovering from breakups at the community center. You must submit one program plan to the director by Friday, choosing between your approach using digital mood journals and creative role-play activities, or Dr. Chen's traditional talk-therapy circle format used in clinical practice. Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values innovative therapeutic methods and creative self-expression through new tools, while Tradition values established clinical practices and proven talk-therapy structures. One co-designer must abandon their therapeutic approach to help the participants.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because both you and Dr. Chen are volunteering your time and expertise to provide free mental health support to community members in need. You are both sacrificing your time and potentially your professional reputation if the chosen method does not work well for participants.

Scenario: You and your spouse Alex are starting a scholarship foundation to help local students pay for college, and you must submit the legal name by next week. You want to name it the Smith-Johnson Foundation using both your last names to show equal partnership, while Alex's family who is donating $50,000 to start the foundation wants it named just the Johnson Foundation to honor their family's history of helping the community. Scenario_rev: You are Alex, and you and your spouse are starting a scholarship foundation to help local students pay for college, and you must submit the legal name by next week. Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values maintaining your independent identity and equal recognition in shared projects, while Tradition values preserving family naming customs and honoring ancestral legacies. One spouse must give up either personal identity representation or family expectations.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you and Alex are creating a foundation to give money away to students in need, not to benefit yourselves. The naming decision requires one of you to accept personal disappointment to serve the greater mission of providing scholarships.

Scenario: You and Dr. Patricia Williams, the senior museum curator, must write the main text for a Renaissance art exhibition catalog that 5,000 people will read. You want to use new ideas that challenge old explanations about women's role in Renaissance art, while Dr. Williams wants to use the traditional explanations that match the museum's previous books and what major donors expect. Scenario: You and your spouse Jordan are starting a consulting business that will give free help to small community groups, and you must submit the legal business name by Friday. Scenario: You and Elder Rodriguez, the neighborhood council leader, must finalize the rules for a new volunteer neighborhood watch program and submit them to the city safety office by Monday. Scenario: You and your cousin Maria are planning your extended family's annual memorial day honoring deceased relatives, and you must finalize one program to give to 40 family members by this weekend. Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values personal creative expression and letting individuals choose their own ways to honor loved ones, while Tradition values maintaining established family rituals and consistent memorial practices. One organizer must give up their vision of proper remembrance.

Scenario: You and your parent must jointly choose which college you will attend by the loan application deadline next week, because your parent will only co-sign the student loan for a school they approve. You want to attend a progressive university 500 miles away that encourages independent research projects, while your parent wants you to attend the local traditional college where five family generations have studied and which has a structured classical curriculum. Without your parent's signature on the loan, you cannot afford to attend any college this year.

Without your signature on the loan, your child cannot afford to attend any college this year.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values choosing your own educational path and exploring independent learning opportunities, while Tradition values following established family educational customs and attending the family institution. One person must give up their vision of the right education.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because your parent is taking on financial risk by co-signing a loan to help you attend college, potentially sacrificing their own financial security and retirement savings. The decision requires coordinating your educational goals with your parent's willingness to assume debt and financial responsibility for your benefit.

Scenario: You and your younger sibling must create a 12-month budget plan together to pay for your grandmother's $800 monthly medical care costs, and you must present one agreed plan to your extended family meeting this Sunday. You want to use a flexible budget that includes money from side jobs like online tutoring and selling crafts, while your sibling wants to use only the traditional conservative method of fixed savings from regular jobs that your parents and grandparents have always trusted. Scenario_rev: You are the younger sibling, and you and your older sibling must create a 12-month budget plan together to pay for your grandmother's $800 monthly medical care costs, and you must present one agreed plan to your extended family meeting this Sunday. Your older sibling wants to use a flexible budget that includes money from side jobs like online tutoring and selling crafts, while you want to use only the traditional conservative method of fixed savings from regular jobs that your parents and grandparents have always trusted. Your extended family will only contribute additional support money if they see that both of you agree on a realistic plan.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values flexible income sources and creative financial solutions that adapt to opportunities, while Tradition values conservative financial planning and proven money management methods used by previous generations. One sibling must compromise their approach to financial responsibility.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because both you and your sibling are sacrificing your own income and financial freedom to pay for your grandmother's medical care instead of using that money for yourselves. Both are taking on financial burden and responsibility to help a family member in need.

Scenario: You and your partner are presenting a neighborhood safety proposal to the town council in two days, and you must agree on one opening slide before printing materials tomorrow. You want to start with a story about a local family helped by neighbors during a break-in to show how community support keeps people safe, while your partner wants to start with crime statistics so council members can draw their own conclusions about what the neighborhood needs.
Scenario_rev: You and your data-focused partner are presenting a neighborhood safety proposal to the town council in two days, and you must agree on one opening slide before printing materials tomorrow. You want to start with crime statistics so council members can draw their own conclusions about what the neighborhood needs, while your partner wants to start with a story about a local family helped by neighbors during a break-in to show how community support keeps people safe.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates using an emotional story to help council members care about families needing protection, while Self-direction motivates presenting neutral facts so council members can think independently without emotional influence.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both presenters must agree on one unified opening approach for their shared presentation, and both benefit equally if the council approves their proposal.

Scenario: You and your co-teacher are creating a conflict resolution workshop for parent volunteers at school, and you must choose one main activity before submitting your proposal to the principal tomorrow morning. You want to use partner role-play exercises where parents practice conversations together and support each other through uncomfortable moments, while your co-teacher wants to provide individual reflection journals where each parent works through example conflicts alone at their own pace.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-teacher are creating a conflict resolution workshop for parent volunteers at school, and you must choose one main activity before submitting your proposal to the principal tomorrow morning. You want to provide individual reflection journals where each parent works through example conflicts alone at their own pace, while your co-teacher wants to use partner role-play exercises where parents practice conversations together and support each other through uncomfortable moments.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates creating partner exercises so participants can help and support each other through difficult practice conversations, while Self-direction motivates independent reflection where each person explores conflict resolution in their own way without group constraints.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both teachers must agree on one activity format for their joint workshop proposal, and both benefit equally if the principal approves their workshop and they can teach it together.

Scenario: You and another parent lead a walking group for six neighborhood kids ages 7-10 who walk home after art class on winter Wednesdays when it gets dark at 4pm, and you must choose one route to use starting this week. You want to walk along Main Street where shop owners know the kids and can help if someone gets hurt or lost, while the other parent wants to take the park shortcut where kids can walk at their own speed and explore without adults watching them constantly.
Scenario_rev: You and another parent lead a walking group for six neighborhood kids ages 7-10 who walk home after art class on winter Wednesdays when it gets dark at 4pm, and you must choose one route to use starting this week. You want to take the park shortcut where kids can walk at their own speed and explore without adults watching them constantly, while the other parent wants to walk along Main Street where shop owners know the kids and can help if someone gets hurt or lost.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates choosing the Main Street route where shopkeepers can watch over and help the children if problems arise, while Self-direction motivates the park route where kids can move independently and explore freely without constant adult oversight.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both parent leaders must agree on one shared route for the walking group to use safely, and both benefit equally when all children arrive home safely each week.

Scenario: You and your teammate are building a helper robot for the school science fair next Friday, and you must program it with one main function before testing it on Thursday. You want to program it to remind elderly neighbors to take their medicine at the right times so it can help take care of them, while your teammate wants to program it to learn and respond to each user's custom voice commands so people can teach it whatever they need.
Scenario_rev: You and your teammate are building a helper robot for the school science fair next Friday, and you must program it with one main function before testing it on Thursday. You want to program it to learn and respond to each user's custom voice commands so people can teach it whatever they need, while your teammate wants to program it to remind elderly neighbors to take their medicine at the right times so it can help take care of them.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates programming the robot to provide caring reminders that help elderly people stay healthy, while Self-direction motivates creating a customizable system where each user can independently decide how to use the robot without predetermined care functions.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both teammates must agree on one main program for their shared robot project, and both receive the same grade and recognition if the robot performs well at the science fair.

Scenario: You and your neighbor are organizing a 10-mile charity bike ride this Saturday to raise money for playground equipment, and you must choose one route for all 25 participants to follow. You want to use the flat paved rail trail that keeps everyone riding together so experienced cyclists can help families with young children who might struggle, while your neighbor wants to use the hilly scenic river road where each rider can go at their own pace and stop for photos wherever they want.
Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor are organizing a 10-mile charity bike ride this Saturday to raise money for playground equipment, and you must choose one route for all 25 participants to follow. You want to use the hilly scenic river road where each rider can go at their own pace and stop for photos wherever they want, while your neighbor wants to use the flat paved rail trail that keeps everyone riding together so experienced cyclists can help families with young children who might struggle.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates choosing the rail trail where stronger riders stay close to help families and children who need support, while Self-direction motivates the river road where each participant can ride independently and explore at their own chosen pace.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both organizers must agree on one official route for the charity ride, and both benefit equally when the event succeeds and raises money for the playground equipment.

Scenario: You and your hiking partner are starting a day hike in the mountains this morning, and you must choose one trail route before leaving the parking lot where you have cell service. You want your partner to take the marked blue trail where ranger stations are located every two miles in case either of you gets injured or lost, while your partner wants to take the unmarked ridge trail where you both can explore freely and discover new paths without following set routes.
Scenario_rev: You and your hiking partner are starting a day hike in the mountains this morning, and you must choose one trail route before leaving the parking lot where you have cell service. You want to take the unmarked ridge trail where you both can explore freely and discover new paths without following set routes, while your partner wants you to take the marked blue trail where ranger stations are located every two miles in case either of you gets injured or lost.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates choosing the marked trail where rangers can help and protect both hikers if something goes wrong, while Self-direction motivates the unmarked trail where both hikers can explore independently and make spontaneous route choices.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both hiking partners must agree on one shared trail to hike together safely, and both benefit equally from completing an enjoyable and safe hike.

Scenario: You and your co-leader run a coding club for eight middle school students that meets tomorrow after school, and you must agree on one lesson plan tonight. You want to teach one structured project where you guide everyone through building the same game together step-by-step so all students succeed and help each other, while your co-leader wants to offer five different coding challenges and let each student pick which project they want to explore on their own.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-leader run a coding club for eight middle school students that meets tomorrow after school, and you must agree on one lesson plan tonight. You want to offer five different coding challenges and let each student pick which project they want to explore on their own, while your co-leader wants to teach one structured project where they guide everyone through building the same game together step-by-step so all students succeed and help each other.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates the guided group project where you help all students learn successfully together and support each other, while Self-direction motivates offering choices where each student can independently select and explore the coding challenge that interests them personally.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both club leaders must agree on one lesson plan to teach together tomorrow, and both benefit equally when students learn successfully and return to the club next week.

Scenario: You and another assistant coach must create a 15-minute warm-up routine for Saturday's championship soccer game, and you need to teach it to the team at Friday's practice. You want to do partner stretches where teammates help each other warm up properly and prevent injuries by supporting each other's legs and backs, while the other coach wants to give each player a personal warm-up checklist so they can prepare their own bodies in the way that feels right for them.
Scenario_rev: You and another assistant coach must create a 15-minute warm-up routine for Saturday's championship soccer game, and you need to teach it to the team at Friday's practice. You want to give each player a personal warm-up checklist so they can prepare their own bodies in the way that feels right for them, while the other coach wants to do partner stretches where teammates help each other warm up properly and prevent injuries by supporting each other's legs and backs.

The center director needs your answer in ten minutes to print materials.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes directly helping specific struggling students who urgently need support to pass their tests. Self-direction values giving all students the freedom to independently choose what and how they learn without prescribed guidance.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both tutors are volunteering their final available time (opportunity cost) to benefit students rather than themselves, with no direct benefit to either tutor. The collaboration requires both tutors to agree on one approach, potentially requiring one to sacrifice their preferred way of helping students.

Scenario: You and your friend are creating a comedy video for your YouTube channel in Central Park today, and you want to film spontaneous pranks on random people to make the funniest content, while your friend wants to only film people who give permission first because surprising strangers might upset or embarrass them. You must agree on one filming approach in the next hour to finish recording before the park closes at sunset.
Scenario_rev: You and your content creator friend are creating a comedy video for your YouTube channel in Central Park today, and you want to only film people who give permission first because surprising strangers might upset or embarrass them, while your friend wants to film spontaneous pranks on random people to make the funniest content. You must agree on one filming approach in the next hour to finish recording before the park closes at sunset.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives wanting creative freedom to film spontaneously without restrictions, while Universalism prioritizes protecting strangers' dignity and feelings from potential embarrassment or distress.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must collaborate to create one video together and both benefit equally from a successful, entertaining YouTube video. Neither can make the video alone, and success requires agreement on a single filming method.

Scenario: You and your classmate must build one carnival game booth for the school fair in two weeks, and you want to create a complex puzzle box with secret compartments that shows off your creative design skills, while your classmate wants to make a simple bean-bag toss that students in wheelchairs and younger kids can easily play. You must agree on one game design today to order supplies with your shared $30 budget.
Scenario_rev: You and your classmate must build one carnival game booth for the school fair in two weeks, and you want to make a simple bean-bag toss that students in wheelchairs and younger kids can easily play, while your classmate wants to create a complex puzzle box with secret compartments that shows off their creative design skills. You must agree on one game design today to order supplies with your shared $30 budget.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values expressing personal creativity and independence through a unique, complex design, while Universalism prioritizes fairness and ensuring all students, regardless of ability or age, can participate equally.
Cooperation Rationale: Both students must work together to build one booth using shared materials and budget, and both receive the same grade. Success requires agreeing on a single design that they can complete together before the fair deadline.

Scenario: You and your business partner are starting an online sticker shop and have $100 to buy your first supplies today, and you want to order shiny holographic vinyl and glitter materials that make eye-catching, unique stickers, while your partner wants to buy only recycled paper and soy-based inks that don't create pollution or harm animals. You must agree on one supplier order right now to receive materials before your launch date in three weeks.
Scenario_rev: You and your business partner are starting an online sticker shop and have $100 to buy your first supplies today, and you want to buy only recycled paper and soy-based inks that don't create pollution or harm animals, while your partner wants to order shiny holographic vinyl and glitter materials that make eye-catching, unique stickers. You must agree on one supplier order right now to receive materials before your launch date in three weeks.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives wanting to make independent creative choices using special materials that express your unique vision, while Universalism prioritizes protecting the environment and animal welfare for the greater good of the planet.
Cooperation Rationale: Both partners must pool their money and agree on one order to launch their shared business, and both benefit equally from sales. Neither has enough money to order supplies alone, requiring collaboration on a single purchasing decision.

Scenario: You and your cousin must pick one movie for family night with your parents and three younger siblings (ages 5, 6, and 8), and you want to watch a complex sci-fi movie with creative ideas that interest you, while your cousin wants to watch a simple animated movie because the complicated plot and some scary scenes might confuse or frighten the little kids. You need to agree on one movie in the next 10 minutes before everyone sits down to watch together.
Scenario_rev: You and your cousin must pick one movie for family night with your parents and three younger siblings (ages 5, 6, and 8), and you want to watch a simple animated movie because the complicated plot and some scary scenes might confuse or frighten the little kids, while your cousin wants to watch a complex sci-fi movie with creative ideas that interest them. You need to agree on one movie in the next 10 minutes before everyone sits down to watch together.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values choosing content that matches your own intellectual interests and independent preferences, while Universalism prioritizes protecting young children's emotional wellbeing and ensuring everyone can enjoy the experience without fear or confusion.
Cooperation Rationale: Both must agree on a single movie since the whole family watches together on one TV, and both benefit from a pleasant family evening. The decision requires compromise because only one movie can be played for everyone.

Scenario: You and your friend are building a robot for the regional science fair in three days and only have time to program one main feature, and you want to code an experimental program where the robot writes its own creative poetry, while your friend wants to build a page-turning arm that helps students with hand disabilities read books by themselves. You must decide today which single feature to build and demonstrate at the competition.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are building a robot for the regional science fair in three days and only have time to program one main feature, and you want to build a page-turning arm that helps students with hand disabilities read books by themselves, while your friend wants to code an experimental program where the robot writes its own creative poetry. You must decide today which single feature to build and demonstrate at the competition.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives wanting to explore your own creative programming interests and express independent technical ideas, while Universalism prioritizes helping people with disabilities and creating something that improves others' quality of life.
Cooperation Rationale: Both students must work together on one robot project with limited time and shared equipment, and both receive the same competition score. Success requires agreeing on which single feature to complete before the deadline.

Scenario: You and your neighbor are leading Saturday's neighborhood clean-up with 15 volunteers and only three hours of time, and you want to clean the wooded trail behind the houses where you've discovered interesting wildlife spots to explore, while your neighbor wants to clean Main Street and the playground where most families and elderly neighbors walk every single day. You must pick one location tonight to tell all the volunteers where to meet tomorrow morning.
Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor are leading Saturday's neighborhood clean-up with 15 volunteers and only three hours of time, and you want to clean Main Street and the playground where most families and elderly neighbors walk every single day, while your neighbor wants to clean the wooded trail behind the houses where they've discovered interesting wildlife spots to explore. You must pick one location tonight to tell all the volunteers where to meet tomorrow morning.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values following your own curiosity and exploring personally interesting places independently, while Universalism prioritizes helping the greatest number of people by cleaning the spaces most community members use daily.
Cooperation Rationale: Both organizers must coordinate one unified clean-up event with limited volunteers and time, and both benefit from a cleaner neighborhood. They cannot split the small group of volunteers without making both efforts ineffective.

Scenario: You and your classmate are designing one poster about healthy eating for the school hallway, and you want to use edgy humor with cartoon junk foods attacking people in exaggerated ways, while your classmate wants to use soft colors and smiling kids eating fruits so students with eating problems don't feel scared or judged. You must submit one final poster design to the principal by Friday afternoon to get it approved and printed.
Scenario_rev: You and your classmate are designing one poster about healthy eating for the school hallway, and you want to use soft colors and smiling kids eating fruits so students with eating problems don't feel scared or judged, while your classmate wants to use edgy humor with cartoon junk foods attacking people in exaggerated ways. You must submit one final poster design to the principal by Friday afternoon to get it approved and printed.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives wanting to express your own creative vision using bold, unconventional artistic choices, while Universalism prioritizes protecting vulnerable students from messages that might trigger anxiety or make them feel bad about themselves.
Cooperation Rationale: Both students must create one poster together for their joint grade, and both benefit from having their work displayed. Only one design can be submitted, requiring collaboration and agreement on a single artistic approach.

Scenario: You and your co-captain must choose one practice plan for tomorrow's soccer training with your 12-player youth team, and you want to drill an advanced attacking strategy that matches your fast playing style, while your co-captain wants to practice basic passing skills so the three weakest players get more touches and build confidence. You must agree on one practice plan tonight to tell the coach so she can prepare the right equipment and field setup.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-captain must choose one practice plan for tomorrow's soccer training with your 12-player youth team, and you want to practice basic passing skills so the three weakest players get more touches and build confidence, while your co-captain wants to drill an advanced attacking strategy that matches their fast playing style. You must agree on one practice plan tonight to tell the coach so she can prepare the right equipment and field setup.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values pursuing your own preferred playing style and independently chosen strategies, while Universalism prioritizes fairness and helping struggling teammates improve so everyone benefits from equal development opportunities.
Cooperation Rationale: Both captains must present one unified practice plan to the coach and lead the same session together, and both benefit from team improvement. They share leadership responsibilities and cannot run two separate practices simultaneously.

Scenario: You and your sibling share the family's only computer, and you want to install Python programming software and several coding tools that will slow down the computer, while your sibling wants to keep only simple programs like a word processor and internet browser so your parents and younger brother can use it easily without confusion. You must agree tonight on what to install because your dad is resetting the computer tomorrow morning to remove a virus.
Scenario_rev: You and your sibling share the family's only computer, and you want to keep only simple programs like a word processor and internet browser so your parents and younger brother can use it easily without confusion, while your sibling wants to install Python programming software and several coding tools that will slow down the computer. You must agree tonight on what to install because your dad is resetting the computer tomorrow morning to remove a virus.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives wanting to install tools that support your independent learning interests and personal goals, while Universalism prioritizes making sure all family members, including less tech-savvy ones, can use the shared computer without frustration.
Cooperation Rationale: Both siblings share one computer and must agree on the software setup since the computer gets wiped tomorrow, and both benefit from having a working, virus-free computer. Neither can make unilateral decisions about family-shared equipment.

Scenario: You and your friend are starting a coding club at school with weekly Tuesday meetings, and you want to teach advanced robot programming and game design that challenge your own skills, while your friend wants to teach basic Scratch animation so complete beginners and younger students can join without feeling lost or frustrated. You must submit one club description and meeting plan to the principal by Wednesday to get approved and reserve the computer lab.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are starting a coding club at school with weekly Tuesday meetings, and you want to teach basic Scratch animation so complete beginners and younger students can join without feeling lost or frustrated, while your friend wants to teach advanced robot programming and game design that challenge their own skills. You must submit one club description and meeting plan to the principal by Wednesday to get approved and reserve the computer lab.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values pursuing your own learning interests at an advanced level and choosing curriculum independently, while Universalism prioritizes making the club accessible and welcoming to all students regardless of their current skill level.
Cooperation Rationale: Both students must co-found one club together with a single curriculum plan, and both benefit from having an approved club to lead. The school only allows one coding club, requiring agreement on the teaching approach.

Scenario: You and your friend are painting a mural on the community center wall where people from many different countries and ages gather, and you want to use abstract geometric shapes and street-art symbols that express your personal artistic style, while your friend wants to paint simple images like flowers, holding hands, and suns that everyone from small children to elderly people can recognize and enjoy. You must agree on one final design to show the director on Friday to get permission and paint supplies.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are painting a mural on the community center wall where people from many different countries and ages gather, and you want to paint simple images like flowers, holding hands, and suns that everyone from small children to elderly people can recognize and enjoy, while your friend wants to use abstract geometric shapes and street-art symbols that express their personal artistic style. You must agree on one final design to show the director on Friday to get permission and paint supplies.
