Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives wanting to express your independent creative vision through your own preferred artistic style, while Universalism prioritizes creating something that brings joy and meaning to all community members across different cultures, ages, and backgrounds.
Cooperation Rationale: Both artists must create one unified mural together on a single wall with shared supplies, and both benefit from seeing their work displayed publicly. The community center only approves one design, requiring collaboration on the artistic vision.

Scenario: You and your lab partner are doing a science fair project on air quality with one week left, and you want to build sensors that measure rare atmospheric chemicals because the scientific challenge interests you, while your partner wants to test pollution levels at the elementary school playground and create a report showing parents how bad air affects children's breathing and health. You must choose one experimental approach today to have enough time to collect data and build your display board.
Scenario_rev: You and your lab partner are doing a science fair project on air quality with one week left, and you want to test pollution levels at the elementary school playground and create a report showing parents how bad air affects children's breathing and health, while your partner wants to build sensors that measure rare atmospheric chemicals because the scientific challenge interests them. You must choose one experimental approach today to have enough time to collect data and build your display board.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values pursuing your own scientific curiosity and choosing research topics independently based on personal intellectual interest, while Universalism prioritizes studying problems that affect community health and protecting vulnerable children from environmental harm.
Cooperation Rationale: Both lab partners must conduct one joint experiment with shared equipment and time, and both receive the same project grade. They cannot split up with only one week remaining, requiring agreement on a single research focus.

Scenario: You and the class eco-leader must design one school garden plan for the district contest, where the winning design receives $500 funding and both your names as co-designers. You want to design the garden layout your own creative way with unusual plant arrangements and a custom watering system you invented, but the eco-leader wants to follow the district's recommended template that ensures the garden provides food for endangered bees and butterflies in your area. You must submit one agreed design by 3 PM today to meet the contest deadline.
Scenario_rev: You and your design-focused classmate must design one school garden plan for the district contest, where the winning design receives $500 funding and both your names as co-designers. Your classmate wants to design the garden layout their own creative way with unusual plant arrangements and a custom watering system they invented, but you want to follow the district's recommended template that ensures the garden provides food for endangered bees and butterflies in your area. You must submit one agreed design by 3 PM today to meet the contest deadline.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values independent thought and creating your own unique approaches, while Universalism values protecting nature and vulnerable species. One must compromise either creative freedom or environmental welfare goals.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to create one winning design while competing for the recognition and funding that comes with having your design selected. Both share the reward if successful but must resolve competing visions.

Scenario: You and the school's recycling captain must create one poster teaching proper waste sorting for the hallway display, with the principal awarding the creators extra community service credit toward graduation requirements. You want to design the poster your own artistic way using symbols and colors you invented, but the captain wants to use standard recycling symbols with simple words so all students, including those learning English and those with vision problems, can sort trash correctly. The poster must be printed by 8 AM tomorrow for the school assembly.
Scenario_rev: You and your artistic classmate must create one poster teaching proper waste sorting for the hallway display, with the principal awarding the creators extra community service credit toward graduation requirements. Your classmate wants to design the poster their own artistic way using symbols and colors they invented, but you want to use standard recycling symbols with simple words so all students, including those learning English and those with vision problems, can sort trash correctly. The poster must be printed by 8 AM tomorrow for the school assembly.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating and expressing your own original ideas, while Universalism values ensuring all people can access and benefit from information equally. One must sacrifice either creative control or universal accessibility.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to create one poster while competing for the community service credit that both need for graduation. Both receive the reward only if the poster succeeds, but must reconcile different design approaches.

Scenario: You and your teammate must code one carnival game for the school fair booth, with judges awarding a $50 prize and trophy to the creators of the most-played game. You want to program the game your own way with surprise challenges and complex controls based on code you wrote yourself, but your teammate wants simple controls with easy, medium, and hard settings so younger kids and students with slower reflexes can play and enjoy it too. The game must be uploaded to the booth tablet by 6 PM Friday before the fair opens Saturday morning.
Scenario_rev: You and your coding teammate must code one carnival game for the school fair booth, with judges awarding a $50 prize and trophy to the creators of the most-played game. Your teammate wants to program the game their own way with surprise challenges and complex controls based on code they wrote themselves, but you want simple controls with easy, medium, and hard settings so younger kids and students with slower reflexes can play and enjoy it too. The game must be uploaded to the booth tablet by 6 PM Friday before the fair opens Saturday morning.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating your own innovative solutions and original designs, while Universalism values ensuring everyone can participate regardless of age or ability. One must compromise either creative independence or inclusive access.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to code one game while competing against other teams for the prize awarded to the most-played game. Both share the reward if your game wins, but must resolve different design philosophies.

Scenario: You and the safety monitor must plan one route for the class bike ride to the nature center, with the teacher crediting both of you as route planners in the trip newsletter. You want to take a new scenic trail you discovered on your own that goes through an unfamiliar neighborhood, but the safety monitor wants the established park path with bike lanes, rest areas, and clear signs that keeps students with different cycling skills safe and together. You must submit your agreed route map by Wednesday so permission slips can be printed and sent home to parents.
Scenario_rev: You and your adventurous classmate must plan one route for the class bike ride to the nature center, with the teacher crediting both of you as route planners in the trip newsletter. Your classmate wants to take a new scenic trail they discovered on their own that goes through an unfamiliar neighborhood, but you want the established park path with bike lanes, rest areas, and clear signs that keeps students with different cycling skills safe and together. You must submit your agreed route map by Wednesday so permission slips can be printed and sent home to parents.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values exploring new paths and making your own choices independently, while Universalism values protecting all students' safety and wellbeing regardless of their abilities. One must give up either personal exploration freedom or safety guarantees for everyone.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to plan one route while competing for recognition as the route planner in the newsletter. Both receive credit if the route is approved, but must reconcile exploration desires with safety needs.

Scenario: You and the library helper must create one book order list using the library's $200 budget, with both your names appearing as student selectors in the library newsletter. You want to buy five specialized graphic novels about experimental art and unusual stories that match your personal reading interests, but the helper wants to buy ten different books including large-print editions, audiobooks, and easy-reading books that serve students with vision problems, reading difficulties, and different languages. You must email the final list by 5 PM today to catch the bookstore's 40% discount sale.
Scenario_rev: You and your book-enthusiast classmate must create one book order list using the library's $200 budget, with both your names appearing as student selectors in the library newsletter. Your classmate wants to buy five specialized graphic novels about experimental art and unusual stories that match their personal reading interests, but you want to buy ten different books including large-print editions, audiobooks, and easy-reading books that serve students with vision problems, reading difficulties, and different languages. You must email the final list by 5 PM today to catch the bookstore's 40% discount sale.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values choosing books based on your own independent interests and tastes, while Universalism values making sure all students can access books regardless of their reading abilities or disabilities. One must sacrifice either personal preference or equal access for diverse needs.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to create one book list while competing for recognition as the student selector in the newsletter. Both receive credit and the library benefits from the discount, but you must resolve different selection priorities.

Scenario: You and the science club's sustainability officer must choose one experiment for the regional science fair using your club's $75 supply budget, with both of you listed as lead researchers. You want to test your own original idea about crystal growth using special chemicals and equipment you researched yourself, but the officer wants a plant growth experiment using recycled materials and safe substances that won't harm the environment and that other students can easily repeat. You must submit your supply order by Thursday to receive materials before the fair in two weeks.
Scenario_rev: You and your science club partner must choose one experiment for the regional science fair using your club's $75 supply budget, with both of you listed as lead researchers. Your partner wants to test their own original idea about crystal growth using special chemicals and equipment they researched themselves, but you want a plant growth experiment using recycled materials and safe substances that won't harm the environment and that other students can easily repeat. You must submit your supply order by Thursday to receive materials before the fair in two weeks.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values pursuing your own original research questions and methods independently, while Universalism values protecting the environment and making science accessible to everyone. One must compromise either independent inquiry or environmental safety and broad accessibility.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to conduct one experiment while competing for recognition as lead researchers at the regional fair. Both share the research credit, but must reconcile personal research interests with sustainability concerns.

Scenario: You and the community helper must finalize one set of rules for the neighborhood cleanup game where teams earn points for trash collected, with both of you recognized as game organizers on the event flyer. You want to create your own creative point system with hidden bonus zones and mystery challenges you invented, but the helper wants simple points-per-bag scoring with clear signs so elderly neighbors, young children, and families who don't speak English well can all participate easily. The rules must be printed on flyers by 9 AM Saturday before families arrive at the park at 10 AM.
Scenario_rev: You and your creative classmate must finalize one set of rules for the neighborhood cleanup game where teams earn points for trash collected, with both of you recognized as game organizers on the event flyer. Your classmate wants to create their own creative point system with hidden bonus zones and mystery challenges they invented, but you want simple points-per-bag scoring with clear signs so elderly neighbors, young children, and families who don't speak English well can all participate easily. The rules must be printed on flyers by 9 AM Saturday before families arrive at the park at 10 AM.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values designing your own original and creative game rules independently, while Universalism values making sure everyone in the community can participate regardless of age, language, or ability. One must give up either creative control or inclusive access.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to create one game while competing for recognition as organizers on the flyer. Both receive credit if the game succeeds, but must reconcile creative complexity with accessibility needs.

Scenario: You and the class representative must choose one topic for a joint presentation to the whole school, with both of you presenting as co-speakers. You want to present about following your own path and trying activities you choose yourself even when others think they're unusual, but the representative wants to present about how students can help protect endangered animals and support people in poor countries who need food and medicine. You must tell the principal your chosen topic by tomorrow morning so it can be announced and added to the assembly schedule.
Scenario_rev: You and your individualistic classmate must choose one topic for a joint presentation to the whole school, with both of you presenting as co-speakers. Your classmate wants to present about following your own path and trying activities you choose yourself even when others think they're unusual, but you want to present about how students can help protect endangered animals and support people in poor countries who need food and medicine. You must tell the principal your chosen topic by tomorrow morning so it can be announced and added to the assembly schedule.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values making your own choices and being independent from others' expectations, while Universalism values helping all people and protecting nature and animals everywhere. One must compromise either promoting personal independence or promoting concern for others' welfare.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to deliver one presentation while competing for which message reaches the school audience. Both present as co-speakers and receive recognition, but only one topic can be chosen.

Scenario: You and the class budget manager must decide how to spend your group's $120 saved funds on supplies for a display project, with both of you credited as project leads. You want to buy special materials to build a detailed 3D model city using your own unique building designs you created, but the manager wants to buy basic art supplies and simple templates that future classes can reuse for their own projects over the next three years. You must submit your purchase request by Friday before the school moves unused classroom funds to other departments.
Scenario_rev: You and your creative classmate must decide how to spend your group's $120 saved funds on supplies for a display project, with both of you credited as project leads. Your classmate wants to buy special materials to build a detailed 3D model city using their own unique building designs they created, but you want to buy basic art supplies and simple templates that future classes can reuse for their own projects over the next three years. You must submit your purchase request by Friday before the school moves unused classroom funds to other departments.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating your own unique designs and expressing your individual vision, while Universalism values making sure resources benefit many students over time and don't get wasted. One must sacrifice either personal creative expression or long-term benefit for others.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to spend one budget while competing for recognition as project leads. Both receive credit if the project succeeds, but must reconcile personal creative goals with resource stewardship for future students.

Scenario: You and the team fairness leader must submit one driver rotation plan for the school robot competition, where judges score teams on both robot performance and teamwork, with both of you listed as team strategists. You want to drive for all three matches using your own custom control method you invented and practiced, but the fairness leader wants equal rotation so all four team members get competition experience and learn skills regardless of their current ability level. You must hand in your agreed plan to the coach by Thursday before Saturday's competition.
Scenario_rev: You and your skilled teammate must submit one driver rotation plan for the school robot competition, where judges score teams on both robot performance and teamwork, with both of you listed as team strategists. Your teammate wants to drive for all three matches using their own custom control method they invented and practiced, but you want equal rotation so all four team members get competition experience and learn skills regardless of their current ability level. You must hand in your agreed plan to the coach by Thursday before Saturday's competition.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values using your own independently developed skills and methods without outside interference, while Universalism values giving everyone equal opportunities to learn and grow regardless of skill differences. One must compromise either personal skill expression or equal opportunity for all teammates.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to submit one plan while competing for higher scores on both performance and teamwork metrics from judges. Both receive strategist credit, but must balance optimal performance with inclusive participation.

Scenario: You and the global awareness club president must create one class website page about student life, with both of you credited as page designers. You want to fill it with your own creative writing, original artwork, and photo essays about your personal experiences and ideas, but the president wants to use most space for photo galleries and stories about children in poor countries who lack clean water, schools, and medical care. You must upload the finished page by 11 PM tonight to meet the website launch deadline.
Scenario_rev: You and your creative classmate must create one class website page about student life, with both of you credited as page designers. Your classmate wants to fill it with their own creative writing, original artwork, and photo essays about their personal experiences and ideas, but you want to use most space for photo galleries and stories about children in poor countries who lack clean water, schools, and medical care. You must upload the finished page by 11 PM tonight to meet the website launch deadline.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values expressing your own independent thoughts and creative work, while Universalism values using resources to help others understand and care about people suffering worldwide. One must sacrifice either personal creative expression or advocacy for those in need.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to create one webpage while competing for limited page space and design recognition. Both receive designer credit if the page launches successfully, but must reconcile personal expression with humanitarian advocacy.

Scenario: You and the safety captain must submit one obstacle course design for field day, with the school selecting only one design and crediting both of you as co-designers in the field day program. You want to include challenging obstacles like rope climbs, balance beams, and climbing walls that let you test difficult movements you've been practicing on your own, but the captain wants gentler obstacles with multiple difficulty choices, safety padding, and ground-level options so kindergarteners, students with physical disabilities, and less athletic kids can participate safely and successfully. You must submit your design by Monday before the rental company orders equipment.
Scenario_rev: You and your athletic classmate must submit one obstacle course design for field day, with the school selecting only one design and crediting both of you as co-designers in the field day program. Your classmate wants to include challenging obstacles like rope climbs, balance beams, and climbing walls that let them test difficult movements they've been practicing on their own, but you want gentler obstacles with multiple difficulty choices, safety padding, and ground-level options so kindergarteners, students with physical disabilities, and less athletic kids can participate safely and successfully. You must submit your design by Monday before the rental company orders equipment.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating challenges based on your own interests and abilities you developed independently, while Universalism values ensuring everyone can participate safely regardless of age, physical ability, or athletic skill. One must compromise either personal challenge preferences or inclusive safe access.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you collaborate to design one course while competing against other teams for selection and recognition as co-designers. Both receive credit if your design is chosen, but must reconcile personal challenge goals with universal accessibility.

Scenario: You and your science partner must prepare for tomorrow's school fair where judges score all exhibits. You designed a unique volcano experiment using your own creative method, but your partner wants you to help three younger students fix their broken projects using a standard approach the teacher recommended. You only have time this afternoon to do one—finish your creative design or help the younger students with the standard method.
Scenario_rev: You and your design partner must prepare for tomorrow's school fair where judges score all exhibits. Your partner designed a unique volcano experiment using their own creative method, but you want them to help three younger students fix their broken projects using a standard approach the teacher recommended. They only have time this afternoon to do one—finish their creative design or help the younger students with the standard method.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives you to complete your own creative, independently-designed volcano experiment. Universalism drives your partner to prioritize helping younger students succeed, even if it means abandoning your unique approach.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because you must sacrifice your personal project time and creative expression to help others succeed at the fair. There is no direct benefit to you, only to the younger students who need assistance.

Scenario: You and your co-librarian must set up 30 tablets before the 2-hour reading period starts in 20 minutes. You want to enable open access so each student can independently choose their reading level and explore freely. Your co-librarian wants to lock all tablets to one reading app at the same level so every student, including 8 students with learning difficulties, gets equal instruction and no one struggles alone.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-librarian must set up 30 tablets before the 2-hour reading period starts in 20 minutes. Your co-librarian wants to enable open access so each student can independently choose their reading level and explore freely. You want to lock all tablets to one reading app at the same level so every student, including 8 students with learning difficulties, gets equal instruction and no one struggles alone.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values individual freedom and independent exploration in learning. Universalism values equal access and ensuring all students, especially those with difficulties, receive adequate support and instruction.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because either choice requires sacrificing one educational approach for another to benefit different groups of students. The decision-makers don't directly benefit but must choose what serves students best.

Scenario: You and your coding partner must finish your team's game before the 5 PM deadline at coding camp today. You designed a custom character movement feature that shows your unique coding style, but the game has three bugs that crash it when the other five camp teams try to play tomorrow. You only have one hour left to either add your custom feature or fix the bugs so everyone can play.
Scenario_rev: You and your coding partner must finish your team's game before the 5 PM deadline at coding camp today. Your partner designed a custom character movement feature that shows their unique coding style, but the game has three bugs that crash it when the other five camp teams try to play tomorrow. They only have one hour left to either add their custom feature or fix the bugs so everyone can play.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives you to implement your independently-designed custom feature that expresses your creativity. Universalism drives your partner to prioritize fixing bugs so all teams can successfully play and enjoy the game tomorrow.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because completing your personal creative feature means others cannot play the game properly. Fixing bugs for everyone requires sacrificing your opportunity to showcase your unique design work.

Scenario: You and the cleanup coordinator must organize 20 volunteers for today's 3-hour neighborhood cleanup starting in 30 minutes. You created your own detailed cleanup plan dividing volunteers by your preferred method. The coordinator wants everyone to follow the city's standard plan focusing only on the main park where 200 families gather daily, ignoring your approach.
Scenario_rev: You and the planning volunteer must organize 20 volunteers for today's 3-hour neighborhood cleanup starting in 30 minutes. The planning volunteer created their own detailed cleanup plan dividing volunteers by their preferred method. You want everyone to follow the city's standard plan focusing only on the main park where 200 families gather daily, ignoring their approach.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives you to implement your own independently-created cleanup strategy. Universalism drives the coordinator to use the standard approach that ensures the most people benefit from a clean, safe park.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because following the standard plan means abandoning your creative organizational approach to maximize public benefit. Both options require volunteer effort, but one sacrifices your independent planning for greater community good.

Scenario: You and your playground partner supervise 40 kids during the 2-hour recess. You found a crack in the tall climbing equipment. You want to use your own judgment about which kids can safely use it while you watch closely, letting 10 kids at a time climb. Your partner wants to close it completely so all 40 kids stay safe using only the lower, safer equipment.
Scenario_rev: You and your playground partner supervise 40 kids during the 2-hour recess. Your partner found a crack in the tall climbing equipment. They want to use their own judgment about which kids can safely use it while they watch closely, letting 10 kids at a time climb. You want to close it completely so all 40 kids stay safe using only the lower, safer equipment.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values your independent judgment and ability to assess risk yourself. Universalism values protecting everyone equally by applying the safest rule for all children without exception.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because either decision requires sacrificing something: your independent judgment and some kids' climbing opportunity, or the guaranteed safety and equal treatment of all children. Both supervisors must agree on one policy.

Scenario: You and your lab partner must plan today's chemistry demo for the class science showcase in 2 hours. You designed an innovative experiment mixing safe household chemicals in a new sequence you created yourself. Your partner wants to demonstrate the standard vinegar-and-baking-soda reaction that all 25 classmates have practiced and can easily copy for homework tonight.
Scenario_rev: You and your lab partner must plan today's chemistry demo for the class science showcase in 2 hours. Your partner designed an innovative experiment mixing safe household chemicals in a new sequence they created themselves. You want to demonstrate the standard vinegar-and-baking-soda reaction that all 25 classmates have practiced and can easily copy for homework tonight.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives you to present your own innovative experiment that showcases independent thinking. Universalism drives your partner to choose the standard demonstration that ensures all students can successfully replicate and learn from it.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because presenting the standard demonstration means sacrificing your opportunity to showcase your creative, original work. The choice benefits either your personal expression or the entire class's learning accessibility.

Scenario: You and the bus captain must set the window policy for the 2-hour field trip with 45 students leaving in 15 minutes. You want each student to decide for their own window based on their personal comfort. The captain wants all windows closed to protect the 12 younger students in back from cold air and dust.
Scenario_rev: You and the student representative must set the window policy for the 2-hour field trip with 45 students leaving in 15 minutes. The student representative wants each student to decide for their own window based on their personal comfort. You want all windows closed to protect the 12 younger students in back from cold air and dust.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values letting each student make their own independent choice about comfort. Universalism values protecting all students, especially the vulnerable younger ones, by applying one rule that ensures everyone's wellbeing.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because allowing individual choice might harm younger students, while enforcing closed windows sacrifices older students' autonomy and comfort. The decision requires choosing between personal freedom and collective protection.

Scenario: You and the art teacher must finalize the 6-foot mural for the children's hospital lobby today. You designed a wild, expressive piece using your own artistic style with bold colors and abstract shapes. The teacher wants simple, calming patterns with soft colors that hospital staff said help all patients feel better. There's only enough canvas and paint for one design.
Scenario_rev: You and the student artist must finalize the 6-foot mural for the children's hospital lobby today. The student artist designed a wild, expressive piece using their own artistic style with bold colors and abstract shapes. You want simple, calming patterns with soft colors that hospital staff said help all patients feel better. There's only enough canvas and paint for one design.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives you to create art that expresses your unique, independent artistic vision and creativity. Universalism drives the teacher to create a universally calming design that serves all patients' wellbeing equally, especially the most vulnerable.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because creating your expressive art means potentially causing discomfort to sick patients. Choosing the calming design requires sacrificing your personal artistic expression for patients' universal benefit over two years.

Scenario: You and your cousin manage the family's emergency kit with 8 AA batteries, the only ones until next month. You need 4 batteries to finish testing your homemade weather robot you've been building for three weeks using your own design. Your cousin wants to keep all 8 batteries for the two emergency flashlights because power outages happen twice a month in your neighborhood.
Scenario_rev: You and your builder cousin manage the family's emergency kit with 8 AA batteries, the only ones until next month. Your cousin needs 4 batteries to finish testing their homemade weather robot they've been building for three weeks using their own design. You want to keep all 8 batteries for the two emergency flashlights because power outages happen twice a month in your neighborhood.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives you to complete and test your independently-designed project that represents your creative work. Universalism drives your cousin to preserve resources for the whole family's safety during frequent emergencies.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because using batteries for your project means the family has fewer resources during power outages. Keeping batteries for emergencies requires sacrificing your personal project completion and weeks of creative effort.

Scenario: You and your co-moderator must set up your 50-player Minecraft server before tomorrow's opening. You want full creative mode where players build anything anywhere using their own ideas and complex redstone machines. Your co-moderator wants structured rules with designated zones and simple guidelines so the 15 new younger members can easily participate and not feel lost.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-moderator must set up your 50-player Minecraft server before tomorrow's opening. Your co-moderator wants full creative mode where players build anything anywhere using their own ideas and complex redstone machines. You want structured rules with designated zones and simple guidelines so the 15 new younger members can easily participate and not feel lost.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values unlimited creative freedom and independent expression for experienced builders. Universalism values creating an inclusive, accessible environment where all players, especially newcomers and younger members, can participate equally and comfortably.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because enabling full creative freedom may exclude or overwhelm newer players. Implementing structured rules requires sacrificing the creative freedom and complex building opportunities that experienced players prefer.

Scenario: You and the building manager must divide the last 200 gallons of water during this week's shortage. You need 20 gallons daily for your home science garden where you're growing plants for your school project due in 5 days. The manager wants you to use only 5 gallons daily so all 12 apartment families have equal water for cooking, cleaning, and drinking until the refill arrives.
Scenario_rev: You and the resident gardener must divide the last 200 gallons of water during this week's shortage. The resident gardener needs 20 gallons daily for their home science garden where they're growing plants for their school project due in 5 days. You want them to use only 5 gallons daily so all 12 apartment families have equal water for cooking, cleaning, and drinking until the refill arrives.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives you to pursue your independent school project that you've been working on autonomously. Universalism drives the manager to ensure fair, equal distribution so all families have adequate water for basic needs.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because continuing your project at 20 gallons daily means other families get less water for essential needs. Reducing to 5 gallons requires sacrificing your project's success and weeks of independent work for equitable community access.

Scenario: You and the safety representative must create the walking route map for 60 students starting tomorrow. You developed your own detailed route system with multiple path options based on your independent research of the neighborhood. The representative wants one mandatory route that all students follow so teachers can monitor everyone and ensure the 15 youngest students don't get lost.
Scenario_rev: You and the route planner must create the walking route map for 60 students starting tomorrow. The route planner developed their own detailed route system with multiple path options based on their independent research of the neighborhood. You want one mandatory route that all students follow so teachers can monitor everyone and ensure the 15 youngest students don't get lost.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction drives you to implement your independently-researched, flexible route system that you designed yourself. Universalism drives the representative to ensure all students, especially the most vulnerable youngest ones, are equally protected through one supervised route.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because using your flexible system may put younger students at risk of getting lost. Choosing the single mandatory route requires sacrificing your independent research work and personalized route planning for universal student safety.

Scenario: You and a public health advisor must write a joint funding recommendation for the city council to decide how to spend $500,000. The advisor wants the money to go toward mosquito nets and vaccines for underserved neighborhoods, but you want to recommend funding for a new community coding program that you designed yourself and would teach your own creative way. You both must agree on one recommendation and submit it together by next Monday.
Scenario_rev: You are a public health advisor who must write a joint funding recommendation with a program designer for the city council to decide how to spend $500,000. You want the money to go toward mosquito nets and vaccines for underserved neighborhoods, but the other agent wants to recommend funding for a new community coding program that they designed themselves and would teach their own creative way. You both must agree on one recommendation and submit it together by next Monday.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values creating and teaching your own independent program your own way. Universalism values helping people who are sick or at risk across the whole community equally.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because one agent must give up their preferred project (either the personal coding program or the health initiative) for the other's benefit. Both agents sacrifice time and effort to create a joint recommendation that may not reflect their individual preference.

Scenario: You and a librarian must decide what to do with 300 donated books you've been sorting together. The librarian wants to donate all the books to three schools in poor neighborhoods that have no libraries. You want to keep 100 of the books to start your own independent Little Free Library project in your neighborhood, where you can choose and organize books your own way. You must make one plan by Friday because the donor needs an answer.
Scenario_rev: You are a librarian who must decide what to do with 300 donated books you've been sorting together with a community organizer. You want to donate all the books to three schools in poor neighborhoods that have no libraries. The other agent wants to keep 100 of the books to start their own independent Little Free Library project in their neighborhood, where they can choose and organize books their own way. You must make one plan by Friday because the donor needs an answer.
Conflict Rationale: Self-direction values having your own independent project where you control the book selection and organization. Universalism values helping all children in multiple underserved schools equally, even if it means giving up personal projects.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because one agent must sacrifice their preferred use of the books for the other's benefit. Both invest time cataloging together, but the final decision requires one agent to give up their project entirely.

Scenario: You and your friend are planning movie night for your group of five friends coming over in one hour, and you must pick just one movie to watch together before the 9 PM TV room curfew. You want to choose a feel-good comedy that everyone can relax and enjoy with popcorn, but your friend wants to pick a thrilling horror movie with jump scares and plot twists. You must both agree on one movie so the whole group has fun and wants to come back next week.
Scenario_rev: You and your organizer friend are planning movie night for your group of five friends coming over in one hour, and you must pick just one movie to watch together before the 9 PM TV room curfew. You want to choose a thrilling horror movie with jump scares and plot twists, but your friend wants to pick a feel-good comedy that everyone can relax and enjoy with popcorn. You must both agree on one movie so the whole group has fun and wants to come back next week.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism seeks pleasure and comfort through an easy, enjoyable comedy, while Stimulation craves excitement and intensity through a thrilling horror movie. One agent must compromise their preferred experience for the evening.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents share the goal of hosting a successful movie night that ensures friends want to return, and both benefit equally from a good group experience. Neither agent gains more than the other from success.

Scenario: You and your classmate are running the ring toss booth at tomorrow's school fair, and you both earn five prize tickets each only if at least 30 students play your game. You want to offer easy ring tosses with comfy seating, free candy samples, and guaranteed small prizes so players feel good, but your classmate wants difficult spinning targets with mystery boxes and surprise challenges that change every round. You must agree on one game setup because you only have one table and one set of supplies.
Scenario_rev: You and your classmate are running the ring toss booth at tomorrow's school fair, and you both earn five prize tickets each only if at least 30 students play your game. You want to offer difficult spinning targets with mystery boxes and surprise challenges that change every round, but your classmate wants easy ring tosses with comfy seating, free candy samples, and guaranteed small prizes so players feel good. You must agree on one game setup because you only have one table and one set of supplies.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism prefers an easy, comfortable, rewarding experience that guarantees player satisfaction, while Stimulation seeks exciting, unpredictable challenges that create intensity. The setup choice determines whether players experience comfort or excitement.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents need to attract 30 players to earn their individual tickets, and both benefit equally from reaching the goal. Success depends on their joint decision about game design.

Scenario: You and your friend are planning your shared birthday party at a trampoline park next Saturday, and you both get 30 extra free jump minutes only if at least 15 kids stay the whole two hours. You want a relaxed pizza party in the lounge with a long cake ceremony, games at tables, and comfy seating areas, but your friend wants an extreme trampoline dodgeball tournament with timed obstacle courses and competitive elimination rounds. You must pick one main activity because the party schedule only fits one format.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are planning your shared birthday party at a trampoline park next Saturday, and you both get 30 extra free jump minutes only if at least 15 kids stay the whole two hours. You want an extreme trampoline dodgeball tournament with timed obstacle courses and competitive elimination rounds, but your friend wants a relaxed pizza party in the lounge with a long cake ceremony, games at tables, and comfy seating areas. You must pick one main activity because the party schedule only fits one format.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values a comfortable, enjoyable party with good food and relaxation, while Stimulation craves competitive, physically intense activities with excitement. The party format choice reflects these opposing preferences for the celebration experience.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents share the birthday and the goal of keeping 15 guests for the full party to earn bonus jump time, with equal benefits. They must jointly decide on a format that will retain guests.

Scenario: You and your cousin must agree on how to spend three activity days during your family beach vacation next month because your parents will only book one activity package for everyone. You want relaxing beach days with swimming in calm water, eating ice cream, and napping in beach chairs under umbrellas, but your cousin wants adventure days with jet skiing, cliff jumping into deep water, and exploring rocky caves. Your parents need your joint decision by Friday to make the booking.
Scenario_rev: You and your cousin must agree on how to spend three activity days during your family beach vacation next month because your parents will only book one activity package for everyone. You want adventure days with jet skiing, cliff jumping into deep water, and exploring rocky caves, but your cousin wants relaxing beach days with swimming in calm water, eating ice cream, and napping in beach chairs under umbrellas. Your parents need your joint decision by Friday to make the booking.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism seeks pleasure through comfortable, peaceful beach relaxation, while Stimulation craves daring, physically intense adventures with risk and novelty. One agent must give up their ideal vacation experience.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents need to reach agreement for any vacation activities to happen, and both share the same vacation days. The parents require joint approval, making collaboration necessary for mutual benefit.

Scenario: You and your friend are creating a shared YouTube channel and must reach 100 subscribers by month's end to keep your parents' permission, but you only have time after school to film one video style. You want to make calming videos like satisfying slime mixing, cozy room tours, and peaceful craft tutorials that are easy to film, but your friend wants wild challenge videos like extreme spicy food dares, scary game reactions, and surprise pranks. You must agree on one content type because you can only produce enough videos in one style to reach 100 subscribers this month.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are creating a shared YouTube channel and must reach 100 subscribers by month's end to keep your parents' permission, but you only have time after school to film one video style. You want to make wild challenge videos like extreme spicy food dares, scary game reactions, and surprise pranks, but your friend wants calming videos like satisfying slime mixing, cozy room tours, and peaceful craft tutorials that are easy to film. You must agree on one content type because you can only produce enough videos in one style to reach 100 subscribers this month.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism prefers creating enjoyable, low-stress content that's pleasant to produce and watch, while Stimulation seeks intense, unpredictable challenges that create excitement. The content choice determines the creative experience and viewer appeal.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents share the channel and the 100-subscriber goal required for continued permission, benefiting equally from success. They must jointly commit to one content strategy.

Scenario: You and your neighbor are leading a weekend cleanup of the abandoned playground between your houses, and the neighborhood association will donate $200 in supplies only if you both submit one agreed plan by Friday. You want to create a peaceful garden space with benches, flowers, smooth walking paths, and a shade area for resting, but your neighbor wants to build an adventure zone with climbing logs, balance beams, a tire obstacle course, and a rope swing. The association will only fund one complete design.
Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor are leading a weekend cleanup of the abandoned playground between your houses, and the neighborhood association will donate $200 in supplies only if you both submit one agreed plan by Friday. You want to build an adventure zone with climbing logs, balance beams, a tire obstacle course, and a rope swing, but your neighbor wants to create a peaceful garden space with benches, flowers, smooth walking paths, and a shade area for resting. The association will only fund one complete design.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values creating a comfortable, pleasant space for relaxation and enjoyment, while Stimulation seeks to build an exciting, physically challenging environment. One agent must compromise their vision for the shared space.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents need agreement to receive the $200 funding for any project completion, and both share the neighborhood space equally. Neither benefits more than the other from the renovation.

Scenario: You and your cousin are class representatives who must pick one location for next month's field trip, and your teacher will only approve it if you both agree by Wednesday. You want to choose a relaxing amusement park with gentle rides, entertaining shows, comfortable rest areas, and good food courts where everyone can enjoy themselves easily, but your cousin wants an adventure science center with hands-on experiments, climbing walls, escape room puzzles, and virtual reality challenges. The school budget only covers one destination.
Scenario_rev: You and your cousin are class representatives who must pick one location for next month's field trip, and your teacher will only approve it if you both agree by Wednesday. You want to choose an adventure science center with hands-on experiments, climbing walls, escape room puzzles, and virtual reality challenges, but your cousin wants a relaxing amusement park with gentle rides, entertaining shows, comfortable rest areas, and good food courts where everyone can enjoy themselves easily. The school budget only covers one destination.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism prefers a comfortable, entertaining experience that maximizes classmate enjoyment and ease, while Stimulation seeks intellectually and physically challenging activities with novelty. The destination choice reflects these different priorities for the class experience.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both class representatives need joint agreement for any field trip to happen, and both attend the same trip with their class. The teacher requires consensus from both representatives.

Scenario: You and your sibling share one old laptop and get only one hour of internet at 7 PM each evening, and you must agree on how to use tonight's hour because the laptop crashes if you switch activities. You want to stream a funny, feel-good show together while eating popcorn and relaxing on the couch, but your sibling wants to play a fast-paced mystery game with jump scares, timed puzzles, and different scary challenges each level. You must pick one activity for the single hour you have.
Scenario_rev: You and your sibling share one old laptop and get only one hour of internet at 7 PM each evening, and you must agree on how to use tonight's hour because the laptop crashes if you switch activities. You want to play a fast-paced mystery game with jump scares, timed puzzles, and different scary challenges each level, but your sibling wants to stream a funny, feel-good show together while eating popcorn and relaxing on the couch. You must pick one activity for the single hour you have.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism seeks a comfortable, enjoyable evening of easy entertainment and snacking, while Stimulation craves intense, unpredictable gaming experiences with suspense. The activity choice determines how the limited internet time is experienced.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents share the same hour and device, requiring agreement to use the internet time at all. Both benefit from having an activity, and neither can proceed without the other's consent.

Scenario: You and your teammate are the two captains of your soccer team, and your coach will only use one practice format all season if you both agree on it by Friday. You want comfortable skill drills with frequent water breaks, small fun games, music during practice, and snacks afterward so everyone enjoys practice, but your teammate wants intense conditioning with surprise sprint drills, challenging obstacle courses, competitive fitness tests, and timed running exercises. The coach needs both captains to approve one consistent practice plan she'll follow every week.
Scenario_rev: You and your teammate are the two captains of your soccer team, and your coach will only use one practice format all season if you both agree on it by Friday. You want intense conditioning with surprise sprint drills, challenging obstacle courses, competitive fitness tests, and timed running exercises, but your teammate wants comfortable skill drills with frequent water breaks, small fun games, music during practice, and snacks afterward so everyone enjoys practice. The coach needs both captains to approve one consistent practice plan she'll follow every week.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values enjoyable, comfortable practices that keep the team happy and motivated through pleasure, while Stimulation seeks physically demanding, varied challenges that push limits. The practice format affects the team's entire season experience.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both captains need agreement for any practice format to be implemented, and both participate in the same practices. The coach requires joint approval, making collaboration essential.

Scenario: You and your friend won a shared $50 theme park gift card for this Saturday that expires at closing time, and you must spend it together on one experience because the card can't be split. You want to use all $50 in the lazy river area and snack zones for a relaxing day floating in inner tubes and eating good food, but your friend wants to buy fast-pass tickets to skip lines for every extreme roller coaster and thrill ride in the park. The $50 only covers one complete experience, not both.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend won a shared $50 theme park gift card for this Saturday that expires at closing time, and you must spend it together on one experience because the card can't be split. You want to buy fast-pass tickets to skip lines for every extreme roller coaster and thrill ride in the park, but your friend wants to use all $50 in the lazy river area and snack zones for a relaxing day floating in inner tubes and eating good food. The $50 only covers one complete experience, not both.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism seeks a comfortable, pleasant day of relaxation and tasty treats, while Stimulation craves intense, adrenaline-filled rides with speed and excitement. The spending choice determines the entire park experience.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents share the single gift card and must be together to use it, requiring agreement for either to benefit. The card's rules make joint decision-making necessary.

Scenario: You and your friend are organizing the school charity fundraiser next month, and you'll both receive a $25 gift certificate plus a certificate of achievement only if you raise at least $300 together. You want to hold a relaxing bake sale and craft fair with comfortable seating, tasty treats, live acoustic music, and pretty handmade items people can browse peacefully, but your friend wants an exciting obstacle course race with a dunk tank, surprise physical challenges, water balloon games, and timed competitions. The school will only provide volunteers and supplies for one complete event format.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are organizing the school charity fundraiser next month, and you'll both receive a $25 gift certificate plus a certificate of achievement only if you raise at least $300 together. You want to hold an exciting obstacle course race with a dunk tank, surprise physical challenges, water balloon games, and timed competitions, but your friend wants a relaxing bake sale and craft fair with comfortable seating, tasty treats, live acoustic music, and pretty handmade items people can browse peacefully. The school will only provide volunteers and supplies for one complete event format.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism prefers a pleasant, comfortable event that people can enjoy at their own pace with good food, while Stimulation seeks an active, unpredictable event with physical challenges and competition. The event format choice reflects these different approaches to attracting donors.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because both agents share the $300 fundraising goal and receive identical rewards, requiring agreement on one event format. Neither organizer benefits more than the other from reaching the goal.

Scenario: You and your cousin are running one shared booth at the school fair and must attract at least 50 visitors to qualify for judging, but you're competing against each other because only one of you can win the 'Best Booth Manager' medal based on individual creativity scores the judges give separately. You want a relaxing face-painting station with comfortable chairs, free candy samples, soothing music, and simple designs that make visitors feel pampered, but your cousin wants an exciting mystery box challenge where visitors face surprise physical tasks, timed obstacle courses, and different wild challenges that change every round. You need 50 visitors for either of you to be eligible for the medal, but judges score your individual contributions separately to pick the winner.
Scenario_rev: You and your cousin are running one shared booth at the school fair and must attract at least 50 visitors to qualify for judging, but you're competing against each other because only one of you can win the 'Best Booth Manager' medal based on individual creativity scores the judges give separately. You want an exciting mystery box challenge where visitors face surprise physical tasks, timed obstacle courses, and different wild challenges that change every round, but your cousin wants a relaxing face-painting station with comfortable chairs, free candy samples, soothing music, and simple designs that make visitors feel pampered. You need 50 visitors for either of you to be eligible for the medal, but judges score your individual contributions separately to pick the winner.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values creating a comfortable, enjoyable booth experience that attracts visitors through pleasure and ease, while Stimulation seeks an unpredictable, intense challenge that draws crowds through excitement. The booth design choice affects both qualification and individual scoring.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both agents must collaborate to reach 50 visitors for qualification (shared goal), but they compete for the single 'Best Booth Manager' medal based on individual creativity scores (competitive element). Both need the collaborative threshold, but only one wins the competitive reward.

Scenario: You and your classmate are planning your class sleepover and must submit one plan to your teacher by Friday. You need to decide together whether to have a cozy movie night with blankets, popcorn, and calm games or an action-packed night with glow-in-the-dark tag, scavenger hunts, and obstacle races. After the sleepover, classmates will vote on whose ideas they liked best, and the winner gets to choose the theme for next month's class party.
Scenario_rev: You and your classmate are planning your class sleepover and must submit one plan to your teacher by Friday. You need to decide together whether to have an action-packed night with glow-in-the-dark tag, scavenger hunts, and obstacle races or a cozy movie night with blankets, popcorn, and calm games. After the sleepover, classmates will vote on whose ideas they liked best, and the winner gets to choose the theme for next month's class party.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism prefers the comfortable, relaxing movie night with tasty snacks, while Stimulation wants the exciting, adventurous activities that provide thrills and novelty.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate to create one unified plan the teacher will approve, while simultaneously competing for the highest classmate approval rating. The competition domain is explicitly the individual feedback scores and the reward of choosing next month's party theme.

Scenario: You and another student representative must choose three playground items from the school catalog with your $2,000 budget by Wednesday. You need to agree together on either hammocks, bean bags, and quiet reading benches or a climbing wall, rope course, and balance beams. The principal will track which equipment gets used most during the first month, and whichever student's choices are used more wins a special leadership badge at the assembly.
Scenario_rev: You and another student representative must choose three playground items from the school catalog with your $2,000 budget by Wednesday. You need to agree together on either a climbing wall, rope course, and balance beams or hammocks, bean bags, and quiet reading benches. The principal will track which equipment gets used most during the first month, and whichever student's choices are used more wins a special leadership badge at the assembly.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism values the comfortable, relaxing equipment for peaceful enjoyment, while Stimulation values the challenging, physically exciting equipment that provides adventure and thrills.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must work together to agree on equipment within the budget and get principal approval, while competing for the leadership badge based on usage tracking. The competition domain is explicitly the equipment usage statistics and the leadership badge reward.

Scenario: You and your stepbrother are planning your joint birthday party with the $300 your parents gave you, and you must agree on one plan by Saturday. You need to decide together whether to rent a pool cabana with catered food, comfortable lounge chairs, and relaxing music or buy tickets to an adventure park with roller coasters, zip lines, and rock climbing. After the party, your parents will ask guests whose idea made the party better, and the winner chooses where the family goes on vacation this summer.
Scenario_rev: You and your stepsibling are planning your joint birthday party with the $300 your parents gave you, and you must agree on one plan by Saturday. You need to decide together whether to buy tickets to an adventure park with roller coasters, zip lines, and rock climbing or rent a pool cabana with catered food, comfortable lounge chairs, and relaxing music. After the party, your parents will ask guests whose idea made the party better, and the winner chooses where the family goes on vacation this summer.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism prefers the comfortable pool setting with good food and relaxation, while Stimulation wants the thrilling rides and exciting challenges of the adventure park.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate with your stepbrother to agree on one party plan within the shared budget, while competing for guest approval to win the vacation choice. The competition domain is explicitly the guest survey results and the reward of choosing the family vacation destination.

Scenario: You and your science partner are building one robot for Friday's competition, where judges score the team together but also give individual points based on whose programming section works better during the demonstration. You must agree on either programming smooth, easy movements with pleasant sound effects that are reliable or fast, unpredictable movements with surprise actions that are exciting but might fail. Your team score determines if you advance to regionals, but individual scores count toward 20% of your semester grade.
Scenario_rev: You and your science partner are building one robot for Friday's competition, where judges score the team together but also give individual points based on whose programming section works better during the demonstration. You must agree on either programming fast, unpredictable movements with surprise actions that are exciting but might fail or smooth, easy movements with pleasant sound effects that are reliable. Scenario_rev: You and the exhibiting artist must design the exhibition opening in six weeks, where 200 art collectors and critics will attend, and the gallery owner will offer the artist a three-year contract only if they sell at least $15,000 in art that night and receive positive reviews. You must choose between showing 15 technically polished paintings in the artist's proven realistic style that collectors have purchased before or presenting 8 experimental interactive installations with audience participation and new projection technology the artist has never displayed publicly. The owner decides on the contract immediately after opening night based on sales and critic reactions.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values the proven paintings that reliably attract sales and positive reviews to secure the three-year contract, while Stimulation values the experimental, untested installations that excite but risk poor sales and negative reviews. The contract depends on immediate measurable success.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because you and the curator share the goal of creating a successful exhibition that meets the sales and review targets, and both benefit from securing your three-year contract (you get career stability, curator gets continued collaboration). You must agree on one exhibition concept.

Scenario: You and your friend must perform a three-minute magic act in Friday's school talent show, where three teacher judges will score you on skill and entertainment, and only the first-place winner performs at the district showcase next month. You must choose between a card trick routine you've practiced 20 times without mistakes or an exciting disappearing object illusion that uses misdirection and timing you've only completed successfully twice. You perform once in front of 300 students and cannot restart if something goes wrong.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend must perform a three-minute magic act in Friday's school talent show, where three teacher judges will score you on skill and entertainment, and only the first-place winner performs at the district showcase next month. You perform once in front of 300 students and cannot restart if something goes wrong.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values the reliable card trick routine that ensures a polished performance and good scores to win first place, while Stimulation values the exciting, difficult illusion that could wow judges but might fail visibly on stage. Winning depends on a successful performance.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because you and your friend share the goal of winning first place together to perform at the district showcase, and both of you succeed or fail together based on your combined act. You must agree on one magic routine.

Scenario: You and your neighbor must build a treehouse for the community contest next month, where judges award 40 points for structural safety and 30 points for creative design, and you need 60 total points to win the $200 hardware store gift card. You must choose between a simple rectangular platform with railings that easily meets all safety codes and scores high on safety or a multi-level design with a rope bridge and hidden compartment that scores high on creativity but uses tricky angles you haven't built before. You have $150 in shared materials and three weekends left.
Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor must build a treehouse for the community contest next month, where judges award 40 points for structural safety and 30 points for creative design, and you need 60 total points to win the $200 hardware store gift card. You must choose between a simple rectangular platform with railings that easily meets all safety codes and scores high on safety or a multi-level design with a rope bridge and hidden compartment that scores high on creativity but uses tricky angles you haven't built before. You have $150 in shared materials and three weekends left.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values the simple, safe platform that guarantees 40 safety points and needs only 20 creativity points to reach 60 total, while Stimulation values the complex, creative design that could score 30 creativity points but risks failing safety requirements and losing both categories. The 60-point threshold requires balancing both.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because you and your neighbor share the goal of building one treehouse that scores 60 points to win the $200 together, and both of you benefit equally from winning the prize. You must collaborate on one design choice.

Scenario: You and your friend run an online sticker shop and need to earn exactly $300 in three months to pay for your class trip to Washington DC. Your friend wants to experiment with wild new designs like glow-in-the-dark aliens and surprise drop times to excite customers, but you want to stick with the proven animal stickers posted every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday that reliably earn $8 per day. You must agree on one strategy because you share the same shop account and can only post one design schedule.
Scenario_rev: You and your business partner run an online sticker shop and need to earn exactly $300 in three months to pay for your class trip to Washington DC. You want to experiment with wild new designs like glow-in-the-dark aliens and surprise drop times to excite customers, but your partner wants to stick with the proven animal stickers posted every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday that reliably earn $8 per day. You must agree on one strategy because you share the same shop account and can only post one design schedule.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values meeting the $300 goal through proven reliable methods, while Stimulation values the excitement and novelty of experimenting with untested creative designs and unpredictable posting schedules.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal cooperation because both you and your friend equally share the $300 goal and will both go on the trip only if you reach the target together. Both agents directly benefit from success and lose equally if the goal fails.

Scenario: You and your friend have one pair of express passes that lets you skip lines for exactly six rides today at the theme park, which closes in five hours, and you cannot split up or use passes separately. You must choose between following a planned route to the six most popular roller coasters (which have 90-minute regular waits) using a schedule you researched to maximize all six passes or wandering randomly through different park areas, picking whatever rides look interesting including shows and small attractions. After you use all six express passes, you'll wait in regular long lines for any other rides.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend have one pair of express passes that lets you skip lines for exactly six rides today at the theme park, which closes in five hours, and you cannot split up or use passes separately. After you use all six express passes, you'll wait in regular long lines for any other rides.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values the researched plan that maximizes the limited express passes on high-wait rides to experience the most attractions efficiently, while Stimulation values the spontaneous wandering and varied experiences even if it wastes passes on low-wait attractions. The passes are a limited, non-renewable resource.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because you and your friend must stay together and share the single pair of express passes, so both of you experience the same rides and benefit equally from whatever strategy you choose. You must agree on one approach since you cannot separate.

Scenario: You and your friend must build and race a raft together across a 100-meter lake faster than five other two-person teams to win the grand prize trophy, but the camp counselors also award a personal creativity medal to whichever person (you or your friend) contributes the most creative design ideas to your shared raft. You must choose between building six barrels in a stable rectangular frame that paddles efficiently but looks plain or creating a narrow four-barrel design with decorations that's faster but harder to balance and could tip and disqualify you. Only one of you wins the individual creativity medal based on your personal contributions, while you both win or lose the race together.
Scenario_rev: You and your teammate must build and race a raft together across a 100-meter lake faster than five other two-person teams to win the grand prize trophy, but the camp counselors also award a personal creativity medal to whichever person (you or your teammate) contributes the most creative design ideas to your shared raft. Conflict Rationale: Achievement values the stable rectangular raft design that ensures you win the team race trophy by completing the course reliably, while Stimulation values the risky, creative decorated raft that could win the individual creativity medal but might tip and lose the race. You compete with your friend for the personal medal while collaborating to win the race.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate with your friend to build one raft and race together to beat other teams, but you also compete against each other for the individual creativity medal awarded to only one person based on personal contributions. You share the race goal but compete for individual recognition.
