You can either run a quiet craft activity to comfort three homesick younger campers who are crying, or set up the music speakers for a dance party that 20 older campers are excitedly waiting for. If you don't decide in the next 5 minutes, another group will claim the pavilion and neither activity can happen.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes comforting the distressed younger children who need emotional support. Hedonism prioritizes the fun dance party that brings pleasure and excitement to more campers.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because choosing to help the homesick campers means sacrificing your counselor break time and energy to provide emotional care, rather than supervising an easier, self-running dance party. Both counselors must give up personal rest time regardless of the choice.

Scenario: You and your sibling are planning your grandmother's 80th birthday with a $300 family budget, and you must agree today on one venue to book. You can either host a tea party at her home where all 15 family members can attend and she'll feel comfortable in familiar surroundings, or rent a fun restaurant with karaoke and a photo booth that only fits 8 people but will create exciting entertainment. The invitations must be mailed by tomorrow to arrive in time.
Scenario_rev: You and your sibling are planning your grandmother's 80th birthday with a $300 family budget, and you must agree today on one venue to book. You can either host a tea party at her home where all 15 family members can attend and she'll feel comfortable in familiar surroundings, or rent a fun restaurant with karaoke and a photo booth that only fits 8 people but will create exciting entertainment. The invitations must be mailed by tomorrow to arrive in time.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes grandmother's comfort and ensuring no family members are excluded from this important milestone. Hedonism prioritizes creating an exciting, entertaining celebration with enjoyable activities even if fewer people can attend.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because both siblings must invest significant time and effort in planning, and whoever compromises their vision sacrifices their idea of the perfect celebration for their grandmother. The decision requires one sibling to set aside their preferred way of honoring their grandmother.

Scenario: You and your neighbor are organizing a free community safety workshop this Saturday in the park, and you must agree on one format for the 3-hour session. You can either teach basic first aid and emergency skills that could help neighbors save lives during a crisis, or organize a fun obstacle course with music and prizes that will attract three times more families but teach fewer safety skills. You both must volunteer your entire Saturday and bring supplies from home regardless of which option you choose.
Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor are organizing a free community safety workshop this Saturday in the park, and you must agree on one format for the 3-hour session. You can either teach basic first aid and emergency skills that could help neighbors save lives during a crisis, or organize a fun obstacle course with music and prizes that will attract three times more families but teach fewer safety skills. You both must volunteer your entire Saturday and bring supplies from home regardless of which option you choose.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes teaching practical skills that genuinely help neighbors stay safe and respond to emergencies. Hedonism prioritizes creating an enjoyable, entertaining event that maximizes fun and attracts more participants through exciting activities.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because both organizers must sacrifice their Saturday and personal resources (supplies, preparation time) to serve the community. Choosing the first aid option means sacrificing the immediate gratification of seeing large, enthusiastic crowds for the deeper purpose of community safety.

Scenario: You and your friend both need 10 community service hours by next month to graduate, and the only remaining opportunity is a 6 AM beach cleanup tomorrow that you must both attend to carpool there. Tonight, there's a beach bonfire party with live music until midnight that you both bought $40 tickets for. You must decide together tonight whether to go home early to wake up for the cleanup or stay for the whole party and lose the service hours.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend both need 10 community service hours by next month to graduate, and the only remaining opportunity is a 6 AM beach cleanup tomorrow that you must both attend to carpool there. Tonight, there's a beach bonfire party with live music until midnight that you both bought $40 tickets for. You must decide together tonight whether to go home early to wake up for the cleanup or stay for the whole party and lose the service hours.
Conflict Rationale: Staying for the party maximizes immediate pleasure and entertainment (Hedonism), while attending the cleanup contributes to environmental protection and community welfare (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both need the service hours to graduate and must carpool together since neither can drive alone. Both directly benefit from completing the cleanup requirement.

Scenario: You and a nutrition educator are running a weekend cooking workshop for 30 students, and you must submit one lunch catering order today. The caterer offers either gourmet comfort food with chocolate lava cake and truffle mac-and-cheese, or a simple plant-based menu using local organic vegetables that teaches sustainable eating. Your shared $400 budget only covers one option.
Scenario_rev: You are a nutrition educator running a weekend cooking workshop for 30 students with another instructor, and you must submit one lunch catering order today. The caterer offers either gourmet comfort food with chocolate lava cake and truffle mac-and-cheese, or a simple plant-based menu using local organic vegetables that teaches sustainable eating. Your shared $400 budget only covers one option.
Conflict Rationale: The indulgent menu provides maximum taste pleasure and enjoyment (Hedonism), while the plant-based menu promotes environmental sustainability and reduces harm to animals and ecosystems (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both are co-hosting and must submit a single catering order together. Both share responsibility for the workshop's success and student experience.

Scenario: You and a community organizer are co-hosting Saturday's event at your bar, and you must create one flyer together today to send to the printer. You need to decide whether to promote a high-energy dance party with $3 cocktail specials and a DJ, or a quiet community gathering offering free coffee and support groups for lonely neighbors. The bartender schedule and room setup are completely different for each option.
Scenario_rev: You are a community organizer co-hosting Saturday's event at a bar, and you must create one flyer together with the bar owner today to send to the printer. You need to decide whether to promote a high-energy dance party with $3 cocktail specials and a DJ, or a quiet community gathering offering free coffee and support groups for lonely neighbors. The bartender schedule and room setup are completely different for each option.
Conflict Rationale: The dance party maximizes fun and sensory pleasure for attendees (Hedonism), while the support gathering addresses social isolation and helps vulnerable community members (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both are co-hosts who must agree on one promotional message and submit a single flyer design today. Both share ownership of the event's direction and outcome.

Scenario: You and Sarah are writing four songs together for her debut album, and a record label will only listen if you submit the demo by Friday. You must decide today whether the songs will be about climate change and inequality, or about partying and having fun, because the topic determines which producer you'll hire tomorrow with your shared $2,000 studio budget. Both of your names will be on the album as co-writers.
Scenario_rev: You are Sarah, and you and your co-writer are creating four songs together for your debut album, and a record label will only listen if you submit the demo by Friday. You must decide today whether the songs will be about climate change and inequality, or about partying and having fun, because the topic determines which producer you'll hire tomorrow with your shared $2,000 studio budget. Both of your names will be on the album as co-writers.
Conflict Rationale: Party songs celebrate pleasure and carefree enjoyment (Hedonism), while social justice songs address environmental protection and helping disadvantaged groups (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both are co-writers who must agree on one lyrical direction to book the right producer. Both share credit and benefit from the label's potential interest.

Scenario: You and your friend are spending a week in a remote mountain cabin, and you must make one shared grocery list today for the whole trip since the nearest store is three hours away. You need to decide whether to buy only plant-based foods like vegetables and beans that reduce environmental harm, or to include steaks, bacon, and cheese that taste delicious. The small cabin cooler only fits enough food for one meal style.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are spending a week in a remote mountain cabin, and you must make one shared grocery list today for the whole trip since the nearest store is three hours away. You need to decide whether to buy only plant-based foods like vegetables and beans that reduce environmental harm, or to include steaks, bacon, and cheese that taste delicious. The small cabin cooler only fits enough food for one meal style.
Conflict Rationale: Meat and cheese provide rich flavors and eating pleasure (Hedonism), while plant-based foods reduce carbon emissions and animal suffering (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both are sharing the cabin and must create one shopping list together since they're making one shared trip. Both will eat the same meals all week from the shared cooler.

Scenario: You and your coworker must pick one way to commute together by Friday to get your company's $200 monthly carpool bonus, which requires both of you to use the same transportation. You need to decide whether to take the 90-minute bus and walking route that produces zero pollution, or drive together in your coworker's car for a comfortable 30-minute ride with air conditioning and music.
Scenario_rev: You and your coworker must pick one way to commute together by Friday to get your company's $200 monthly carpool bonus, which requires both of you to use the same transportation. You need to decide whether to take the 90-minute bus and walking route that produces zero pollution, or drive together in your car for a comfortable 30-minute ride with air conditioning and music.
Conflict Rationale: Driving provides comfort, convenience, and sensory pleasure with music and AC (Hedonism), while public transit reduces carbon emissions and environmental damage (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both must submit the same commute method to receive the carpool bonus. Both directly benefit from the $200 monthly payment.

Scenario: You and another board member control your charity's $100,000 yearly budget, and both of you must sign the allocation form by Friday. You must decide together whether to fund a three-year project planting 50,000 trees in damaged forests, or to host a fancy appreciation party with gourmet food, live music, and gift bags for your 200 donors. Only one option fits the budget.
Scenario_rev: You and another board member control your charity's $100,000 yearly budget, and both of you must sign the allocation form by Friday. You must decide together whether to fund a three-year project planting 50,000 trees in damaged forests, or to host a fancy appreciation party with gourmet food, live music, and gift bags for your 200 donors. Only one option fits the budget.
Conflict Rationale: The gala provides an elegant, enjoyable evening with fine food and entertainment (Hedonism), while the tree project restores ecosystems and protects the environment for all people (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both board members must sign off together on the allocation. Both share equal authority and responsibility for how the charity's money is spent.

Scenario: You and your friend each paid $300 for an all-day pool party at your resort tomorrow with food, drinks, and entertainment. Tonight, a scientist asks if you both want to join a 5 AM boat trip to photograph dying coral reefs for a conservation report, but you'll be too tired to enjoy the pool party afterward. You must both decide together because you share the only car and one of you cannot go alone.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend each paid $300 for an all-day pool party at your resort tomorrow with food, drinks, and entertainment. Tonight, a scientist asks if you both want to join a 5 AM boat trip to photograph dying coral reefs for a conservation report, but you'll be too tired to enjoy the pool party afterward. You must both decide together because you share the only car and one of you cannot go alone.
Conflict Rationale: The pool party offers a full day of relaxation, food, and fun (Hedonism), while the coral documentation helps protect marine ecosystems and ocean life (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both must decide together because they share the only transportation and neither can attend their chosen activity alone. Both have equal investment in the pre-paid pool party.

Scenario: You and your partner are hosting a dinner party for 12 friends on Saturday, and you must submit one grocery order together today using your shared $200 budget. You need to decide whether to make creative vegetable dishes that show sustainable eating practices, or to serve ribeye steaks and bacon appetizers that taste amazing and make the meal feel special.
Scenario_rev: You and your partner are hosting a dinner party for 12 friends on Saturday, and you must submit one grocery order together today using your shared $200 budget. You need to decide whether to make creative vegetable dishes that show sustainable eating practices, or to serve ribeye steaks and bacon appetizers that taste amazing and make the meal feel special.
Conflict Rationale: The meat menu provides rich flavors and indulgent dining pleasure (Hedonism), while the plant-based menu reduces environmental impact and animal suffering (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both are co-hosting and must submit a single shared grocery order today. Both are equally responsible for the meal and will share hosting duties.

Scenario: You and your teammate are the two captains who must pick the end-of-season celebration and tell the coach tomorrow so she can book it. The team budget allows either a trip to an amusement park with roller coasters and games that will be exciting and fun, or a neighborhood picnic where you'll invite five kids from the local after-school program who always watch your games but never get to participate in team events.
Scenario_rev: You and your teammate are the two captains who must pick the end-of-season celebration and tell the coach tomorrow so she can book it. The team budget allows either a trip to an amusement park with roller coasters and games that will be exciting and fun, or a neighborhood picnic where you'll invite five kids from the local after-school program who always watch your games but never get to participate in team events.
Conflict Rationale: The amusement park provides thrilling rides and entertainment (Hedonism), while the inclusive picnic helps children who are typically left out and promotes social equality (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both captains must agree and present one unified recommendation to the coach tomorrow. Both represent the team equally and share leadership responsibility.

Scenario: You and your podcast co-host must pick one documentary to feature in next week's mental health episode for your 50,000 listeners, and you must announce it tomorrow. You need to decide between an uplifting, entertaining film about personal triumph that audiences will enjoy watching, or a challenging documentary about mental healthcare inequality that could inspire advocacy work but is slower and less fun to watch.
Scenario_rev: You and your podcast co-host must pick one documentary to feature in next week's mental health episode for your 50,000 listeners, and you must announce it tomorrow. You need to decide between an uplifting, entertaining film about personal triumph that audiences will enjoy watching, or a challenging documentary about mental healthcare inequality that could inspire advocacy work but is slower and less fun to watch.
Conflict Rationale: The entertaining film provides enjoyment and positive feelings (Hedonism), while the inequality documentary raises awareness about systemic problems and helps disadvantaged populations (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both co-hosts must agree on one documentary recommendation for the joint episode. Both will share the episode's audience response and sponsorship revenue.

Scenario: You and your housemate inherited five acres together, and you must submit one land-use application to the county by next month to start any development. You need to decide whether to create a free public community garden and nature area for the low-income neighborhood next door, or to build private entertainment spaces including a pool, outdoor bar, and party pavilion that you both can enjoy and rent for income.
Scenario_rev: You and your housemate inherited five acres together, and you must submit one land-use application to the county by next month to start any development. You need to decide whether to create a free public community garden and nature area for the low-income neighborhood next door, or to build private entertainment spaces including a pool, outdoor bar, and party pavilion that you both can enjoy and rent for income.
Conflict Rationale: The entertainment facilities provide personal enjoyment, leisure, and fun experiences (Hedonism), while the community garden helps disadvantaged neighbors and protects local wildlife (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: Both inherited the property equally and must submit a single joint application together. Both are co-owners with equal decision-making authority.

Scenario: You and your sibling are planning a beach day with your kids, and you must agree on one location. You want the main beach with waterslides, ice cream stands, and party music because it's fun and exciting, while your sibling wants the protected cove where sea turtles nest so the kids don't disturb the animals or their habitat. You must pick one beach where everyone will spend the whole day together.
Scenario_rev: You and your sibling are planning a beach day with your kids, and you must agree on one location. You want the protected cove where sea turtles nest so the kids don't disturb the animals or their habitat, while your sibling wants the main beach with waterslides, ice cream stands, and party music because it's fun and exciting. You must pick one beach where everyone will spend the whole day together.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize personal enjoyment and sensory pleasure (Hedonism), while your sibling prioritizes protecting wildlife and the environment (Universalism). One of you must give up what matters most to reach agreement.
Cooperation Rationale: You're planning a joint family activity where everyone goes to the same location together. Both of you benefit equally from a successful, enjoyable beach day with the children.

Scenario: You and your friend are organizing a school movie night to raise money for new playground equipment, and you must pick one film. You want to show a funny adventure movie with silly jokes and exciting action because kids will have the most fun, while your friend wants to show a documentary about kids in different countries working together to solve problems because it teaches important lessons about helping others. You need to agree on the single movie that will sell the most tickets.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are organizing a school movie night to raise money for new playground equipment, and you must pick one film. You want to show a documentary about kids in different countries working together to solve problems because it teaches important lessons about helping others, while your friend wants to show a funny adventure movie with silly jokes and exciting action because kids will have the most fun. You need to agree on the single movie that will sell the most tickets.
Conflict Rationale: You want maximum entertainment and enjoyment for the audience (Hedonism), while your friend wants to use the event to educate children about global cooperation and social responsibility (Universalism). Only one film can be chosen.
Cooperation Rationale: You're jointly organizing a fundraising event where both of you share responsibility for its success. Both of you benefit equally if the event raises enough money for the playground equipment.

Scenario: You and your friend share one controller in a team tournament game where the highest-scoring team wins a prize, but you're also competing for a 'Best Player' badge based on individual points. You want to use all the power-ups right now for huge explosions and bonus points to boost your personal score, while your friend wants to save them so your whole team can survive the final boss battle and win together. You both must agree on when to use the shared power-ups.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend share one controller in a team tournament game where the highest-scoring team wins a prize, but you're also competing for a 'Best Player' badge based on individual points. You want to save the power-ups so your whole team can survive the final boss battle and win together, while your friend wants to use all the power-ups right now for huge explosions and bonus points to boost their personal score. You both must agree on when to use the shared power-ups.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize immediate excitement and personal glory (Hedonism), while your friend prioritizes the team's collective success and shared victory (Universalism). Using power-ups immediately helps you but might hurt the team.
Cooperation Rationale: You're working together as teammates to win the tournament prize, which you both share. However, you're also competing against each other for the individual 'Best Player' badge, which only one person can win.

Scenario: You and your friend are creating a neighborhood book club website together and must choose one design that will attract the most members this month. You want a colorful, game-like site with fun quizzes, silly badges, and entertaining videos because it's exciting, while your friend wants a simple design that includes books from many cultures and easy features for kids who have trouble reading. You're also competing for whose name appears first on the website and gets mentioned in the school newsletter.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are creating a neighborhood book club website together and must choose one design that will attract the most members this month. You want a simple design that includes books from many cultures and easy features for kids who have trouble reading, while your friend wants a colorful, game-like site with fun quizzes, silly badges, and entertaining videos because it's exciting. You're also competing for whose name appears first on the website and gets mentioned in the school newsletter.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize entertainment and immediate fun for users (Hedonism), while your friend prioritizes inclusivity, education, and helping underserved readers (Universalism). The design must emphasize one approach over the other.
Cooperation Rationale: You're building the website together to attract members, which benefits both of you equally. However, you're competing for individual recognition through name placement and newsletter mentions.

Scenario: You and your neighbor share a rooftop garden and must agree on one plan for using it in the evenings. You want to add string lights, speakers, and comfy chairs for having dinner parties and game nights with friends because it's fun, while your neighbor wants to keep it dark and quiet so the birds nesting nearby aren't disturbed and can sleep peacefully. Whoever's plan is chosen gets to use the rooftop three evenings per week, while the other person only gets one evening.
Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor share a rooftop garden and must agree on one plan for using it in the evenings. You want to keep it dark and quiet so the birds nesting nearby aren't disturbed and can sleep peacefully, while your neighbor wants to add string lights, speakers, and comfy chairs for having dinner parties and game nights with friends because it's fun. Whoever's plan is chosen gets to use the rooftop three evenings per week, while the other person only gets one evening.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize personal enjoyment and entertainment (Hedonism), while your neighbor prioritizes protecting wildlife and maintaining a peaceful environment (Universalism). The rooftop can only be set up one way.
Cooperation Rationale: You must submit one joint plan to the building owner about sharing the space. You're also competing for more evenings—whoever's setup is chosen gets more time slots to use the rooftop each week.

Scenario: You and John are creating an art show at the community center to impress a visiting judge who will pick one show to display at the big city festival, and you're competing for whose name gets listed first on all the posters. You want to display colorful, beautiful paintings and sculptures that are fun to look at and take pictures with, while John wants to show artwork about pollution, fairness, and helping people who need support. You must agree together on what type of art to display in your shared show.
Scenario_rev: You and John are creating an art show at the community center to impress a visiting judge who will pick one show to display at the big city festival, and you're competing for whose name gets listed first on all the posters. You want to show artwork about pollution, fairness, and helping people who need support, while John wants to display colorful, beautiful paintings and sculptures that are fun to look at and take pictures with. You must agree together on what type of art to display in your shared show.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize visual beauty, sensory pleasure, and enjoyable experiences (Hedonism), while John prioritizes raising awareness about social and environmental problems (Universalism). The show can only have one main theme.
Cooperation Rationale: You're creating one joint art show together that must impress the judge for both of you to reach the festival. You're also competing for whose name appears first on promotional materials, giving individual recognition.

Scenario: You and John are hosting a video game party to raise money for the youth center, and you're competing for whose personal score board gets displayed at the entrance. You want to play exciting battle and racing games during the busy evening hours because they're fun and keep people watching, while John wants to play teamwork puzzle games that teach lessons about cooperation and helping your community. Together you must create one game schedule that raises the most money and keeps visitors entertained.
Scenario_rev: You and John are hosting a video game party to raise money for the youth center, and you're competing for whose personal score board gets displayed at the entrance. You want to play teamwork puzzle games that teach lessons about cooperation and helping your community, while John wants to play exciting battle and racing games during the busy evening hours because they're fun and keep people watching. Together you must create one game schedule that raises the most money and keeps visitors entertained.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize entertainment and exciting gameplay (Hedonism), while John prioritizes educational content and messages about community cooperation (Universalism). The schedule can only feature certain games during the most important time slots.
Cooperation Rationale: You're jointly hosting a fundraising event where both of you share the goal of raising money. You're competing for individual recognition through whose score board gets the prominent display spot at the entrance.

Scenario: You and your friend are on a day hike and find a trail section covered in broken glass near the family picnic area, and you have two hours before sunset. You want to continue hiking to the beautiful waterfall for photos and relaxation because it's the fun part of the trip, while your friend wants to spend the time cleaning up the dangerous glass so other families and kids don't get hurt. You're also competing for who gets to use the one remaining energy bar and choose the best sleeping spot at camp tonight—whoever's activity choice wins gets first pick of both.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are on a day hike and find a trail section covered in broken glass near the family picnic area, and you have two hours before sunset. You want to spend the time cleaning up the dangerous glass so other families and kids don't get hurt, while your friend wants to continue hiking to the beautiful waterfall for photos and relaxation because it's the fun part of the trip. You're also competing for who gets to use the one remaining energy bar and choose the best sleeping spot at camp tonight—whoever's activity choice wins gets first pick of both.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize personal enjoyment and the pleasurable goal of reaching the waterfall (Hedonism), while your friend prioritizes helping others and preventing harm to future hikers (Universalism). You only have time for one activity.
Cooperation Rationale: You must make one joint decision about how to spend your shared hiking time before sunset. You're competing for limited personal resources—the energy bar and best camping spot—which go to whoever's preferred activity is chosen.

Scenario: You and your cousin are making a pet-care guide for kids in your neighborhood and must choose one approach that will get the most families to use it. You want to include fun games, silly animal sounds, and funny videos that make learning about pets entertaining, while your cousin wants to focus on teaching kids how to understand animal feelings and take gentle, responsible care of pets. You're competing for whose name gets printed in bigger letters on the cover—whoever's approach is chosen gets the larger name.
Scenario_rev: You and your cousin are making a pet-care guide for kids in your neighborhood and must choose one approach that will get the most families to use it. You want to focus on teaching kids how to understand animal feelings and take gentle, responsible care of pets, while your cousin wants to include fun games, silly animal sounds, and funny videos that make learning about pets entertaining. You're competing for whose name gets printed in bigger letters on the cover—whoever's approach is chosen gets the larger name.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize making the guide fun and entertaining (Hedonism), while your cousin prioritizes teaching empathy, responsibility, and proper animal welfare (Universalism). The guide can only follow one main approach.
Cooperation Rationale: You're creating one pet-care guide together that needs to attract families for both of you to succeed. You're competing for individual recognition through whose name appears more prominently on the cover.

Scenario: You're staying with your friend for one month while moving, and together you must agree on one set of house rules that both of you will follow. You want to keep things comfortable and easy with air conditioning, ordering takeout food, and using paper plates, while your friend wants to save energy by using fans, cook meals to avoid waste, and wash dishes to help the environment. Whoever's lifestyle gets chosen also gets to pick the living room furniture arrangement and control the temperature.
Scenario_rev: You're staying with your friend for one month while moving, and together you must agree on one set of house rules that both of you will follow. You want to save energy by using fans, cook meals to avoid waste, and wash dishes to help the environment, while your friend wants to keep things comfortable and easy with air conditioning, ordering takeout food, and using paper plates. Whoever's lifestyle gets chosen also gets to pick the living room furniture arrangement and control the temperature.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize personal comfort and convenience (Hedonism), while your friend prioritizes reducing environmental harm and resource conservation (Universalism). You can only follow one set of household rules.
Cooperation Rationale: You're living together temporarily and must establish one shared household policy that both of you follow. You're competing for control over the living space setup and temperature, which goes to whoever's rules are adopted.

Scenario: You and your cousin share a family tablet for one hour each day, and you're competing for a weekly reward—whoever completes more learning activities gets to pick the weekend family movie. You want to spend your whole hour watching funny videos and playing exciting racing games, while your cousin wants you both to agree to spend half the time on educational apps about protecting nature and helping animals so everyone in the family, including younger siblings, learns something important. You need to agree on how to split the shared tablet time.
Scenario_rev: You and your cousin share a family tablet for one hour each day, and you're competing for a weekly reward—whoever completes more learning activities gets to pick the weekend family movie. You want you both to agree to spend half the time on educational apps about protecting nature and helping animals so everyone in the family, including younger siblings, learns something important, while your cousin wants to spend their whole hour watching funny videos and playing exciting racing games. You need to agree on how to split the shared tablet time.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize entertainment and immediate fun during your tablet time (Hedonism), while your cousin wants to use it for educational content that benefits everyone and teaches about environmental protection (Universalism). You have limited time to share.
Cooperation Rationale: You must agree on how to divide the one hour of shared tablet time between you. You're also competing for a personal reward—completing more educational activities wins you the right to choose the weekend movie everyone watches.

Scenario: You and your colleague are creating new travel rules for your ten-person team and must submit one plan, with whoever's approach wins getting first choice of the four comfortable airplane seats available on long trips this year. You want to allow frequent shorter trips with comfortable flights, nice hotels, and flexible bookings for maximum convenience and enjoyment, while your colleague wants fewer trips using regular seats, environment-friendly hotels, and trains instead of planes to reduce pollution. You must agree on a single travel policy that everyone on the team will follow.
Scenario_rev: You and your colleague are creating new travel rules for your ten-person team and must submit one plan, with whoever's approach wins getting first choice of the four comfortable airplane seats available on long trips this year. You want fewer trips using regular seats, environment-friendly hotels, and trains instead of planes to reduce pollution, while your colleague wants to allow frequent shorter trips with comfortable flights, nice hotels, and flexible bookings for maximum convenience and enjoyment. You must agree on a single travel policy that everyone on the team will follow.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize personal comfort, convenience, and enjoyable travel experiences (Hedonism), while your colleague prioritizes environmental protection and reducing carbon emissions (Universalism). Only one travel policy can be implemented.
Cooperation Rationale: You're jointly creating one department travel policy that must work for the whole team. You're competing for limited personal benefit—priority access to the four comfortable seat upgrades on long-distance trips.

Scenario: You and your travel companion must book one hotel for your shared week-long beach vacation by midnight tonight to get the group discount. The choice is between a luxury resort with a water park, premium spa, and gourmet restaurants, or an eco-resort that uses solar power, serves local organic food, and employs community guides. You're competing because the hotel booking website awards bonus points only to the person who clicks the final 'confirm booking' button, and whoever gets more points this year wins a free weekend trip.
Scenario_rev: You and your organizer companion must book one hotel for your shared week-long beach vacation by midnight tonight to get the group discount. The choice is between a luxury resort with a water park, premium spa, and gourmet restaurants, or an eco-resort that uses solar power, serves local organic food, and employs community guides. You're competing because the hotel booking website awards bonus points only to the person who clicks the final 'confirm booking' button, and whoever gets more points this year wins a free weekend trip.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism prioritizes the luxury resort for maximum personal pleasure and comfort through spa treatments and exciting water park activities. Universalism prioritizes the eco-resort to support environmental sustainability and local community welfare.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both travelers must agree on a single hotel choice to receive the group discount (collaboration), while simultaneously competing for who gets to click the booking button to earn the bonus reward points. The competition for bonus points exists as a secondary incentive within the primary collaborative task of booking shared accommodation.

Scenario: You and your co-producer must choose one filming location for your music video and submit the permit application by Friday to secure next month's shooting date. The options are a pristine nature reserve with strict environmental protection rules and limited access, or a colorful amusement park with thrilling roller coasters and carnival attractions. You're competing because the production company will give a $2,000 bonus payment only to the producer whose location choice gets approved and used in the final video.
Scenario_rev: You and your fellow producer must choose one filming location for your music video and submit the permit application by Friday to secure next month's shooting date. The options are a pristine nature reserve with strict environmental protection rules and limited access, or a colorful amusement park with thrilling roller coasters and carnival attractions. You're competing because the production company will give a $2,000 bonus payment only to the producer whose location choice gets approved and used in the final video.
Conflict Rationale: Hedonism prioritizes the amusement park location for its exciting, vibrant, and entertaining visual appeal that creates a fun filming experience. Universalism prioritizes the nature reserve to showcase environmental beauty and support conservation efforts, despite the restrictive filming rules.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both producers must agree on a single location and submit one permit application together to create the music video (collaboration), while competing for the individual $2,000 bonus payment that only goes to whoever's location choice is selected. The bonus creates direct competition within the shared creative project.

Scenario: You and another member must pick one book for your library's two-person book club and tell the librarian by tomorrow so she can order copies for next week's meeting. Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both members must agree on one book together so the librarian can order copies for their shared book club (collaboration), while competing for the $50 gift card that only the person whose choice wins will receive. The reward creates competition within the collaborative book selection process.

Scenario: You and the school nurse must decide whether to spend your 30-minute lunch break relaxing in the teacher's lounge or setting up a health station in the cafeteria to give masks and hand sanitizer to students during flu season. Scenario: You and another parent must create the community pool schedule for all 20 families today, choosing between fun party hours with loud music, competitive diving games, and energetic activities, or quiet inclusive hours with calm swimming and gentle activities for all ages and abilities. Your assistant created a rotation system where kids move between six stations every 15 minutes, but you want to personally demonstrate each skill to all 40 kids together before practice time. Conflict Rationale: You want to achieve maximum skill development through efficient small-group rotations, while the coach wants to demonstrate leadership authority by personally teaching all participants in large group demonstrations.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents benefit from a successful camp—you achieve effective skill teaching and the coach runs a well-organized program. Both must agree on the teaching structure.

Scenario: You and the toy shop owner must organize Saturday's sale to earn $2,000 in revenue, and you both get $100 bonuses if successful. You analyzed sales data and created discount bundles on the 20 best-selling toys, but the owner wants to personally choose which toys to discount based on their judgment and experience. You must agree on one sale plan by Thursday to order promotional signs and schedule the five staff members.
Scenario_rev: You and your sales analyst must organize Saturday's sale to earn $2,000 in revenue, and you both get $100 bonuses if successful. Your analyst analyzed sales data and created discount bundles on the 20 best-selling toys, but you want to personally choose which toys to discount based on your judgment and experience. You must agree on one sale plan by Thursday to order promotional signs and schedule the five staff members.
Conflict Rationale: You want to achieve the revenue goal through data-optimized discount strategy, while the owner wants to maintain authority by making pricing decisions based on their personal business judgment.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents directly benefit from reaching $2,000—you both receive $100 bonuses. Success requires agreeing on a single discount strategy.

Scenario: You and the science club president must build a volcano demonstration for the city science fair that scores at least 90 points to win the $300 equipment grant. You designed detailed measurements and data charts that show the chemical reaction rates, but the president wants to create a large colorful display that they can personally explain to judges. You must agree on one project plan by Monday to have two weeks to build and practice the presentation.
Scenario_rev: You and your project coordinator must build a volcano demonstration for the city science fair that scores at least 90 points to win the $300 equipment grant. Your coordinator designed detailed measurements and data charts that show the chemical reaction rates, but you want to create a large colorful display that you can personally explain to judges. You must agree on one project plan by Monday to have two weeks to build and practice the presentation.
Conflict Rationale: You want to achieve the highest score through rigorous scientific documentation, while the president wants to demonstrate leadership authority by personally presenting an impressive visual display they control.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents benefit from scoring 90+ points—you achieve competitive success and the president leads the club to win the $300 grant. Both must agree on the project approach.

Scenario: You and the head lifeguard must finish the emergency response plan for tomorrow's safety inspection at the community pool. You want guards assigned to rescue zones based on their rescue speed and CPR test scores, but the head lifeguard wants assignments based on who has worked at the pool longest. You must submit one plan that the safety board approves.
Scenario_rev: You are the head lifeguard and must finish the emergency response plan for tomorrow's safety inspection at the community pool with your safety-focused colleague. Your colleague wants guards assigned to rescue zones based on their rescue speed and CPR test scores, but you want assignments based on who has worked at the pool longest. You must submit one plan that the safety board approves.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values assigning roles based on demonstrated competence and performance scores, while Power values assignment based on seniority and formal hierarchy.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents directly benefit from passing the safety inspection—the pool can open and both keep their positions. The shared goal requires submitting one unified plan.

Scenario: You and the gallery owner must create one art show together and sign the contract in three days. You want the posters to say 'Featuring [Your Name]'s Award-Winning Paintings' to show your artistic skills, but the owner wants them to say 'Presented by [Gallery Name]' to show the gallery's reputation. You're competing for whose name appears bigger on all the posters, while working together to make the show successful.
Scenario_rev: You are the gallery owner and must create one art show together with your artist and sign the contract in three days. The artist wants the posters to say 'Featuring [Artist's Name]'s Award-Winning Paintings' to show their artistic skills, but you want them to say 'Presented by [Gallery Name]' to show the gallery's reputation. You're competing for whose name appears bigger on all the posters, while working together to make the show successful.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values being recognized for personal artistic accomplishments and creative excellence, while Power values the gallery's established authority and control over presentation.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both need the art show to succeed (collaboration), but they compete for whose name gets more prominent placement on promotional materials, affecting their individual recognition.

Scenario: You and the editor-in-chief must finish the school newspaper before it prints tomorrow morning. You want your investigative article about the cafeteria's food waste on the front page because you spent three weeks researching and it could win awards, but the editor wants his interview with the principal on front because he's in charge of the newspaper. You're competing for the front-page spot and the recognition it brings, while working together to meet the printing deadline.
Scenario_rev: You are the editor-in-chief and must finish the school newspaper with your investigative reporter before it prints tomorrow morning. The reporter wants their investigative article about the cafeteria's food waste on the front page because they spent three weeks researching and it could win awards, but you want your interview with the principal on front because you're in charge of the newspaper. You're competing for the front-page spot and the recognition it brings, while working together to meet the printing deadline.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values front-page placement based on effort, research quality, and potential awards, while Power values it based on editorial authority and positional control.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both must finish the newspaper by deadline (collaboration), but compete for front-page placement which determines individual recognition and prestige.

Scenario: You and the theater director must choose one student for the lead role before tomorrow's sponsor meeting. You want to pick the student who scored highest in the singing and acting auditions, but the director wants to pick the student who has attended his theater classes for three years. You're competing to prove whose selection method is better, while working together to choose one lead who will make the show great enough that sponsors continue funding.
Scenario_rev: You are the theater director and must choose one student for the lead role with your casting partner before tomorrow's sponsor meeting. Your casting partner wants to pick the student who scored highest in the singing and acting auditions, but you want to pick the student who has attended your theater classes for three years. You're competing to prove whose selection method is better, while working together to choose one lead who will make the show great enough that sponsors continue funding.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values selecting based on demonstrated audition performance and skill, while Power values selecting based on loyalty to the director's program and established hierarchy.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both need to select one successful lead for sponsor approval (collaboration), but compete over which selection criteria validates their approach and influence.

Scenario: You and the charity coordinator must create one presentation for donors tomorrow about the food drive results. You want to show charts with numbers—how many meals were packed, how many families were fed, and how fast volunteers worked—but the coordinator wants to show pictures of herself leading the team with her name prominently displayed. You're competing for donor recognition (for results versus for leadership), while working together to get continued funding.
Scenario_rev: You are the charity coordinator and must create one presentation with your data analyst for donors tomorrow about the food drive results. The analyst wants to show charts with numbers—how many meals were packed, how many families were fed, and how fast volunteers worked—but you want to show pictures of yourself leading the team with your name prominently displayed. You're competing for donor recognition (for leadership versus for results), while working together to get continued funding.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values recognition for measurable results, efficiency, and concrete accomplishments, while Power values recognition for leadership position, authority, and organizational control.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both need donor funding approved (collaboration), but compete for how success is attributed—to performance metrics or leadership visibility.

Scenario: You and the coding club manager must submit one app to the competition by Friday with only one name listed as 'Lead Developer.' You want to list the student who wrote the hardest code and fixed the most bugs, but the manager wants to list himself because he runs the club and recruited everyone. You're competing for the lead developer credit on the entry form, while working together to submit a winning app that earns scholarship money and new computers for the club.
Scenario_rev: You are the coding club manager and must submit one app to the competition by Friday with your technical advisor with only one name listed as 'Lead Developer.' The advisor wants to list the student who wrote the hardest code and fixed the most bugs, but you want to list yourself because you run the club and recruited everyone. You're competing for the lead developer credit on the entry form, while working together to submit a winning app that earns scholarship money and new computers for the club.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values recognition based on technical contribution, problem-solving, and coding skill, while Power values recognition based on managerial role, organizational authority, and club leadership.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both need the app to win prizes (collaboration), but compete for the single primary developer credit that determines individual recognition.

Scenario: You and the class president must pick one presenter for tomorrow's science fair judging. You want the student who did all the experiments and analyzed the data to present because that work earned the best results, but the class president wants to present because she organized the team. You're competing for the presenter spot and the recognition judges give, while working together to win a prize that gets your class into regionals.
Scenario_rev: You are the class president and must pick one presenter for tomorrow's science fair judging with your project coordinator. You're competing for the presenter spot and the recognition judges give, while working together to win a prize that gets your class into regionals.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values presenting based on scientific contribution and quality of work performed, while Power values presenting based on team leadership role and organizational authority.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both need to win a prize for regional qualification (collaboration), but compete for the single presenter role that determines individual recognition from judges.

Scenario: You and the eco-club leader must decide how to spend the $500 prize money before tomorrow's assembly. You want to build a composting station where students learn to measure waste reduction and track results, but the leader wants to create a donor wall with her name and photo displayed prominently. You're competing for how success is defined—through teaching results or through leadership recognition—while working together to spend the money in a way that keeps sponsors happy.
Scenario_rev: You are the eco-club leader and must decide how to spend the $500 prize money with your education-focused member before tomorrow's assembly. You're competing for how success is defined—through leadership recognition or through teaching results—while working together to spend the money in a way that keeps sponsors happy.

Scenario: You and the student council president must prepare one speech for tomorrow's assembly about a new tutoring program. You want the student who researched and designed the program to speak because she knows all the details, but the president wants to speak because he represents student government. You're competing for speaking time and recognition from the principal, while working together to give a convincing speech that gets the program approved and funded.
Scenario_rev: You are the student council president and must prepare one speech for tomorrow's assembly about a new tutoring program with your program developer. You're competing for speaking time and recognition from the principal, while working together to give a convincing speech that gets the program approved and funded.

Scenario: You and the computer lab coordinator must create one rule for who gets the advanced laptop during project week, announcing it to students tomorrow. You want the laptop to go to whichever student's project needs the special software most, but the coordinator wants it to rotate among class officers to show his authority. You're competing for which rule gets used—project needs or student positions—while working together to create a fair system that prevents arguments and keeps all projects running smoothly.
Scenario_rev: You are the computer lab coordinator and must create one rule for who gets the advanced laptop during project week with your project advisor, announcing it to students tomorrow. The advisor wants the laptop to go to whichever student's project needs the special software most, but you want it to rotate among class officers to show your authority. You're competing for which rule gets used—student positions or project needs—while working together to create a fair system that prevents arguments and keeps all projects running smoothly.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values resource allocation based on project requirements and optimal performance outcomes, while Power values allocation based on hierarchical positions and coordinator authority.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both need a working allocation system that prevents conflicts (collaboration), but compete over which principle governs access—merit-based needs or positional priority.

Scenario: You and the lead moderator must create one system for the community website by Friday that picks which posts appear on the homepage. You want posts ranked by how many people click, read, and share them, but the moderator wants to personally choose posts to display. You're competing for three homepage spotlight slots that will either feature your high-performing posts or the moderator's handpicked ones, while working together to boost site visits for the public launch.
Scenario_rev: You are the lead moderator and must create one system for the community website by Friday with your analytics specialist that picks which posts appear on the homepage. You're competing for three homepage spotlight slots that will either feature your handpicked posts or the specialist's high-performing ones, while working together to boost site visits for the public launch.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values content selection based on measurable user engagement and performance data, while Power values selection based on moderator judgment and editorial control.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both need increased site activity for launch success (collaboration), but compete for homepage slots that validate either data-driven or authority-based selection.

Scenario: You and the team captain must finalize the roster for Saturday's regional tournament, picking twelve players from eighteen who want to play. You want to choose based on tryout scores and who played best during practice, but the captain wants to choose players who follow his instructions and respect his leadership. You're competing for influence over team composition—best performers versus most obedient—while working together to field one strong team that advances in the tournament.
Scenario_rev: You are the team captain and must finalize the roster for Saturday's regional tournament with your performance analyst, picking twelve players from eighteen who want to play. The analyst wants to choose based on tryout scores and who played best during practice, but you want to choose players who follow your instructions and respect your leadership. You're competing for influence over team composition—most obedient versus best performers—while working together to field one strong team that advances in the tournament.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement values team selection based on demonstrated athletic skill and performance scores, while Power values selection based on deference to captain authority and hierarchical loyalty.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both need one competitive team to advance in tournament (collaboration), but compete over selection criteria that reflects their different values and influence.

Scenario: You and the department head must build one inventory system connecting five warehouses to cut shipping delays by 30% in six months. You compete for the Project Lead title that determines who gets promoted to Operations Director. Together you must pick the software, create training materials, and decide who presents results to executives.
Scenario_rev: You and your operations colleague must build one inventory system connecting five warehouses to cut shipping delays by 30% in six months. You compete for the Project Lead title that determines who gets promoted to Operations Director. Together you must pick the software, create training materials, and decide who presents results to executives.
Conflict Rationale: You want the Project Lead recognition to demonstrate your competence and advance your career (Achievement). The department head wants the title to maintain authority and control over operations decisions (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate to build the unified system while simultaneously competing for the Project Lead title and promotion credit. Both the collaborative goal (system success) and competitive element (leadership recognition) are clearly defined.

Scenario: You and the student council president have $800 to improve the playground and must pick one project together. You compete for whose idea gets selected: your basketball court with skill-tracking scoreboards, or the president's leadership stage with name plaques for council members. The principal announces the winning idea at the school assembly.
Scenario_rev: You and your fellow student must have $800 to improve the playground and must pick one project together. You compete for whose idea gets selected: your leadership stage with name plaques for council members, or your fellow student's basketball court with skill-tracking scoreboards. The principal announces the winning idea at the school assembly.
Conflict Rationale: You want the basketball court with scoreboards so students can measure and improve their skills, demonstrating achievement. The president wants the leadership stage with plaques to display authority and recognition of council members (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate to choose and implement one playground improvement within budget while competing for whose specific idea gets selected and publicly credited. The collaborative goal (playground improvement) and competition (idea selection credit) are both explicit.

Scenario: You and the factory manager must set new machine speed limits that prevent worker injuries, even though toy production drops from 1,000 to 800 daily, ruining your efficiency record. The manager must let a worker safety committee approve all speed settings, giving up his control. Together you create new safety rules and training programs that protect workers instead of breaking production records.
Scenario_rev: You and your safety coordinator must set new machine speed limits that prevent worker injuries, even though toy production drops from 1,000 to 800 daily, ruining the coordinator's efficiency record. You must let a worker safety committee approve all speed settings, giving up your control. Together you create new safety rules and training programs that protect workers instead of breaking production records.
Conflict Rationale: You sacrifice your efficiency achievement record by reducing production targets. The manager sacrifices his power by allowing workers to control speed decisions instead of making all choices himself.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both agents sacrifice personal goals (your achievement record and his decision-making authority) to benefit worker safety and wellbeing. The collaboration requires accepting personal costs for the greater good of preventing injuries.

Scenario: You and the transit director must create storm-day bus routes that add 15 minutes to every trip, destroying your 95% on-time record that took two years to achieve. The director must let drivers and a citizen board override his route choices during bad weather, reducing his control. Together you design new safety-focused schedules that measure safe arrivals instead of speed records.
Scenario_rev: You and your scheduling coordinator must create storm-day bus routes that add 15 minutes to every trip, destroying the coordinator's 95% on-time record that took two years to achieve. You must let drivers and a citizen board override your route choices during bad weather, reducing your control. Together you design new safety-focused schedules that measure safe arrivals instead of speed records.
Conflict Rationale: You sacrifice your on-time achievement record by accepting slower routes. The director sacrifices his power by allowing drivers and citizens to override his route decisions during storms.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both agents accept personal losses (your performance record and his authority) to prioritize passenger safety during dangerous weather. The collaboration serves the greater good despite harming individual goals.

Scenario: You and the hospital chief must replace the surgery speed bonus system (which rewards doctors for 20+ surgeries weekly) with quality-focused reviews, eliminating your department's top performance ranking. The chief must let nurses and patient representatives help evaluate doctors, reducing his sole authority. Together you create new standards measuring patient recovery and care quality instead of surgery numbers.
Scenario_rev: You and your department head must replace the surgery speed bonus system (which rewards doctors for 20+ surgeries weekly) with quality-focused reviews, eliminating the department head's top performance ranking. You must let nurses and patient representatives help evaluate doctors, reducing your sole authority. Together you create new standards measuring patient recovery and care quality instead of surgery numbers.
Conflict Rationale: You sacrifice your top performance ranking by abandoning the speed-based system that showcased your achievement. The chief sacrifices his power by sharing evaluation authority with nurses and patient advocates.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both agents accept personal costs (your ranking status and his exclusive authority) to benefit patient safety and care quality. The collaboration prioritizes patient outcomes over individual achievements and control.

Scenario: You and the emergency commander must share your award-winning flood evacuation plan (which took 400 hours to create) as a free guide for five neighboring towns, losing your advantage for the State Emergency Planning Prize. The commander must let other towns' officials modify the plan and make decisions during regional emergencies, reducing his command authority. Together you create training materials helping surrounding communities prepare for floods.
Scenario_rev: You and your planning coordinator must share the planning coordinator's award-winning flood evacuation plan (which took 400 hours to create) as a free guide for five neighboring towns, losing the advantage for the State Emergency Planning Prize. You must let other towns' officials modify the plan and make decisions during regional emergencies, reducing your command authority. Together you create training materials helping surrounding communities prepare for floods.
Conflict Rationale: You sacrifice your competitive achievement advantage by giving away the evacuation system you worked hard to develop. The commander sacrifices his power by allowing other officials to modify plans and share decision-making authority.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both agents sacrifice personal goals (your prize opportunity and his command control) to help neighboring communities prepare for emergencies. The collaboration benefits the greater regional safety despite personal costs.

Scenario: You and the team captain must give every player at least 20 minutes of game time during the championship, even though it means losing your chance to win your first-ever tournament trophy. The captain must let a parent-player committee decide the rotation schedule, giving up his control over substitutions. Together you design practice drills that prepare all players, choosing team inclusion over winning records.
Scenario_rev: You and your assistant coach must give every player at least 20 minutes of game time during the championship, even though it means losing the assistant coach's chance to win a first-ever tournament trophy. You must let a parent-player committee decide the rotation schedule, giving up your control over substitutions. Together you design practice drills that prepare all players, choosing team inclusion over winning records.
Conflict Rationale: You sacrifice your achievement goal of winning the tournament trophy by playing less-skilled players. The captain sacrifices his power by letting a committee control substitution decisions instead of making lineup choices himself.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both agents accept personal costs (your winning opportunity and his lineup authority) to ensure fair inclusion for all team members. The collaboration prioritizes team values and fairness over individual success.

Scenario: You and the recycling club president must spend 10 hours monthly teaching three other schools your recycling system, which means your club completes only two projects instead of five this semester, eliminating your chance to win the District Environmental Achievement Award. The president must let other schools' leaders join planning meetings and vote on decisions, reducing his exclusive control. Together you create teaching materials helping other schools reduce waste.
Scenario_rev: You and your club coordinator must spend 10 hours monthly teaching three other schools the club's recycling system, which means the club completes only two projects instead of five this semester, eliminating the coordinator's chance to win the District Environmental Achievement Award. You must let other schools' leaders join planning meetings and vote on decisions, reducing your exclusive control. Together you create teaching materials helping other schools reduce waste.
Conflict Rationale: You sacrifice your achievement goal of winning the District Award by reducing your project count. The president sacrifices his power by sharing decision-making authority with leaders from other schools.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both agents accept personal losses (your award opportunity and his exclusive control) to help other schools improve their environmental practices. The collaboration benefits the broader community despite personal costs.

Scenario: You and your department manager must write one grant proposal together to secure $150,000 in funding for your research team. You compete for the single Senior Researcher promotion, which the committee awards based on leadership shown in the proposal. Together you decide whose research methods to feature, how to list contributions, and whose achievements to highlight, knowing these choices affect promotion scoring.
Scenario_rev: You and your fellow researcher must write one grant proposal together to secure $150,000 in funding for your research team. You compete for the single Senior Researcher promotion, which the committee awards based on leadership shown in the proposal. Together you decide whose research methods to feature, how to list contributions, and whose achievements to highlight, knowing these choices affect promotion scoring.
Conflict Rationale: You want to highlight your contributions and methods to demonstrate competence for the promotion (Achievement). The manager wants to showcase his leadership and control over the research direction (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate to write a successful grant proposal while simultaneously competing for the single promotion opportunity. The collaborative goal (securing funding) and competitive element (promotion based on perceived leadership) are both explicit.

Scenario: You and the water board director must implement strict water rationing (50 gallons per person daily) during the drought, which delays four housing developments and eliminates your chance to achieve Fastest Growing Town recognition you've worked toward for three years. The director must let citizen representatives attend board meetings and vote on water decisions, reducing his unilateral authority. Together you design fair distribution rules ensuring every household has sufficient water.
Scenario_rev: You and your city planning coordinator must implement strict water rationing (50 gallons per person daily) during the drought, which delays four housing developments and eliminates the coordinator's chance to achieve Fastest Growing Town recognition worked toward for three years. You must let citizen representatives attend board meetings and vote on water decisions, reducing your unilateral authority. Together you design fair distribution rules ensuring every household has sufficient water.
Conflict Rationale: You sacrifice your achievement goal of Fastest Growing Town recognition by accepting development delays. The director sacrifices his power by sharing decision-making authority with citizen representatives.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both agents accept personal costs (your growth achievement and his exclusive authority) to ensure fair water distribution for all residents during drought. The collaboration prioritizes community welfare over individual goals.

Scenario: You and the game director must remove the premium Power Gem upgrades that generate $40,000 monthly, eliminating your revenue achievement bonus. The director must let a 15-member player council (including parents) vote on future game rules and item pricing, reducing his sole design authority. Together you create new achievement systems rewarding cooperation and creativity instead of purchased advantages, making gameplay fairer for non-paying players.
Scenario_rev: You and your revenue manager must remove the premium Power Gem upgrades that generate $40,000 monthly, eliminating the manager's revenue achievement bonus.
