Together you create new achievement systems rewarding cooperation and creativity instead of purchased advantages, making gameplay fairer for non-paying players.
Conflict Rationale: You sacrifice your revenue achievement bonus by removing the profitable premium items. The director sacrifices his power by allowing a player council to vote on design decisions instead of controlling all game rules himself.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both agents accept personal losses (your financial bonus and his design authority) to benefit players who cannot afford purchases. The collaboration prioritizes fair gameplay and community welfare over individual gains.

Scenario: You and another fitness competitor must design one obstacle course challenge together for the community fair's charity fundraiser. You compete for the Fair's Strongest Athlete title, which judges award based on who completes the course fastest with best form. Together you choose obstacle types, difficulty levels, and safety rules, knowing your design affects both the charity's success and your competition performance.
Scenario_rev: You and your fellow fitness competitor must design one obstacle course challenge together for the community fair's charity fundraiser. You compete for the Fair's Strongest Athlete title, which judges award based on who completes the course fastest with best form. Together you choose obstacle types, difficulty levels, and safety rules, knowing your design affects both the charity's success and your competition performance.
Conflict Rationale: You want to design obstacles that showcase your specific strengths to win the title and demonstrate superior fitness (Achievement). The other competitor wants to design obstacles that display their dominance and control over the event (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because you must collaborate to create a successful charity obstacle course while simultaneously competing for the Strongest Athlete title. The collaborative goal (successful fundraiser) and competitive element (performance-based title) are both clearly defined.

Scenario: You and the event coordinator must organize the school science fair with 15 participating classes. You want to create achievement certificates for the top 3 projects based on scientific merit so students can showcase their accomplishments, but the coordinator insists on giving every student identical participation ribbons and wants her name listed as 'Director' on all materials and announcements. If you don't agree on the format by Friday, the fair gets cancelled and students lose their chance to present their projects.
Scenario_rev: You and your organizing colleague must organize the school science fair with 15 participating classes. The colleague wants to create achievement certificates for the top 3 projects based on scientific merit so students can showcase their accomplishments, but you insist on giving every student identical participation ribbons and want your name listed as 'Director' on all materials and announcements. If you don't agree on the format by Friday, the fair gets cancelled and students lose their chance to present their projects.
Conflict Rationale: You value recognizing demonstrated competence and excellence through merit-based awards (Achievement), while the coordinator prioritizes her authoritative title and control over recognition (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: Both of you sacrifice preferred elements—you give up some merit recognition and the coordinator shares decision-making authority—to ensure students benefit from having the fair at all. This is Altrusive because both agents must compromise for the students' benefit rather than personal gain.

Scenario: You and the team captain must decide whether to share your winning robot design with 8 other schools who are struggling, three weeks before the regional championship. Sharing the design would help more schools compete successfully but reduces your team's advantage and chance of winning first place. The captain agrees to share only if all materials prominently display 'Developed under [Captain's Name] Leadership' and other teams must ask his permission before modifying anything.
Scenario_rev: You and your strategic teammate must decide whether to share your winning robot design with 8 other schools who are struggling, three weeks before the regional championship. Sharing the design would help more schools compete successfully but reduces your team's advantage and chance of winning first place. You agree to share only if all materials prominently display 'Developed under [Your Name] Leadership' and other teams must ask your permission before modifying anything.
Conflict Rationale: You must sacrifice your Achievement value (winning through superior competence) to help others, while the captain insists on Power (authority and control over others' use of the design).
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents sacrifice something for others' benefit—you give up competitive advantage and the captain shares proprietary work—making this Altrusive. The collaboration serves other teams rather than either agent's direct benefit.

Scenario: You and the program director must create new nutrition guidelines for 200 young athletes, recommending water instead of sports drinks based on health research. You want to base the guidelines purely on scientific evidence to create the most effective health program, but the director insists she must approve every single recommendation and wants her title as 'Chief Health Authority' printed on all materials sent to parents. If you cannot agree by next week, the guidelines will not be released and parents will continue using unhealthy practices.
Scenario_rev: You and the nutrition specialist must create new nutrition guidelines for 200 young athletes, recommending water instead of sports drinks based on health research. The specialist wants to base the guidelines purely on scientific evidence to create the most effective health program, but you insist you must approve every single recommendation and want your title as 'Chief Health Authority' printed on all materials sent to parents. If you cannot agree by next week, the guidelines will not be released and parents will continue using unhealthy practices.
Conflict Rationale: You value creating an excellent, evidence-based program that demonstrates competence (Achievement), while the director prioritizes her authoritative position and control over the final decisions (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents work toward children's health despite personal costs—you compromise on pure scientific standards and the director shares authority—making it Altrusive. The primary beneficiaries are the children, not the agents themselves.

Scenario: You and the computer science teacher must create free coding tutorial videos to help 12 struggling schools prepare for the state competition in one month. Making these tutorials gives other schools your successful techniques and reduces your school's chance of winning the top prize. The teacher agrees only if she is listed as 'Lead Instructor' on all videos and gets to decide which schools can access the materials and which cannot.
Scenario_rev: You and your curriculum developer must create free coding tutorial videos to help 12 struggling schools prepare for the state competition in one month. Making these tutorials gives other schools your successful techniques and reduces your school's chance of winning the top prize. You agree only if you are listed as 'Lead Instructor' on all videos and get to decide which schools can access the materials and which cannot.
Conflict Rationale: You must sacrifice competitive advantage and your Achievement value (winning through superior skills) to help others, while the teacher demands Power (authority to control access and recognition as the leader).
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents give up something for other schools' benefit—you sacrifice competitive edge and the teacher shares expertise without compensation—making this Altrusive. The primary beneficiaries are the struggling schools, not you or the teacher.

Scenario: You and your classmate are making a science presentation about planets that's worth 30% of your final grade, due in three days. You want to add exciting facts from new NASA discoveries to impress the teacher and get extra credit, while your classmate wants to use only the textbook so nothing is wrong. You both must agree on which sources to use before making the slides.
Scenario_rev: You and your classmate are making a science presentation about planets that's worth 30% of your final grade, due in three days. You want to use only the textbook so nothing is wrong, while your classmate wants to add exciting facts from new NASA discoveries to impress the teacher and get extra credit. You both must agree on which sources to use before making the slides.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement drives you to pursue excellence and recognition through impressive, cutting-edge content that could earn extra credit. Security motivates your classmate to avoid risk by using only verified, teacher-approved sources to ensure accuracy and prevent grade loss.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both students must work together to create one shared presentation and both receive the same grade. Success or failure of the presentation affects both students equally.

Scenario: You and your gallery partner must decide how to spend your $15,000 budget for next month's art show. You want to showcase an exciting new artist with unusual digital art that might attract famous art critics and buyers, while your partner wants to display popular local artists whose paintings always sell. You must pick one option and spend all the money this week.
Scenario_rev: You and your gallery partner must decide how to spend your $15,000 budget for next month's art show. You want to display popular local artists whose paintings always sell, while your partner wants to showcase an exciting new artist with unusual digital art that might attract famous art critics and buyers. You must pick one option and spend all the money this week.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement drives you to take risks on innovative work that could bring critical acclaim and elevate the gallery's status. Security motivates your partner to choose reliable, established artists with proven sales to ensure financial stability.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both partners must agree on one exhibition format and share the financial outcome. Both benefit from success or lose from failure of the chosen exhibition.

Scenario: You are a chef who must decide with the restaurant manager whether to cook for a 150-person wedding in two days. You want to accept it because success would bring you big catering contracts and earn you both $3,000 bonuses, while the manager worries your tired staff might make mistakes and ruin the restaurant's reputation. You both must decide together in one hour whether to say yes or no.
Scenario_rev: You are the restaurant manager who must decide with the chef whether to cook for a 150-person wedding in two days. You worry your tired staff might make mistakes and ruin the restaurant's reputation, while the chef wants to accept it because success would bring you both big catering contracts and earn you both $3,000 bonuses. You both must decide together in one hour whether to say yes or no.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement drives you to seize this opportunity for professional advancement, new business relationships, and financial reward. Security motivates the manager to protect staff wellbeing and prevent service failures that could damage the restaurant's established reputation.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must jointly decide and both share the financial bonus if successful. Both also share responsibility for consequences affecting the restaurant's operations and reputation.

Scenario: You and your friend are making a dance for the school talent show next Friday to win the $100 prize. You want to include three backflips and fast spins that could win first place but might cause falls if you mess up the timing, while your friend wants simple dance steps you can do perfectly without mistakes. You must agree on the moves and practice them together this week.
Scenario_rev: You and your friend are making a dance for the school talent show next Friday to win the $100 prize. You must agree on the moves and practice them together this week.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement drives you to perform difficult, impressive moves that could win the competition and gain recognition. Security motivates your friend to avoid risky choreography that could result in visible mistakes or injuries during the performance.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must perform the same routine together and both share the prize money if they win. Success requires coordinated practice and agreement on choreography that both can execute.

Scenario: You own a small bakery and must decide with your accountant how to use your last $8,000 in savings this month. You want to spend it on Instagram ads and weekend catering to attract new customers and grow your business, while your accountant wants to save the money and stop buying expensive ingredients to survive the next six months. You must agree on a money plan by Friday to either start advertising or make cuts.

You must agree on a money plan by Friday to either start advertising or make cuts.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement drives you to invest in business growth and expansion opportunities that could increase success and market presence. Security motivates the accountant to preserve resources and reduce financial risk to ensure the bakery's survival during uncertain times.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must agree on the financial decision that affects the bakery's future. Both share the outcome whether the business grows from investment or survives through conservation.

Scenario: You are a teacher who must decide with the department head whether to offer a difficult extra-credit project worth 15% of the grade. You want to add it so top students can improve their scores and show advanced skills, while the department head worries it will make struggling students feel more stressed and cause parents to complain. Scenario_rev: You are the department head who must decide with a teacher whether to offer a difficult extra-credit project worth 15% of the grade. Scenario: You and your cousin are running a lemonade stand on Saturday to earn $40 total for new bikes. You want to set up at the busy corner near the grocery store where 100 people walk by each hour, but there's fast traffic and no shade, while your cousin wants the quiet park where only 20 people pass by but it's safe from cars. Scenario_rev: You and your cousin are running a lemonade stand on Saturday to earn $40 total for new bikes. Conflict Rationale: Achievement drives you to choose the high-traffic location that maximizes earning potential and goal attainment. Security motivates your cousin to prioritize physical safety by avoiding traffic hazards, even if it reduces earning opportunities.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must work the same lemonade stand together and both share the earnings toward the $40 goal. Success requires joint agreement on location and shared effort at the chosen spot.

Scenario: You and your cousin are planning a school carnival to raise $500 for your class trip, and you must decide the format. You want Friday evening from 6-9 PM with music, games, and lights to attract lots of families and make more money, while your cousin wants Saturday afternoon from 12-3 PM with simple activities that are easier to supervise. Scenario_rev: You and your cousin are planning a school carnival to raise $500 for your class trip, and you must decide the format. Conflict Rationale: Achievement drives you to create an ambitious, exciting event that maximizes attendance and fundraising success. Security motivates your cousin to choose a simpler, more controlled format that's easier to manage and reduces risk of problems.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must organize the same carnival together and both benefit from reaching the $500 fundraising goal. Both share responsibility for the event's success or failure.

Scenario: You and your business partner Sam are launching an online course next month and must choose your marketing plan. You want to use bold, attention-grabbing social media ads targeting young professionals to get maximum sales and visibility, while Sam wants to use gentle ads with customer reviews sent to his current followers to protect his reputation. Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both partners must agree on one marketing strategy and both share the financial outcome of the course launch. Both benefit from sales success or lose from marketing failure.

Scenario: You and your company's security expert must decide whether to rebuild the customer app using newer, faster technology that speeds things up by 40% but hasn't been fully tested for safety problems. You both must sign approval for the six-month project starting Monday, and finishing it successfully gets you both a big yearly bonus. If you wait to decide, the project budget gets cut in half.
Scenario_rev: You are the company's security expert who must decide with your colleague whether to rebuild the customer app using newer, faster technology that speeds things up by 40% but hasn't been fully tested for safety problems. Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must approve the same project decision and both receive the bonus if performance targets are met. Both share responsibility for the project's technical and security outcomes.

Scenario: You and your sibling must choose a business partner for your family's small store since your parents are retiring. Scenario_rev: You and your sibling must choose a business partner for your family's small store since your parents are retiring. Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must jointly agree on one partnership and both share ownership of the store's future. Both benefit from business success or suffer from failure under the chosen partnership.

Scenario: You and your roommate both work from home and must create a weekly schedule for the one quiet study room that only one person can use at a time. You both need to complete your weekly work goals in this room, and the apartment lease requires you to share spaces fairly or face eviction. You want the room during 2-4 PM for important client video calls that could earn you a promotion, while your roommate needs those same hours for sleep after night shifts. You must collaborate to design a fair weekly schedule that meets both your needs, while competing for priority during the 2-4 PM overlap when you both need it most.
Scenario_rev: You and your roommate both work from home and must create a weekly schedule for the one quiet study room that only one person can use at a time. You must collaborate to design a fair weekly schedule that meets both your needs, while competing for priority during the 2-4 PM overlap when you both need it most.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement drives you to secure prime hours for career-advancing client calls. Security motivates your roommate to protect consistent rest time essential for health and job performance after night shifts.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you must collaborate to create one acceptable shared schedule that prevents lease violation and eviction, while competing for priority access to the limited 2-4 PM time slot. The collaborative goal is maintaining the housing arrangement through fair scheduling, while the competition is for the overlapping hours.

Scenario: You and your coworker must choose one cost-cutting plan to present to the board by Friday: cut 15% of costs with workflow changes, cut 8% with small changes, or cut 3% safely. Management will promote whoever's preferred approach is in the final plan you both agree on. Your team of 20 people depends on this decision for their job security.
Scenario_rev: You and your colleague must choose one cost-cutting plan to present to the board by Friday: cut 15% of costs with workflow changes, cut 8% with small changes, or cut 3% safely. Conflict Rationale: Achievement values the bold 15% cuts that impress management and earn the promotion, while Security values protecting the team's jobs and avoiding risky workflow disruptions.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both must agree on one unified plan to present (collaboration), while competing for the single promotion that goes to whoever's approach is adopted (competition for recognition and career advancement).

Scenario: You and your classmate are both failing science class and must complete one joint project worth 40% of your grade, choosing between building a robot or making a poster. Conflict Rationale: Achievement wants the ambitious robot to impress the teacher and win the bonus point, while Security prefers the safer poster that won't risk technical failure and guarantees passing the class.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both students must work together on one shared project to pass the class (collaboration), while competing for the single bonus point awarded to the most creative contributor (competition for individual recognition).

Scenario: You and John, a professional athlete, must create one training schedule for his championship in six weeks, choosing between intense daily workouts or a moderate plan with rest days. Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both must agree on one training plan to prepare John for competition (collaboration), while competing for sole credit and recognition when presenting to sponsors (competition for professional reputation).

Scenario: You and another climber must pick one route to climb an unnamed mountain tomorrow: a fast one-day climb with minimal safety gear, or a slower three-day climb with extra ropes and anchors. Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both climbers must work together on one agreed route to successfully complete the climb (collaboration), while competing for sole recognition as lead climber in the magazine feature (competition for individual credit).

Scenario: You and the quality testing manager must create one plan to fix your company's late software release, choosing between a risky six-week schedule with faster testing or a safe ten-week schedule with careful testing. Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both must create one unified recovery plan to address the delayed release (collaboration), while competing for sole presentation rights and budget control based on whose approach is adopted (competition for organizational influence).

Scenario: You and your partner have $3,000 in savings and must decide together whether to pay for baby supplies and rent, or spend it on a coding bootcamp that could triple one person's income in three months. Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both must agree on one spending decision that affects their shared household finances (collaboration), while competing to be the one who presents and gets credit for the financial decision at the family dinner (competition for recognition as household leader).

Scenario: You are a pianist and must work with the concert hall director to choose your recital program for next month, deciding between a difficult new piece that could impress critics or your practiced classical music that guarantees a good performance. Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both must agree on one recital program for the concert to happen (collaboration), while competing to be the one quoted and credited in promotional materials as the program's creative vision (competition for recognition).

Scenario: You and your lab partner must submit one engineering project proposal by Friday, choosing between an automated system that could earn top grades but might not work, or a simple manual design that will likely get average grades. The professor gives individual grades based on how innovative your project is and only writes recommendation letters for students whose projects are excellent. You must both sign one proposal.
Scenario_rev: You and your lab partner must submit one engineering project proposal by Friday, choosing between an automated system that could earn top grades but might not work, or a simple manual design that will likely get average grades. Scenario: You and your former colleague must create one consulting proposal to redesign a small company's database, choosing between a safe $5,000 project paid upfront, or an ambitious $12,000 project paid only after three months of work. Only one of you can be named lead consultant on the contract, which controls future business with this client. You must submit one proposal by Monday morning.
Scenario_rev: You and your former colleague must create one consulting proposal to redesign a small company's database, choosing between a safe $5,000 project paid upfront, or an ambitious $12,000 project paid only after three months of work. Conflict Rationale: Achievement wants the ambitious $12,000 project to maximize profit and demonstrate expertise, while Security prefers the $5,000 upfront payment to ensure guaranteed income without payment risk.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both must create one unified proposal to win the consulting contract (collaboration), while competing for the lead consultant designation that determines who owns the client relationship and future work (competition for business control).

Scenario: You and your former colleague must write one business plan for a government grant to open a computer repair shop, choosing between serving 20 local customers monthly with steady income, or targeting 50+ business customers with higher profits but unpredictable payments. Only one of you can be the primary grant holder and business owner on the application. You must submit one complete plan by Friday.

Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both must create one business plan to win the government grant (collaboration), while competing for primary grant holder designation that determines who legally owns and controls the business (competition for ownership).

Scenario: You and Sarah are both marketing consultants advising John, a business owner, on his $10,000 budget, choosing between spending $8,000 on traditional mail or shifting $6,000 to digital ads with $4,000 on mail. John will pick only one of you as his ongoing advisor based on whose recommendation he follows next quarter. You must present one unified recommendation to John by Wednesday.
Scenario_rev: You and Sarah are both marketing consultants advising John, a business owner, on his $10,000 budget, choosing between spending $8,000 on traditional mail or shifting $6,000 to digital ads with $4,000 on mail. John will pick only one of you as his ongoing advisor based on whose recommendation he follows next quarter. You must present one unified recommendation to John by Wednesday.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement wants to recommend the digital ad shift to demonstrate modern marketing expertise and innovation, while Security prefers traditional mail campaigns with proven results and lower risk of wasted budget.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Coopetitive because both consultants must create one joint recommendation to advise John effectively (collaboration), while competing to have their approach chosen, which determines who becomes the ongoing advisor with continued income (competition for client retention).

Scenario: You and your colleague must decide whether to run a free Saturday job skills workshop for 15 unemployed young people, which means giving up paid overtime worth $200 each week for three months and paying $50 monthly for the community center. Your direct manager has expressed concerns that weekend unavailability shows lack of dedication and could negatively impact your performance review and job security. However, the company's senior leadership has been looking for employees who demonstrate initiative and community leadership, and successful completion of this program would be featured in the company newsletter and presented at the annual leadership conference.
Scenario_rev: You and your colleague must decide whether to run a free Saturday job skills workshop for 15 unemployed young people, which means giving up paid overtime worth $200 each week for three months and paying $50 monthly for the community center. Your direct manager has expressed concerns that weekend unavailability shows lack of dedication and could negatively impact your performance review and job security. However, the company's senior leadership has been looking for employees who demonstrate initiative and community leadership, and successful completion of this program would be featured in the company newsletter and presented at the annual leadership conference.
Conflict Rationale: Achievement fears that giving up paid weekend overtime and reducing availability will hurt your performance review and career advancement, while Security worries that running the workshop creates financial instability by losing $600 monthly in overtime income.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both must commit time, money, and career risk together to provide free training for unemployed youth (collaboration for others' benefit), requiring both to sacrifice paid overtime income and accept potential negative career consequences without personal gain (altruistic cost).

Scenario: You and the safety manager must create a joint proposal for the CEO about whether to accept a rush manufacturing order that could win your company Industry Supplier of the Year, but requires running equipment beyond recommended safety intervals for three weeks. You want to accept the order to demonstrate your facility's capabilities and win the award, while the safety manager wants to decline to avoid equipment failures and worker safety risks. You must both sign and submit the same recommendation by 5 PM today.
Scenario_rev: You and your ambitious operations colleague must create a joint proposal for the CEO about whether to accept a rush manufacturing order that could win your company Industry Supplier of the Year, but requires running equipment beyond recommended safety intervals for three weeks. You must both sign and submit the same recommendation by 5 PM today.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize the achievement of industry recognition and proving manufacturing excellence, while the safety manager prioritizes maintaining safe working conditions and preventing equipment-related risks.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because accepting the order potentially benefits the company and its reputation, but creates personal risk and burden for both decision-makers (you risk your safety reputation, the manager risks liability) for the greater organizational good.

Scenario: You and the curriculum director must create one joint recommendation to the school board about whether to keep the accelerated math program that produces award-winning students but causes high stress and dropout rates, or replace it with a slower-paced program. You want to keep the accelerated program because it wins state mathematics competitions and attracts high-achieving families to the district, while the curriculum director wants to switch to reduce student anxiety and ensure all students succeed. You must both sign the same proposal by Friday's board meeting.
Scenario_rev: You and your achievement-focused colleague must create one joint recommendation to the school board about whether to keep the accelerated math program that produces award-winning students but causes high stress and dropout rates, or replace it with a slower-paced program. You want to switch to a slower-paced program to reduce student anxiety and ensure all students succeed, while your colleague wants to keep the accelerated program because it wins state mathematics competitions and attracts high-achieving families to the district. You must both sign the same proposal by Friday's board meeting.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize the achievement of academic excellence and competitive success that brings recognition to the school, while the curriculum director prioritizes student wellbeing and creating a safe, supportive learning environment.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because either choice serves students' needs and the school's mission, but requires both decision-makers to sacrifice personal priorities—you may sacrifice competitive standing, the director may sacrifice comprehensive student support systems.

Scenario: You and the finance manager must decide whether to donate your department's emergency reserve fund of $5,000 to help ten laid-off employees complete their professional certifications, or keep it for unexpected department costs. You want to donate the money because helping these employees could earn your department a Corporate Social Responsibility award and positive media attention, while the finance manager wants to preserve the safety fund to protect against budget surprises. You must both sign the same fund transfer form by Wednesday.
Scenario_rev: You and your public relations colleague must decide whether to donate your department's emergency reserve fund of $5,000 to help ten laid-off employees complete their professional certifications, or keep it for unexpected department costs. You want to preserve the safety fund to protect against budget surprises, while your public relations colleague wants to donate the money because helping these employees could earn your department a Corporate Social Responsibility award and positive media attention. You must both sign the same fund transfer form by Wednesday.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize achieving recognition for social impact and demonstrating leadership in employee support, while the finance manager prioritizes maintaining financial security and having resources available for unexpected problems.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because donating helps laid-off employees at a potential cost to both decision-makers—you both lose access to emergency funds and could face problems if unexpected costs arise, sacrificing departmental security for others' benefit.

Scenario: You and the event coordinator must decide whether to volunteer to organize an emergency fundraiser concert in 48 hours after the community center's original fundraiser venue flooded, which could save the center's music program for 50 kids but requires you both to work non-stop. You want to take on this high-visibility rescue effort that could demonstrate your event management skills and earn media coverage, while the event coordinator wants to decline because organizing such a rushed event could fail badly and damage both your reputations. You must tell the community center director your joint decision in the next hour.
Scenario_rev: You and your ambitious colleague must decide whether to volunteer to organize an emergency fundraiser concert in 48 hours after the community center's original fundraiser venue flooded, which could save the center's music program for 50 kids but requires you both to work non-stop. You want to decline because organizing such a rushed event could fail badly and damage both your reputations, while your ambitious colleague wants to take on this high-visibility rescue effort that could demonstrate event management skills and earn media coverage. You must tell the community center director your joint decision in the next hour.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize the achievement opportunity of pulling off an impressive emergency event that showcases your abilities, while the event coordinator prioritizes avoiding the security risk of a potentially disastrous failure that could harm professional reputations.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because volunteering helps save a children's program at personal cost to both of you—you sacrifice your weekend and risk your reputations if the rushed event fails, doing this for the community's benefit rather than your own.

Scenario: You and the database administrator must decide whether to volunteer to migrate the company's customer database to a new system over the holiday weekend, which would prevent service disruptions for 10,000 customers but means you both work 30 hours during your vacation. You want to take on this high-visibility technical project because successfully completing it could earn you both the Quarterly Excellence Award and recognition from executives, while the database administrator wants to wait until after holidays because rushing the migration could cause data loss and system crashes. You must tell your IT director your joint decision by 6 PM today.
Scenario_rev: You and your career-driven colleague must decide whether to volunteer to migrate the company's customer database to a new system over the holiday weekend, which would prevent service disruptions for 10,000 customers but means you both work 30 hours during your vacation. Conflict Rationale: You prioritize achieving recognition through successfully completing a challenging technical project under pressure, while the database administrator prioritizes the security of customer data and preventing system failures by taking adequate time.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because volunteering serves the company and its customers by preventing service disruptions, but requires both of you to sacrifice personal vacation time and take on stress and risk for the organization's benefit.

Scenario: You and the safety inspector must decide whether to approve opening the new stadium for tomorrow's championship game that would host 15,000 fans and generate crucial revenue, even though two emergency exit inspections are incomplete. Scenario: You and the HR director must decide whether to volunteer to create and lead a new mentorship program for 30 junior employees, which requires you both to commit 8 hours weekly for six months but receives no additional pay or resources. Scenario: You and the operations manager must decide whether to volunteer to lead a six-month turnaround project at the company's failing Atlanta branch, which could save 40 jobs but requires you both to relocate and work 60-hour weeks with no guaranteed success. Scenario: You and the training director must decide whether to volunteer to create and run a free 12-week coding bootcamp for 25 unemployed workers, which requires you both to work every Saturday but could win the company a Community Impact Award. Scenario: You and the finance director must decide whether to donate your department's remaining $8,000 quarterly budget to help the struggling customer service department upgrade their phone system, or keep it for your own team's upcoming conference attendance. Scenario: You and the research compliance officer must decide whether to expand your product testing study from 50 to 200 participants to generate stronger results faster, or keep the smaller group with more intensive monitoring. Scenario: You and the city volunteer coordinator are organizing a park clean-up day to win a $5,000 'Most Improved Park' award from the city council. Scenario: You and John, a veteran employee who strictly follows procedures, must present one workflow proposal to the executive board next Tuesday. You want to make the traditional family sugar cookies used at every school event for ten years, but your cousin insists on baking their award-winning chocolate cookies that won first place last month and will likely sell out fast to reach the goal. Conflict Rationale: You value using your proven successful recipe to achieve the fundraising goal most effectively, while your cousin values preserving the family tradition at school events. Limited ingredients and time force choosing only one recipe.
Cooperation Rationale: Both of you donate your entire Saturday to bake and sell cookies for the school library. Neither of you keeps any money, making this a selfless effort to help students get new books.

Scenario: You and your uncle, the school safety monitor, are organizing visitor check-in for today's school festival where 400 families will attend. You want to use your new digital system that checks people in twice as fast and creates better safety records, but your uncle insists on the traditional paper sign-in method used at every festival for fifteen years. You must pick one system now because the festival opens in two hours and training volunteers on both would create confusion.
Scenario_rev: You and your niece/nephew, the tech-savvy organizer, are organizing visitor check-in for today's school festival where 400 families will attend. You want to use the traditional paper sign-in method used at every festival for fifteen years, but your niece/nephew insists on using their new digital system that checks people in twice as fast and creates better safety records. You must pick one system now because the festival opens in two hours and training volunteers on both would create confusion.
Conflict Rationale: You value achieving superior safety results through efficient modern technology that processes visitors faster, while your uncle values preserving the traditional trusted method. Training time constraints require selecting one check-in system.
Cooperation Rationale: Both of you volunteer your entire Saturday without pay to ensure students and families stay safe at the festival. Neither of you gains personally from organizing this safety system.

Scenario: You and the experienced violin teacher are organizing free music lessons every Tuesday for twelve younger students whose families cannot afford classes. You want to use a modern method that gets students performance-ready in three months to build their confidence quickly, but the teacher insists on the traditional technique passed down through generations that takes longer. You must decide on one teaching approach today so you can order the correct music books and prepare consistent lesson plans.
Scenario_rev: You and the eager co-organizer are organizing free music lessons every Tuesday for twelve younger students whose families cannot afford classes. You want to use the traditional technique passed down through generations that takes longer, but the co-organizer insists on using a modern method that gets students performance-ready in three months to build their confidence quickly. You must decide on one teaching approach today so you can order the correct music books and prepare consistent lesson plans.
Conflict Rationale: You value achieving visible student progress and performance success quickly through modern methods, while the violin teacher values preserving the traditional technique with deep historical roots. Budget constraints allow purchasing only one set of teaching materials.
Cooperation Rationale: Both of you donate every Tuesday evening to teach students who otherwise could not access music education. Neither of you receives payment for these volunteer lessons.

Scenario: You and your bandmate are performing a benefit concert tomorrow night to raise $1,000 for the music program at an underfunded elementary school. You want to play a modernized whistling arrangement that will impress the 200 audience members and encourage higher donations, but your bandmate insists on the traditional piece your father taught you played exactly as written for generations. You must finalize one arrangement today because tomorrow's rehearsal is your only practice time before the concert.
Scenario_rev: You and your bandmate are performing a benefit concert tomorrow night to raise $1,000 for the music program at an underfunded elementary school. You want to play the traditional piece your father taught you played exactly as written for generations, but your bandmate insists on playing a modernized whistling arrangement that will impress the 200 audience members and encourage higher donations. You must finalize one arrangement today because tomorrow's rehearsal is your only practice time before the concert.
Conflict Rationale: You value achieving the best fundraising results through an impressive modern performance, while your bandmate values preserving the traditional musical arrangement passed down through family. Limited rehearsal time requires committing to one version.
Cooperation Rationale: Both of you perform for free and donate your time to help elementary students receive instruments and music education. Neither of you receives payment or personal benefit from the concert.

Scenario: You and a senior cadet are organizing tomorrow's memorial service honoring fifteen fallen community heroes, and 300 people will attend. You want to create a streamlined modern ceremony that efficiently highlights each person's major achievements in two hours, but the senior cadet insists on preserving the traditional lengthy formal program that takes four hours like it has for thirty years. You must finalize one program format today to print materials and brief the twelve participants on their roles.
Scenario_rev: You and a reform-minded organizer are organizing tomorrow's memorial service honoring fifteen fallen community heroes, and 300 people will attend. You want to preserve the traditional lengthy formal program that takes four hours like it has for thirty years, but the organizer insists on creating a streamlined modern ceremony that efficiently highlights each person's major achievements in two hours. You must finalize one program format today to print materials and brief the twelve participants on their roles.
Conflict Rationale: You value efficiently achieving a meaningful tribute that respects attendees' time while showcasing each hero's accomplishments, while the senior cadet values preserving the traditional solemn ceremony. Printing deadlines require choosing one program length and structure.
Cooperation Rationale: Both of you volunteer extra hours beyond your regular training duties to organize this community memorial service. Neither of you receives compensation or personal recognition for this organizational work.

Scenario: You and Dr. Martinez must write one 2,000-word essay for a museum catalog due in six weeks. You want to focus on your new theory about painting techniques because art journals might publish it and boost your academic reputation. Dr. Martinez wants to write about unknown local artists' personal struggles so these artists can get more gallery opportunities and sell their work.
Scenario_rev: You and your academic colleague must write one 2,000-word essay for a museum catalog due in six weeks. You want to write about unknown local artists' personal struggles so these artists can get more gallery opportunities and sell their work. Your colleague wants to focus on their new theory about painting techniques because art journals might publish it and boost their academic reputation.
Conflict Rationale: Your achievement value drives you toward scholarly recognition through theoretical innovation. Dr. Martinez's benevolence value motivates helping disadvantaged artists gain practical opportunities and recognition.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must co-author and approve the same essay, and both benefit from the publication—you gain a prestigious portfolio piece and Dr. Martinez gains a completed scholarly contribution. Success requires genuine collaboration and mutual agreement on the approach.

Scenario: You and Sarah, the volunteer coordinator, must submit one budget plan to your donor by Friday for a $500,000 grant. You want to put 70% into a new job-training program that tracks how many teens get jobs, which will prove your charity works well and help you get more grants. Sarah wants to keep 70% in the mentorship program where volunteers build close friendships with struggling kids, even though you can't easily count the results.
Scenario_rev: You and your charity director must submit one budget plan to your donor by Friday for a $500,000 grant. You want to keep 70% in the mentorship program where volunteers build close friendships with struggling kids, even though you can't easily count the results. Your director wants to put 70% into a new job-training program that tracks how many teens get jobs, which will prove your charity works well and help you get more grants.
Conflict Rationale: Your achievement value pushes you toward measurable outcomes and demonstrable effectiveness that prove organizational success. Sarah's benevolence value prioritizes the caring relationships and emotional support that help vulnerable youth, even without clear metrics.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must sign the same proposal, and both share the outcome—either both keep their jobs with renewed funding or both face program cuts. The donor requires one unified budget, making collaboration necessary.

Scenario_rev: You and your manager must decide whether to bring your six junior employees to an awards ceremony tomorrow where your company is being honored. Conflict Rationale: Your achievement value drives you to showcase success and pursue networking opportunities that advance your company's reputation. Emma's benevolence value motivates her to protect employees' wellbeing and prioritize their personal needs during difficult times.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both are business partners who must submit one RSVP together and both share the consequences—either both gain networking opportunities or both miss them. The decision affects your shared company's representation.

Conflict Rationale: Your achievement value drives you toward showcasing measurable success and competitive outcomes that demonstrate program effectiveness. Maya's benevolence value motivates her to emphasize how the program helps struggling students who need support the most.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must approve the same statement, and both benefit from the newspaper coverage—you gain program visibility and Maya gains recognition as a youth leader. The newspaper will only print one version that both sign.

Scenario: You and Lisa must decide by Monday whether your after-school program will join a televised talent competition. Scenario_rev: You and your program director must decide by Monday whether your after-school program will join a televised talent competition. Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both must sign the participation contract together, and both share the outcome—either both gain the competition opportunity and potential funding, or both maintain the current program structure. Neither can proceed without the other's agreement.

Scenario: You and Robert must split your 20 weekly lab hours for the next two months before your grant presentation. Scenario_rev: You and your research partner must split your 20 weekly lab hours for the next two months before your grant presentation. Scenario: You and Jordan must create one project timeline for your engineering assignment worth 40% of your grade, due Friday. Scenario_rev: You and your project partner must create one project timeline for your engineering assignment worth 40% of your grade, due Friday.
