Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you sacrifice your lunch break free time to redo your project, giving up personal time to help the new students feel welcome.

Scenario: You and the HR director must decide whether to both take a day off without pay next week to speak at the company meeting about why the wellness program should continue, which helps 200 workers with stress and health problems. The company policy says employees should report concerns through the official written form system, but the HR director wants to skip that and talk directly to the senior executives so they see her as a strong leader. You both must submit one decision letter to the ethics office by Friday.
Scenario_rev: You and the employee advocate must decide whether to both take a day off without pay next week to speak at the company meeting about why the wellness program should continue, which helps 200 workers with stress and health problems. The company policy says employees should report concerns through the official written form system, but you want to skip that and talk directly to the senior executives so they see you as a strong leader. You both must submit one decision letter to the ethics office by Friday.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the official company process for reporting concerns (Conformity), while the HR director wants to bypass the rules to directly influence powerful executives (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you lose a day's pay and use your personal time to help 200 coworkers keep their wellness support, sacrificing money for others' benefit.

Scenario: You and the tree club president must decide whether to spend your Saturdays for the next month planting trees in the public park to help elderly neighbors who can't do it themselves. The town rule says all volunteer tree planting must happen in the assigned public spaces, but the president wants to plant in the rich neighborhood's private yards instead so wealthy residents will see the work and give big donations that make the club more important. You both must submit one location choice to the town office today.
Scenario_rev: You and the club member must decide whether to spend your Saturdays for the next month planting trees in the public park to help elderly neighbors who can't do it themselves. The town rule says all volunteer tree planting must happen in the assigned public spaces, but you want to plant in the rich neighborhood's private yards instead so wealthy residents will see the work and give big donations that make the club more important. You both must submit one location choice to the town office today.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the town's rule about planting in assigned public spaces (Conformity), while the president wants to work in private areas to impress wealthy people and increase the club's influence (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you give up your Saturday free time to plant trees for elderly people who need help, sacrificing your weekend fun for their benefit.

Scenario: You and the HR manager must decide whether to both volunteer 10 hours each week for the next month to create a mentorship program that helps new employees learn their jobs. The company's rules say all mentors must receive the same training and help employees equally, but the HR manager wants to create a special senior mentor group that only helps the best workers and gets private meetings with company executives. You both must give one program plan to the CEO next Monday.
Scenario_rev: You and the program coordinator must decide whether to both volunteer 10 hours each week for the next month to create a mentorship program that helps new employees learn their jobs. The company's rules say all mentors must receive the same training and help employees equally, but you want to create a special senior mentor group that only helps the best workers and gets private meetings with company executives. You both must give one program plan to the CEO next Monday.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the company's equal-opportunity rules for mentorship (Conformity), while the HR manager wants to create an exclusive tier that gives certain people privileged access to executives (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you volunteer significant personal time each week to help new employees succeed, sacrificing your free time for their benefit.

Scenario: You and the community center director must decide whether to both work extra evening shifts for free next month to pay for a professional helper who can solve the fight between two youth groups. The center's rules say student conflicts should be solved using the regular peer mediation process where students talk it out themselves, but the director wants to hire an outside expert instead so the board members see him as a strong problem-solver. You both must agree on one approach to start this week.
Scenario_rev: You and the youth coordinator must decide whether to both work extra evening shifts for free next month to pay for a professional helper who can solve the fight between two youth groups. The center's rules say student conflicts should be solved using the regular peer mediation process where students talk it out themselves, but you want to hire an outside expert instead so the board members see you as a strong problem-solver. You both must agree on one approach to start this week.
Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the center's standard peer mediation rules (Conformity), while the director wants to bypass the process to demonstrate leadership ability to powerful board members (Power).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you work extra evening shifts without pay to help resolve the youth groups' conflict, sacrificing your personal time for the community's benefit.

Scenario: You and the parent association president must choose the ticket system for next month's talent show by Friday. You want the new digital system where you approve all ticket sales and see buyer information, but the president wants printed tickets sold at the door like always. You both need 200 attendees to raise $2,000 for library books.
Scenario_rev: You and your organizer colleague must choose the ticket system for next month's talent show by Friday. You want printed tickets sold at the door like always, but your colleague wants the new digital system where they approve all ticket sales and see buyer information. You both need 200 attendees to raise $2,000 for library books.
Conflict Rationale: You want control over ticket sales and access to attendee data (Power), while the president wants to maintain the school's long-standing paper ticket tradition (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents directly benefit from achieving the fundraising goal together, and success requires mutual agreement on the system. Neither can proceed without the other's cooperation.

Scenario: You and the head librarian must pick the book checkout system before school starts in two weeks. You want the digital platform where you set borrowing limits and track which books each child reads, but the librarian wants the manual logbook used for 15 years. You both need 80% of children borrowing books to win a $10,000 grant.
Scenario_rev: You and your administrative colleague must pick the book checkout system before school starts in two weeks. You want the manual logbook used for 15 years, but your colleague wants the digital platform where they set borrowing limits and track which books each child reads. You both need 80% of children borrowing books to win a $10,000 grant.
Conflict Rationale: You want authority to set rules and monitor individual reading data (Power), while the librarian wants to preserve the manual system that respects established library practices (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents share the goal of securing the grant and both benefit equally from increased borrowing rates. Success requires joint implementation of one agreed system.

Scenario: You and the sports coordinator must choose the league ranking system before registration closes in one week. You want the new analytics system where you track each player's performance and create official rankings, but the coordinator wants the simple win-loss record used for 25 years. You both need 120 kids to register so the league can form eight teams and get three sponsor donations.
Scenario_rev: You and your administrative colleague must choose the league ranking system before registration closes in one week. You want the simple win-loss record used for 25 years, but your colleague wants the new analytics system where they track each player's performance and create official rankings. You both need 120 kids to register so the league can form eight teams and get three sponsor donations.
Conflict Rationale: You want control over performance measurement and the authority to rank players (Power), while the coordinator wants to maintain the traditional simple system that has worked for decades (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents need sufficient registration numbers to make the league viable, and both benefit from sponsor support. Agreement on one system is necessary to proceed with registration.

Scenario: You and the transportation supervisor must pick bus routes for the three-day festival starting Friday, deciding by Wednesday. You want the new system where you monitor all buses on a map and change routes whenever you want, but the supervisor wants the fixed neighborhood routes used for 18 years. You both need to move 5,000 visitors smoothly to keep the festival successful.
Scenario_rev: You and your planning colleague must pick bus routes for the three-day festival starting Friday, deciding by Wednesday. You want the fixed neighborhood routes used for 18 years, but your colleague wants the new system where they monitor all buses on a map and change routes whenever they want. You both need to move 5,000 visitors smoothly to keep the festival successful.
Conflict Rationale: You want authority to monitor buses and make routing decisions in real-time (Power), while the supervisor wants to maintain the predictable routes that festival-goers have relied upon (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents share responsibility for festival transportation success, and both benefit from efficient visitor movement. Neither can implement a system without the other's agreement.

Scenario: You and the village elder must decide how families get garden plots before planting starts in three weeks. You want the digital system where you receive all applications and decide who gets which plot, but the elder wants families to claim plots in person like they have for generations. You both need 30 families growing vegetables to supply the community food bank.
Scenario_rev: You and your administrative colleague must decide how families get garden plots before planting starts in three weeks. You want families to claim plots in person like they have for generations, but your colleague wants the digital system where they receive all applications and decide who gets which plot. You both need 30 families growing vegetables to supply the community food bank.
Conflict Rationale: You want control over the assignment process and decision-making authority (Power), while the elder wants to preserve the community gathering and informal claiming tradition (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents need sufficient family participation to meet food bank supply goals, and both benefit from a successful garden. Agreement on one system is required to begin planting.

Scenario: You and the parade director must decide the float order for Saturday's parade by tonight. You want the corporate sponsorship plan where you choose which paying sponsors march at the front, but the director wants volunteer community groups leading like they have for 30 years. You both need 3,000 spectators and $8,000 in vendor fees to cover costs and fund next year's parade.
Scenario_rev: You and your planning colleague must decide the float order for Saturday's parade by tonight. You want volunteer community groups leading like they have for 30 years, but your colleague wants the corporate sponsorship plan where they choose which paying sponsors march at the front. You both need 3,000 spectators and $8,000 in vendor fees to cover costs and fund next year's parade.
Conflict Rationale: You want authority to determine parade order and prioritize sponsors who provide resources (Power), while the director wants to honor the tradition of community volunteers leading (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents need spectator turnout and vendor revenue for parade sustainability, and both benefit from financial success. Agreement tonight is necessary to print programs and notify participants.

Scenario: You and the senior physician must choose the appointment reminder system before patient visits double next month. You want automated texts and emails that you send and track to see who opens them, but the physician wants staff to make personal phone calls like the clinic has done for 20 years. You both need to reduce missed appointments from 25% to under 10% to keep insurance payments.
Scenario_rev: You and your administrative colleague must choose the appointment reminder system before patient visits double next month. You want staff to make personal phone calls like the clinic has done for 20 years, but your colleague wants automated texts and emails that they send and track to see who opens them. You both need to reduce missed appointments from 25% to under 10% to keep insurance payments.
Conflict Rationale: You want control over the reminder process and access to tracking data (Power), while the physician wants to maintain the personal touch and established calling practices (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents share the goal of reducing missed appointments to maintain revenue, and both benefit from insurance reimbursement. Joint agreement is needed for implementation.

Scenario: You and the fourth-grade teacher must pick the behavior tracking system before school starts in five days. You want the digital points system where you record every behavior and show scores on a classroom screen, but the teacher wants the sticker chart method used successfully for 15 years. You both need 90% of students meeting behavior goals so the class earns extra art and music time.
Scenario_rev: You and your administrative colleague must pick the behavior tracking system before school starts in five days. You want the sticker chart method used successfully for 15 years, but your colleague wants the digital points system where they record every behavior and show scores on a classroom screen. You both need 90% of students meeting behavior goals so the class earns extra art and music time.
Conflict Rationale: You want authority to monitor and publicly display behavior scores (Power), while the teacher wants to continue the familiar sticker system that has proven effective (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents share the goal of achieving the 90% behavior threshold to unlock rewards, and both benefit when students succeed. One unified system must be chosen.

Scenario: You and the lead engineer must choose how to present project results to the funding committee before next week's review. You want the interactive dashboard where you control what data shows and update it during the meeting, but the engineer wants printed reports submitted quarterly like the team has done for 12 years. You both need committee approval to receive the next $200,000 for your water purification project.
Scenario_rev: You and your project manager colleague must choose how to present project results to the funding committee before next week's review. You want printed reports submitted quarterly like the team has done for 12 years, but your colleague wants the interactive dashboard where they control what data shows and update it during the meeting. You both need committee approval to receive the next $200,000 for your water purification project.
Conflict Rationale: You want control over data presentation and the authority to adjust information dynamically (Power), while the engineer wants to maintain the established reporting format (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents need funding approval for the project to continue, and both benefit equally from securing the $200,000 installment. Agreement on presentation format is required.

Scenario: You and the recycling coordinator must choose the waste bins for the new park opening Saturday, deciding by Friday. You want smart bins with sensors that you monitor to see what people throw away and give them feedback messages, but the coordinator wants the simple three-bin system used in all town parks for 20 years. You both need 75% correct sorting to meet the state grant requirements worth $15,000.
Scenario_rev: You and your planning colleague must choose the waste bins for the new park opening Saturday, deciding by Friday. You want the simple three-bin system used in all town parks for 20 years, but your colleague wants smart bins with sensors that they monitor to see what people throw away and give them feedback messages. You both need 75% correct sorting to meet the state grant requirements worth $15,000.
Conflict Rationale: You want control over monitoring waste data and authority to provide sorting instructions (Power), while the coordinator wants the proven simple system that maintains consistency across parks (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents share the goal of meeting grant requirements, and both benefit from the $15,000 funding. Agreement is needed to install bins before opening.

Scenario: You and the fire captain must choose the emergency alert system before wildfire season starts in two weeks. You want the cell phone network where you decide when to send alerts and what messages to write, but the captain wants the traditional sirens and church bells the town has used for 40 years. You both need 95% of residents receiving and responding to warnings within five minutes to meet new state safety rules.
Scenario_rev: You and your emergency management colleague must choose the emergency alert system before wildfire season starts in two weeks. You want the traditional sirens and church bells the town has used for 40 years, but your colleague wants the cell phone network where they decide when to send alerts and what messages to write. You both need 95% of residents receiving and responding to warnings within five minutes to meet new state safety rules.
Conflict Rationale: You want authority to control alert timing and messaging (Power), while the captain wants to rely on the time-tested warning system the community knows (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents share responsibility for resident safety and meeting state requirements, and both benefit from effective alert response. One system must be chosen immediately.

Scenario: You and the principal must design the student council election before nominations open Monday, deciding by Friday. You want the appointment system where you interview candidates and select leaders, but the principal wants the school-wide voting process used for 25 years. You both need the council working in three weeks to approve the $5,000 activities budget, and you compete to have your preferred candidate win the president position based on whichever election process is chosen.
Scenario_rev: You and your administrative colleague must design the student council election before nominations open Monday, deciding by Friday. You want the school-wide voting process used for 25 years, but your colleague wants the appointment system where they interview candidates and select leaders. You both need the council working in three weeks to approve the $5,000 activities budget, and you compete to have your preferred candidate win the president position based on whichever election process is chosen.
Conflict Rationale: You want authority to select leaders through controlled interviews (Power), while the principal wants to preserve the democratic voting tradition (Tradition).
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents must collaborate to establish one election process that seats a functioning council, while competing for their preferred candidate to win the presidency. The competition domain is which candidate becomes president, determined by the chosen election format.

Scenario: You and the farm cooperative treasurer must agree on one sales method for selling 5,000 pounds of tomatoes before they spoil in two weeks: your online platform where you control all pricing and order approvals, or the traditional Saturday farmer's market booth the families have run for 30 years. You both share the revenue equally, but whoever's method sells more produce gets to choose the sales approach for the next three harvests and will be featured in the cooperative's newsletter as the sales innovator.
Scenario_rev: You and your cooperative organizer must agree on one sales method for selling 5,000 pounds of tomatoes before they spoil in two weeks: the organizer's online platform where they control all pricing and order approvals, or your traditional Saturday farmer's market booth the families have run for 30 years. You both share the revenue equally, but whoever's method sells more produce gets to choose the sales approach for the next three harvests and will be featured in the cooperative's newsletter as the sales innovator.
Conflict Rationale: You want centralized control over pricing and sales decisions to maximize efficiency and your authority. The treasurer wants to preserve the traditional market approach that has maintained family and community relationships over decades.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you both must agree on one method and share the revenue (collaboration), while competing to prove whose approach sells more produce to gain decision-making authority and recognition for future seasons. The competition is for both future control over sales methods and public recognition as the sales innovator.

Scenario: You and the current soccer team captain must agree on one new captain selection rule to present to the league before Friday's registration deadline: your point-based system where the player with the highest skill scores (which you track as team manager) becomes captain, or the captain's traditional rule where the player who has been on the team longest automatically leads. The league requires both of you to sign the proposal, and whoever's rule gets adopted determines the next captain and gets their name listed as the rule creator in the official league handbook.
Scenario_rev: You and the team manager must agree on one new captain selection rule to present to the league before Friday's registration deadline: the manager's point-based system where the player with the highest skill scores (which they track as team manager) becomes captain, or your traditional rule where the player who has been on the team longest automatically leads. The league requires both of you to sign the proposal, and whoever's rule gets adopted determines the next captain and gets their name listed as the rule creator in the official league handbook.
Conflict Rationale: You want a merit-based system that gives you influence through tracking and evaluating performance. The captain wants to maintain the traditional seniority rule that honors long-term commitment and experience.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you both must submit one unanimous proposal together (collaboration), while competing to have your preferred rule adopted, which determines future captaincy and who receives recognition as the rule's architect in official league records. The competition is for both decision-making influence and public recognition.

Scenario: You and the cultural events coordinator must submit one stage plan for the town's harvest festival by Monday: your design with one main stage and a central sound booth you would operate and control, or the coordinator's plan for three small performance circles that the town has used for 50 years. The town council will pick the better plan, and the winning designer gets to lead all festival planning next year and receives a $500 stipend plus recognition at the opening ceremony.
Scenario_rev: You and the stage designer must submit one stage plan for the town's harvest festival by Monday: the designer's design with one main stage and a central sound booth they would operate and control, or your plan for three small performance circles that the town has used for 50 years. The town council will pick the better plan, and the winning designer gets to lead all festival planning next year and receives a $500 stipend plus recognition at the opening ceremony.
Conflict Rationale: You want centralized control over the technical setup and performance scheduling through one main stage. The coordinator wants to preserve the traditional multi-circle format that has served the community's cultural practices for decades.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you both must create and submit one unified plan together (collaboration), while competing to have your design chosen, which determines who leads future festival planning and receives the stipend and public recognition. The competition is for future authority, financial reward, and public acknowledgment.

Scenario: You and the head librarian must agree on one way to reorganize the history section before the county inspection next week: your thematic system grouping books by influence and power (with you choosing the featured displays), or the librarian's chronological arrangement that students have used for 40 years. The inspection score determines which system stays, and the winner becomes the lead organizer for the upcoming library renovation project with a $2,000 project bonus.
Scenario_rev: You and the reorganization coordinator must agree on one way to reorganize the history section before the county inspection next week: the coordinator's thematic system grouping books by influence and power (with them choosing the featured displays), or your chronological arrangement that students have used for 40 years. The inspection score determines which system stays, and the winner becomes the lead organizer for the upcoming library renovation project with a $2,000 project bonus.
Conflict Rationale: You want to organize books by themes of power and influence, giving you authority to curate what gets featured. The librarian wants to keep the chronological system that students know and that has worked reliably for four decades.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you both must implement one agreed system before inspection (collaboration), while competing for the higher inspection rating that determines which system remains and who leads the renovation project with its associated bonus. The competition is for both organizational control and financial reward.

Scenario: You and the building superintendent must present one heating schedule proposal to the residents' board before winter: your flexible system where a residents' committee (which you would chair) can adjust building-wide heating times based on weekly weather forecasts, or the superintendent's fixed schedule based on 20-year heating patterns that residents currently follow. The board will evaluate energy costs and comfort surveys after one month, and the better system becomes permanent with its designer receiving a $1,000 efficiency bonus and the title of Energy Coordinator.
Scenario_rev: You and the residents' committee chair must present one heating schedule proposal to the residents' board before winter: the committee chair's flexible system where a residents' committee (which they would chair) can adjust building-wide heating times based on weekly weather forecasts, or your fixed schedule based on 20-year heating patterns that residents currently follow. The board will evaluate energy costs and comfort surveys after one month, and the better system becomes permanent with its designer receiving a $1,000 efficiency bonus and the title of Energy Coordinator.
Conflict Rationale: You want a flexible system that gives you authority to make heating decisions through committee leadership. The superintendent wants to maintain the reliable fixed schedule that has worked traditionally and requires no ongoing decisions.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you both must present one unified proposal to the board (collaboration), while competing for better performance results that determine which system continues and who receives the bonus and coordinator title. The competition is for both decision-making authority and financial reward with recognition.

Scenario: You and the senior scout leader must agree on one communication method for the five-day camping trip before you leave Saturday: your walkie-talkie system where all messages go through you as coordinator for quick decisions, or the leader's traditional twice-daily campfire meetings where all 20 scouts discuss plans together as the troop has done for 60 years. The troop committee will review safety logs and scout feedback after the trip, and the winning method becomes official policy with its creator earning the Lead Coordinator badge and leading the summer expedition.
Scenario_rev: You and the communications coordinator must agree on one communication method for the five-day camping trip before you leave Saturday: the coordinator's walkie-talkie system where all messages go through them as coordinator for quick decisions, or your traditional twice-daily campfire meetings where all 20 scouts discuss plans together as the troop has done for 60 years. The troop committee will review safety logs and scout feedback after the trip, and the winning method becomes official policy with its creator earning the Lead Coordinator badge and leading the summer expedition.
Conflict Rationale: You want centralized communication that lets you coordinate and make quick decisions as the hub of information. The leader wants to preserve the traditional group meetings that have built scout community and shared decision-making for six decades.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you both must use one agreed communication system during the trip (collaboration), while competing for better safety and coordination results that determine which method becomes policy and who earns the badge and leads future trips. The competition is for both policy control and leadership recognition.

Scenario: You and the neighborhood watch coordinator must agree on one reporting system before the July 4th weekend when break-ins increase: your online platform where reports come to a response team that you lead to assign priority levels, or the coordinator's traditional weekly meetings where all 50 neighbors share concerns in person as the neighborhood has done for 25 years. The neighborhood association will track response speed and resident satisfaction over July, and the better system becomes permanent with its creator becoming the paid Safety Director earning $3,000 annually.
Scenario_rev: You and the online platform developer must agree on one reporting system before the July 4th weekend when break-ins increase: the developer's online platform where reports come to a response team that they lead to assign priority levels, or your traditional weekly meetings where all 50 neighbors share concerns in person as the neighborhood has done for 25 years. The neighborhood association will track response speed and resident satisfaction over July, and the better system becomes permanent with its creator becoming the paid Safety Director earning $3,000 annually.
Conflict Rationale: You want a digital system that gives you leadership authority to prioritize and coordinate responses efficiently. The coordinator wants to keep the traditional face-to-face meetings that have maintained neighborhood relationships and collective awareness for 25 years.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because you both must implement one system together during the high-crime period (collaboration), while competing for better performance metrics that determine which system continues and who becomes the paid Safety Director. The competition is for both leadership authority and financial compensation.

Scenario: You and the school principal must decide how to spend a $50,000 donation before the deadline next Friday: new computers and science equipment that would create a modern STEM lab preparing students for technology careers, or restoring the 80-year-old auditorium that hosts traditional ceremonies but is now structurally unsafe. Either choice requires both of you to spend 50 hours over the next six weeks coordinating contractors and setup, time you desperately need for other urgent responsibilities, but 600 students and their families are counting on you to use this money wisely.
Scenario_rev: You and the technology advocate must decide how to spend a $50,000 donation before the deadline next Friday: the advocate's proposal for new computers and science equipment that would create a modern STEM lab preparing students for technology careers, or your plan for restoring the 80-year-old auditorium that hosts traditional ceremonies but is now structurally unsafe. Either choice requires both of you to spend 50 hours over the next six weeks coordinating contractors and setup, time you desperately need for other urgent responsibilities, but 600 students and their families are counting on you to use this money wisely.
Conflict Rationale: The decision requires choosing between modernization that would enhance student competitiveness and future opportunities, versus preserving the traditional ceremonial space that holds cultural and historical significance for the school community. Both of you must sacrifice significant personal time regardless of which choice serves the community.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you both must work together to make one decision and implement it while sacrificing 50 hours of your limited time that you need for other pressing work obligations. The sacrifice is the substantial time commitment required regardless of the decision outcome, not just value suppression.

Scenario: You and the village council elder must decide what to do with the town's only recreational space by next month: convert it into a modern sports complex with lights and equipment that attracts youth programs and regional tournaments (raising the town's profile), or preserve it as the simple grass field used for 100 years of traditional harvest festivals and cultural gatherings. Either choice requires both of you to lead 60 hours of volunteer work over two months managing the project instead of your paid jobs, but all 500 town families need this decision made soon.
Scenario_rev: You and the modernization advocate must decide what to do with the town's only recreational space by next month: the advocate's plan to convert it into a modern sports complex with lights and equipment that attracts youth programs and regional tournaments (raising the town's profile), or your proposal to preserve it as the simple grass field used for 100 years of traditional harvest festivals and cultural gatherings. Either choice requires both of you to lead 60 hours of volunteer work over two months managing the project instead of your paid jobs, but all 500 town families need this decision made soon.
Conflict Rationale: The decision requires choosing between modernization that would increase the town's influence and ability to host competitive events, versus preserving the traditional space that has served cultural ceremonies for a century. Both of you must sacrifice substantial work income regardless of which option serves the community.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you both must work together to decide and then lead the volunteer implementation while sacrificing 60 hours of paid work time that you both need financially. The sacrifice is the significant lost income and time required regardless of the decision outcome.

Scenario: You and the parish council leader must decide how to use a $15,000 security grant by month's end: install cameras, alarms, and electronic access controls that protect valuable items and let the church serve as a secure voting location for 2,000 voters, or keep the traditional open-door policy from sunrise to sunset that has welcomed all visitors for 70 years. Either choice requires both of you to volunteer 40 hours over the next month training staff or managing installation instead of spending time with your families during the holidays, but the congregation of 500 members needs this building to remain safe and functional.
Scenario_rev: You and the security modernization advocate must decide how to use a $15,000 security grant by month's end: the advocate's plan to install cameras, alarms, and electronic access controls that protect valuable items and let the church serve as a secure voting location for 2,000 voters, or your traditional open-door policy from sunrise to sunset that has welcomed all visitors for 70 years. Either choice requires both of you to volunteer 40 hours over the next month training staff or managing installation instead of spending time with your families during the holidays, but the congregation of 500 members needs this building to remain safe and functional.
Conflict Rationale: The decision requires choosing between implementing controlled security systems that give designated people authority over building access, versus maintaining the traditional open-door practice that has defined the church's welcoming mission for seven decades. Both of you must sacrifice family time during the holidays regardless of which choice serves the congregation.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you both must work together to decide and implement one security approach while sacrificing 40 hours of precious holiday family time that you both value. The sacrifice is the significant personal time commitment during a meaningful period regardless of the decision outcome.

Scenario: You and the neighborhood watch captain must choose one safety approach for your 200-home community before summer: a mobile app with real-time alerts and a response team structure where designated block leaders (coordinated by a central committee) manage incidents, or the traditional practice where neighbors watch informally and talk to each other from porches as they have for 40 years. Either choice requires both of you to volunteer 60 hours over three months (training residents on the app or organizing the informal watch schedules) instead of using your limited free time for personal needs, but residents have demanded better security after recent break-ins.
Scenario_rev: You and the technology coordinator must choose one safety approach for your 200-home community before summer: the coordinator's mobile app with real-time alerts and a response team structure where designated block leaders (coordinated by a central committee) manage incidents, or your traditional practice where neighbors watch informally and talk to each other from porches as they have for 40 years. Either choice requires both of you to volunteer 60 hours over three months (training residents on the app or organizing the informal watch schedules) instead of using your limited free time for personal needs, but residents have demanded better security after recent break-ins.
Conflict Rationale: The decision requires choosing between creating an organized system with designated leadership and coordinated response authority, versus maintaining the traditional informal neighbor-to-neighbor approach that has built community relationships for decades. Both of you must sacrifice substantial personal free time regardless of which method protects the neighborhood.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altrusive because you both must work together to select and implement one safety system while sacrificing 60 hours of your scarce personal time that you need for yourselves and your families. The sacrifice is the significant time commitment required regardless of which system is chosen.

Scenario: You and the school librarian must decide how to use 500 square feet of library space before students return in January: create a technology center with tablets and coding stations that prepares students for modern careers, or preserve the complete classic literature collection that has been maintained for 60 years and holds deep meaning for alumni families. Either choice requires both of you to work 40 unpaid hours during your winter break reorganizing the entire library instead of taking your planned vacation, but 800 students need better learning resources when school starts.
Scenario_rev: You and the technology advocate must decide how to use 500 square feet of library space before students return in January: the advocate's plan to create a technology center with tablets and coding stations that prepares students for modern careers, or your proposal to preserve the complete classic literature collection that has been maintained for 60 years and holds deep meaning for alumni families. Scenario: You are a city planner, and you must work with the neighborhood council president to decide how to allocate the community's emergency generator during tomorrow's planned power outage—either to your new centralized control center that would coordinate all neighborhood services, or to keep it at the traditional community center where families have gathered for 50 years. You want the Premium Wilderness Package ($120 per person, fixed schedule with mandatory 6 AM wake-up and guided hikes) so you can be the trip leader who tells everyone what to do and when, showing you're in charge. Your friend wants the Basic Campground ($40 per person, flexible schedule) so the two friends with less money can afford to come and everyone can rest when they're tired.
Conflict Rationale: Your friend's preference shows Power (wanting to be the leader with authority and control over everyone's activities). Your preference shows Benevolence (caring for friends' financial situations and physical wellbeing needs).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because you're co-organizing together and the group asked you both to decide jointly and book tonight. Both your names are on the trip invitation and both benefit from a successful trip.

Scenario: You and your older sibling are organizing a family reunion dinner for 20 relatives next month and must decide together on the format by Thursday to send invitations. Your sibling wants a formal seated dinner with assigned seats, planned speeches where they present the family history, and a strict schedule they control, establishing themselves as the family leader. You want a casual backyard gathering where everyone sits where they want, bringing dishes to share, so elderly relatives can rest when tired and young children can play freely.
Scenario_rev: You and your younger sibling are organizing a family reunion dinner for 20 relatives next month and must decide together on the format by Thursday to send invitations. Conflict Rationale: Your sibling's preference shows Power (establishing authority as family leader and controlling the event structure). Your preference shows Benevolence (caring for different relatives' comfort, needs, and wellbeing).
Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because you're co-hosting together, splitting costs equally, and both your names are on the invitation. Both benefit from a successful reunion and both must agree to send invitations.

Scenario: You and your principal are designing an anti-bullying program for your middle school (400 students) launching next month, and must both approve the final plan before printing materials next week. Cooperation Rationale: This is Reciprocal because you're co-designing together and both must approve and sign off on the unified program before implementation. Both benefit from an effective program that improves school climate.

Scenario: You and your co-parent must write one discipline plan for your 7-year-old son's classroom disruptions (talking out, leaving his seat) and both sign it to give to his teacher by Friday. Scenario: You and the school principal must create one behavior plan for Alex, a 4th grader who disrupts class daily, to submit to the district counselor by Friday. Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents must collaborate to create one unified behavior plan that will actually help Alex, while simultaneously competing for recognition and resources (the grant) based on whose philosophy the plan reflects. Both the collaborative goal (helping Alex) and competitive element (grant funding) are explicit.
