Scenario: You and the library director must decide on a laptop lending program for 20 children from struggling families who have no computers at home. You want to lend laptops with just a simple form so no child feels unwelcome or ashamed, but the director wants families to pay a $50 deposit and sign liability papers to protect the library's $8,000 investment. Together, you must get at least 15 of the 20 children to improve their homework scores by 30% over three months.
Scenario_rev: You and your coordinator colleague must decide on a laptop lending program for 20 children from struggling families who have no computers at home. The coordinator wants to lend laptops with just a simple form so no child feels unwelcome or ashamed, but you want families to pay a $50 deposit and sign liability papers to protect the library's $8,000 investment. Together, you must get at least 15 of the 20 children to improve their homework scores by 30% over three months.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates helping all children feel welcomed and supported without barriers that might shame them. Security motivates protecting the library's resources through safeguards and financial accountability.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents work together toward the shared goal of improving children's homework performance, and both benefit when the program succeeds through better educational outcomes and program validation. Both must agree on the same policy for it to be implemented.

Scenario: You and the HR director must train a struggling new employee named Maria before the busy holiday rush in two weeks. You want to assign her a friendly coworker buddy who will patiently help her practice and build her confidence, but the HR director wants her to complete training videos alone and take timed tests to verify she knows safety rules. Together, you must help Maria successfully serve 20 customers per hour without errors by opening day.
Scenario_rev: You and your supervisor colleague must train a struggling new employee named Maria before the busy holiday rush in two weeks. The supervisor wants to assign her a friendly coworker buddy who will patiently help her practice and build her confidence, but you want her to complete training videos alone and take timed tests to verify she knows safety rules. Together, you must help Maria successfully serve 20 customers per hour without errors by opening day.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates providing warm, patient, relationship-based support to help Maria feel cared for and confident. Security motivates ensuring Maria demonstrates verified competence through standardized procedures to protect customers and the business.
Cooperation Rationale: This is reciprocal because both agents collaborate toward the shared goal of Maria's successful training, and both benefit when she performs well (business runs smoothly, employee succeeds). Both must agree on one training approach.

Scenario: You and the safety officer must plan a neighborhood block party for 100 people with a $500 budget, competing to have your approach featured in the town newsletter. You want to spend money on shared play equipment and potluck tables where neighbors can mix and help each other, but the safety officer wants to spend on fencing, volunteer guards, and check-in lists to control who enters. Together, you must create one plan that the neighborhood votes to approve.
Scenario_rev: You and the community organizer must plan a neighborhood block party for 100 people with a $500 budget, competing to have your approach featured in the town newsletter. The organizer wants to spend money on shared play equipment and potluck tables where neighbors can mix and help each other, but you want to spend on fencing, volunteer guards, and check-in lists to control who enters. Together, you must create one plan that the neighborhood votes to approve.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates creating an open, welcoming environment where neighbors care for each other and build relationships. Security motivates protecting attendees through controlled access and monitoring who participates.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate to create one approved block party plan (shared goal), while competing for recognition in the town newsletter as the model approach. The competition is for public recognition and validation of their method.

Scenario: You and the shelter director must create one visiting policy for the next three months, competing to have your approach highlighted in the annual report as the adoption success key. You want to let 15 children per week visit the 12 shy shelter animals in small groups with gentle guidance to give the animals love and comfort, but the director wants only 5 screened adults per week with strict rules to prevent animal stress or injury. Together, you must increase adoptions of these shy animals by 50%.
Scenario_rev: You and the volunteer coordinator must create one visiting policy for the next three months, competing to have your approach highlighted in the annual report as the adoption success key. The coordinator wants to let 15 children per week visit the 12 shy shelter animals in small groups with gentle guidance to give the animals love and comfort, but you want only 5 screened adults per week with strict rules to prevent animal stress or injury. Together, you must increase adoptions of these shy animals by 50%.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates allowing more people, especially children, to provide care and affection to help lonely animals feel loved. Security motivates protecting vulnerable animals from potential harm through careful screening and controlled interactions.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate toward the shared goal of increasing adoptions by 50%, while competing for recognition in the annual report as the architect of the successful approach. The competition is for professional credit and validation.

Scenario: You and the school nurse must create one visiting policy for a student recovering from surgery in the nurse's office for three days, competing to have the principal adopt your approach school-wide. You want to allow five different friends to visit for 10 minutes each day to cheer up the lonely student and lift their spirits, but the nurse wants only one friend for 15 minutes daily to reduce germs and keep the student resting. Together, you must keep the student comfortable and healing well enough to return to class on day four.
Scenario_rev: You and the guidance counselor must create one visiting policy for a student recovering from surgery in the nurse's office for three days, competing to have the principal adopt your approach school-wide. The counselor wants to allow five different friends to visit for 10 minutes each day to cheer up the lonely student and lift their spirits, but you want only one friend for 15 minutes daily to reduce germs and keep the student resting. Together, you must keep the student comfortable and healing well enough to return to class on day four.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates providing emotional comfort and companionship to prevent the student from feeling isolated and sad during recovery. Security motivates protecting the student's health by minimizing infection risk and ensuring proper rest.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate to create one policy that keeps the student comfortable and ensures recovery by day four, while competing to have the principal choose their approach as the school-wide standard. The competition is for policy adoption and professional influence.

Scenario: You and a senior librarian must create one reading program proposal for a $5,000 school grant, competing to be named lead designer in the district newsletter. You want an open program where any student can borrow 5 books at once with no fines so struggling readers feel welcome and supported, but the librarian wants students to earn borrowing privileges by completing reading logs to ensure books are properly tracked and returned. Together, you must design one program that increases student reading by 40% over six months.
Scenario_rev: You and a teacher advocate must create one reading program proposal for a $5,000 school grant, competing to be named lead designer in the district newsletter. The advocate wants an open program where any student can borrow 5 books at once with no fines so struggling readers feel welcome and supported, but you want students to earn borrowing privileges by completing reading logs to ensure books are properly tracked and returned. Together, you must design one program that increases student reading by 40% over six months.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates removing barriers so all students, especially struggling ones, feel welcomed and cared for without shame about late returns. Security motivates protecting the library's book collection through accountability systems and verified responsibility.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate to create one grant proposal that achieves the 40% reading increase goal, while competing for recognition as the lead designer in the district newsletter. The competition is for professional credit and public acknowledgment.

Scenario: You and the science lab manager must create one borrowing policy for 20 experiment kits worth $50 each, competing to present your policy as best practice at the district meeting. You want students to take kits home for 3 days so they can help friends and family learn science together, but the manager wants same-day returns with signed forms and in-school-only use to prevent lost or damaged equipment. Together, you must increase science homework completion from 60% to 85% over two months.
Scenario_rev: You and the science teacher must create one borrowing policy for 20 experiment kits worth $50 each, competing to present your policy as best practice at the district meeting. The teacher wants students to take kits home for 3 days so they can help friends and family learn science together, but you want same-day returns with signed forms and in-school-only use to prevent lost or damaged equipment. Together, you must increase science homework completion from 60% to 85% over two months.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates allowing students to share learning and help others by taking kits home to teach friends and family. Security motivates protecting the expensive equipment through strict tracking and controlled use within supervised school settings.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate toward the shared goal of increasing homework completion from 60% to 85%, while competing to have their policy presented at the district meeting as the model approach. The competition is for professional recognition and district-wide influence.

Scenario: You and the daycare director must create one plan for a grandmother who volunteers 15 hours weekly with toddlers, competing to have your approach featured in the daycare newsletter. You want to keep the arrangement flexible and informal so she can help out of love and give extra hugs when children are sad, but the director wants to make her an official paid employee with set hours and training requirements to ensure reliable professional coverage. Together, you must ensure dependable care for 12 toddlers every weekday afternoon.
Scenario_rev: You and the volunteer coordinator must create one plan for a grandmother who volunteers 15 hours weekly with toddlers, competing to have your approach featured in the daycare newsletter. The coordinator wants to keep the arrangement flexible and informal so she can help out of love and give extra hugs when children are sad, but you want to make her an official paid employee with set hours and training requirements to ensure reliable professional coverage. Together, you must ensure dependable care for 12 toddlers every weekday afternoon.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates preserving the warm, loving, relationship-based care where the grandmother helps from the heart. Security motivates establishing reliable, professional standards with formal commitments to ensure children always have adequate supervision.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate to ensure reliable toddler coverage every weekday, while competing to have their volunteer management approach featured in the newsletter as the model. The competition is for recognition and validation of their management philosophy.

Scenario: You and the property manager must create one rental plan for 3 empty rooms in your building, competing to present your approach at the landlord association as the best model. You want to rent all 3 rooms to struggling families at $400 each with flexible payment to help people who desperately need housing, but the manager wants to rent 2 rooms at market rate ($800) and only 1 to a needy family to ensure the $2,000 monthly building costs are safely covered. Together, you must keep all rooms filled for 12 months and cover all building costs.
Scenario_rev: You and the community housing advocate must create one rental plan for 3 empty rooms in your building, competing to present your approach at the landlord association as the best model. The advocate wants to rent all 3 rooms to struggling families at $400 each with flexible payment to help people who desperately need housing, but you want to rent 2 rooms at market rate ($800) and only 1 to a needy family to ensure the $2,000 monthly building costs are safely covered. Together, you must keep all rooms filled for 12 months and cover all building costs.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates helping more struggling families by providing affordable housing to those in desperate need of shelter. Security motivates protecting the building's financial stability through reliable market-rate income that guarantees cost coverage.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate to keep rooms filled and cover the $2,000 monthly costs for 12 months, while competing to present their rental philosophy at the landlord association meeting as the superior approach. The competition is for professional influence and validation in the landlord community.

Scenario: You and the venue manager must solve a wedding dessert crisis after the cake was dropped, competing to have your solution praised in the online review the bride will write. You want to order a $300 replacement cake arriving in 90 minutes that matches the bride's dream wedding and makes her feel special, but the manager wants $50 of store-bought cookies available in 20 minutes to safely ensure dessert is ready when the 80 guests finish dinner in 2 hours. Together, you must serve dessert to all guests before they leave.
Scenario_rev: You and the wedding planner must solve a wedding dessert crisis after the cake was dropped, competing to have your solution praised in the online review the bride will write. The planner wants to order a $300 replacement cake arriving in 90 minutes that matches the bride's dream wedding and makes her feel special, but you want $50 of store-bought cookies available in 20 minutes to safely ensure dessert is ready when the 80 guests finish dinner in 2 hours. Together, you must serve dessert to all guests before they leave.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates caring about the bride's emotional happiness and making her feel her special day is honored despite the accident. Security motivates ensuring a guaranteed dessert solution that eliminates the risk of guests waiting or leaving without dessert.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate to ensure 80 wedding guests receive dessert before leaving, while competing to have their solution praised in the bride's online review. The competition is for reputation and customer satisfaction ratings.

Scenario: You and the school counselor must create one support plan using a $200 monthly budget for a student struggling with homework, competing to have your approach featured in the district newsletter. You want to spend the budget connecting the student with caring peer tutors who can become friends and provide emotional support during difficult times, but the counselor wants structured after-school classes with attendance tracking and regular grade reports to ensure accountability. Together, you must increase the student's homework completion from 40% to 80% in two months.
Scenario_rev: You and the student support teacher must create one support plan using a $200 monthly budget for a student struggling with homework, competing to have your approach featured in the district newsletter. The teacher wants to spend the budget connecting the student with caring peer tutors who can become friends and provide emotional support during difficult times, but you want structured after-school classes with attendance tracking and regular grade reports to ensure accountability. Together, you must increase the student's homework completion from 40% to 80% in two months.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates building caring relationships where the student feels supported and emotionally connected to helpers who genuinely care. Security motivates establishing structured accountability systems that track progress and ensure the student stays on a verified path to improvement.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate to increase the student's homework completion from 40% to 80%, while competing to have their support approach featured in the district success newsletter. The competition is for professional recognition and validation of their educational philosophy.

Scenario: You and the motel owner must create one pet policy for a sold-out holiday weekend with 20 rooms, competing to have your policy adopted permanently on the website. You want to allow pets in 15 rooms with a small $20 cleaning fee so families traveling with beloved pets feel welcome and included, but the owner wants only 5 rooms with strict size rules and a $75 deposit to protect against property damage. Together, you must fill at least 18 of the 20 rooms for the holiday weekend.
Scenario_rev: You and the customer service coordinator must create one pet policy for a sold-out holiday weekend with 20 rooms, competing to have your policy adopted permanently on the website. The coordinator wants to allow pets in 15 rooms with a small $20 cleaning fee so families traveling with beloved pets feel welcome and included, but you want only 5 rooms with strict size rules and a $75 deposit to protect against property damage. Together, you must fill at least 18 of the 20 rooms for the holiday weekend.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence motivates welcoming more families with pets so people can travel with their beloved animals and feel cared for. Security motivates protecting the property and other guests from potential damage and disturbances through strict controls and financial safeguards.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents collaborate to fill at least 18 rooms for the profitable holiday weekend, while competing to have their pet policy adopted as the permanent standard featured on the website. The competition is for long-term policy control and business philosophy validation.

Scenario: You and the camp director must create one flashlight policy for all 8 cabins using your 20 flashlights. You want to give each cabin 2 flashlights now so younger campers feel safe walking to bathrooms at night, while the director wants to give only 1 per cabin and save 12 for emergencies. You're competing to get your recommended number adopted as the official policy that all staff must follow.
Scenario_rev: You and your organizing colleague must create one flashlight policy for all 8 cabins using your 20 flashlights. The camp director wants to give only 1 per cabin and save 12 for emergencies, while you want to give each cabin 2 flashlights now so younger campers feel safe walking to bathrooms at night. You're competing to get your recommended number adopted as the official policy that all staff must follow.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes immediate comfort and emotional safety for children who feel scared, while Security prioritizes maintaining reserves to handle potential power outages or emergencies.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both must agree on a single distribution policy that applies to all cabins, while competing to have their preferred allocation ratio adopted. If they cannot agree, no policy gets implemented and flashlights remain undistributed.

Scenario: You and the school counselor must create one re-entry plan for a student returning after missing 30 days for mental health treatment. You want flexible half-days with daily check-ins so the student doesn't feel overwhelmed, while the counselor wants required full days with weekly counseling to prevent falling behind. The principal will recognize whoever's approach becomes the core structure of the approved plan at the next staff meeting.
Scenario_rev: You and your planning colleague must create one re-entry plan for a student returning after missing 30 days for mental health treatment. The school counselor wants required full days with weekly counseling to prevent falling behind, while you want flexible half-days with daily check-ins so the student doesn't feel overwhelmed. The principal will recognize whoever's approach becomes the core structure of the approved plan at the next staff meeting.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes gentle emotional support and reducing stress for the struggling student, while Security prioritizes structured routines and monitoring to prevent academic failure or mental health relapse.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both must jointly create and sign one plan that determines the student's schedule, while competing for their approach to be the primary framework. Without agreement, the student cannot return to school on schedule.

Scenario: You and the neighborhood association president must design one garden layout for the empty lot using 12 raised beds. You want open shared beds where neighbors plant together and newcomers can join easily, while the president wants numbered beds with locks to prevent arguments over stolen vegetables. Whoever's design gets majority support from the five-person planning committee will be named lead garden coordinator with a small stipend.
Scenario_rev: You and your organizing neighbor must design one garden layout for the empty lot using 12 raised beds. The neighborhood association president wants numbered beds with locks to prevent arguments over stolen vegetables, while you want open shared beds where neighbors plant together and newcomers can join easily. Whoever's design gets majority support from the five-person planning committee will be named lead garden coordinator with a small stipend.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes building community connections and making everyone feel welcome through shared spaces, while Security prioritizes preventing theft and disputes through clear ownership boundaries.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both must submit one joint design to the planning committee but are competing for their layout approach to be adopted and for the coordinator recognition. If they submit competing designs, the committee will reject both and hire an outside landscape designer.

Scenario: You and the school bus driver must create one seating chart for the 40 students on your route. You want to seat shy kids with friendly classmates so they have someone to talk to and feel included, while the driver wants assigned seats with talkative students separated to prevent accidents during stops. The principal will implement whichever arrangement proves safer during the one-month trial period you both supervise.
Scenario_rev: You and your routing colleague must create one seating chart for the 40 students on your route. The school bus driver wants assigned seats with talkative students separated to prevent accidents during stops, while you want to seat shy kids with friendly classmates so they have someone to talk to and feel included. The principal will implement whichever arrangement proves safer during the one-month trial period you both supervise.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes reducing loneliness and helping children form supportive friendships during the bus ride, while Security prioritizes preventing injuries from students moving around or distracting the driver.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both must agree on one seating chart that the driver can enforce, while competing to prove their approach works better during the trial month. If they cannot agree, students choose their own seats daily, creating chaos.

Scenario: You and the volunteer coordinator must decide on one policy for the community meal program: accept anyone who arrives hungry without checking ID, or keep the current system where only registered members with ID cards get meals. You want to help homeless people and newcomers who don't have ID, while the coordinator wants to make sure the food doesn't run out for the 50 regular members. You must give the food bank board one recommendation tomorrow.
Scenario_rev: You and your program colleague must decide on one policy for the community meal program: accept anyone who arrives hungry without checking ID, or keep the current system where only registered members with ID cards get meals. The volunteer coordinator wants to make sure the food doesn't run out for the 50 regular members, while you want to help homeless people and newcomers who don't have ID. You must give the food bank board one recommendation tomorrow.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes helping anyone in need and making vulnerable people feel welcome without barriers, while Security prioritizes ensuring reliable food availability and preventing program collapse from overuse.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both must jointly recommend one policy that serves others rather than themselves, requiring compromise on deeply held values. The decision requires both to accept potential criticism from board members or community members for the policy they support.

Scenario: You and the youth services director must recommend how to use the vacant community center: create an open drop-in space where any teen can walk in for activities and support, or a structured after-school program requiring registration and supervised entry. You want a welcoming place where troubled teens can get help without paperwork, while the director wants controlled access to keep all participants safe. You must give the city council one proposal that determines how the $50,000 renovation budget is spent.
Scenario_rev: You and your planning colleague must recommend how to use the vacant community center: create an open drop-in space where any teen can walk in for activities and support, or a structured after-school program requiring registration and supervised entry. The youth services director wants controlled access to keep all participants safe, while you want a welcoming place where troubled teens can get help without paperwork. You must give the city council one proposal that determines how the $50,000 renovation budget is spent.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes removing barriers so at-risk and marginalized teens can access support without intimidating requirements, while Security prioritizes supervised environments to prevent incidents and protect all youth from potential harm.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both must jointly propose one design serving vulnerable youth rather than themselves, accepting potential blame if incidents occur or teens are excluded. The decision requires sacrifice of professional safety as they stake their reputations on the approach.

Scenario: You and the shop owner must decide how to use the last $500 from your fundraiser: donate it now to buy emergency blankets for homeless people before winter starts next week, or print flyers to attract more donors for a bigger campaign next month. You want to help people sleeping outside right now, while the owner wants to build a larger donor base for long-term support. You must tell the printer by tomorrow whether to proceed with the flyer order or cancel it.
Scenario_rev: You and your fundraising colleague must decide how to use the last $500 from your fundraiser: donate it now to buy emergency blankets for homeless people before winter starts next week, or print flyers to attract more donors for a bigger campaign next month. The shop owner wants to build a larger donor base for long-term support, while you want to help people sleeping outside right now. You must tell the printer by tomorrow whether to proceed with the flyer order or cancel it.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes immediate relief for people currently suffering in the cold, while Security prioritizes sustainable long-term funding to ensure the program can continue helping people reliably.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both must jointly decide how to allocate communal charitable funds for others' benefit, not their own. Each must accept the opportunity cost of not pursuing their preferred approach, and potential criticism from donors or beneficiaries.

Scenario: You and the HR director must recommend what to do about an employee who has missed performance targets for three quarters: move them to a training role with six months of coaching support, or end their employment to meet the budget cut that saves seven other team members' jobs. You want to give them another chance to improve and contribute, while the HR director wants to eliminate the position to protect the team's financial stability. You must give the executive committee one recommendation by Friday.
Scenario_rev: You and your management colleague must recommend what to do about an employee who has missed performance targets for three quarters: move them to a training role with six months of coaching support, or end their employment to meet the budget cut that saves seven other team members' jobs. The HR director wants to eliminate the position to protect the team's financial stability, while you want to give them another chance to improve and contribute. You must give the executive committee one recommendation by Friday.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes giving the struggling employee compassionate support and opportunity to recover their career, while Security prioritizes protecting the larger group's job stability and organizational financial health.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both must make a joint recommendation affecting others' welfare while accepting personal professional risk and emotional burden. Each must accept responsibility for either one person's job loss or potential team layoffs, sacrificing peace of mind regardless of the choice.

Scenario: You and the shelter manager must decide how to use 15 volunteer hours tomorrow: provide intense one-on-one job coaching for five residents at immediate risk of returning to the streets without employment, or run a basic resume workshop for all 15 residents to prevent complaints about unfair treatment. You want to focus help on those most at risk, while the manager wants to treat everyone equally to avoid conflict. You must tell the volunteer coordinator tonight which program to schedule.
Scenario_rev: You and your program colleague must decide how to use 15 volunteer hours tomorrow: provide intense one-on-one job coaching for five residents at immediate risk of returning to the streets without employment, or run a basic resume workshop for all 15 residents to prevent complaints about unfair treatment. The shelter manager wants to treat everyone equally to avoid conflict, while you want to focus help on those most at risk. You must tell the volunteer coordinator tonight which program to schedule.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes giving intensive care to those most vulnerable and in crisis even if others feel overlooked, while Security prioritizes maintaining shelter harmony and preventing resentment that could escalate into conflicts.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both must jointly decide how limited volunteer resources serve others rather than themselves, accepting responsibility for those who don't receive help. Each must accept potential criticism from residents or volunteers regardless of the choice made.

Scenario: You and the parent association treasurer must create one policy for the $2,000 school emergency fund: allow teachers to immediately give small amounts to students facing sudden hardships like lost lunch money or torn shoes, or require written applications reviewed weekly to make sure the money lasts all year. You want quick help available when children are upset or embarrassed, while the treasurer wants protected reserves so the fund never runs empty. You must give the principal one policy before the fund opens next week.
Scenario_rev: You and your administrative colleague must create one policy for the $2,000 school emergency fund: allow teachers to immediately give small amounts to students facing sudden hardships like lost lunch money or torn shoes, or require written applications reviewed weekly to make sure the money lasts all year. The parent association treasurer wants protected reserves so the fund never runs empty, while you want quick help available when children are upset or embarrassed. You must give the principal one policy before the fund opens next week.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes immediate compassionate response to children's acute distress and dignity, while Security prioritizes ensuring reliable fund availability throughout the year so all future needs can be met.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both must jointly create one policy serving students rather than themselves, accepting potential criticism if funds run out or children go without help. Each must accept responsibility for either fund depletion or children suffering during waiting periods.

Scenario: You and the library director must decide what to do with a donated rare history book worth $2,000: add it to the lending collection where students and community members can check it out for research, or keep it in the locked archives where it's protected but only available by appointment. You want the book accessible to help people with school projects and family history research, while the director wants it preserved from damage and potential loss. You must give the library board one decision that determines the book's placement permanently.
Scenario_rev: You and your administrative colleague must decide what to do with a donated rare history book worth $2,000: add it to the lending collection where students and community members can check it out for research, or keep it in the locked archives where it's protected but only available by appointment. The library director wants it preserved from damage and potential loss, while you want the book accessible to help people with school projects and family history research. You must give the library board one decision that determines the book's placement permanently.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes making knowledge accessible to anyone who needs it for learning and personal growth, while Security prioritizes protecting the irreplaceable resource from wear and loss to ensure future generations can access it.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both must jointly decide how a donated community resource serves others rather than themselves, accepting responsibility if the book deteriorates or remains underused. Each must accept potential criticism from donors, patrons, or board members for the placement chosen.

Scenario: You and the museum curator must decide whether to display donated wartime letters in an open exhibit where visitors can read them up close, or keep them in protective storage with only photographs available for viewing. You want the actual letters displayed so visitors can connect emotionally with the personal stories and handwriting, while the curator wants them preserved from light damage and handling. You must give the board one exhibition plan that determines how the letters are shown for the next five years.
Scenario_rev: You and your collections colleague must decide whether to display donated wartime letters in an open exhibit where visitors can read them up close, or keep them in protective storage with only photographs available for viewing. The museum curator wants them preserved from light damage and handling, while you want the actual letters displayed so visitors can connect emotionally with the personal stories and handwriting. You must give the board one exhibition plan that determines how the letters are shown for the next five years.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes creating emotional connections and meaningful learning experiences for current visitors through authentic artifacts, while Security prioritizes preserving fragile historical materials from deterioration so future generations can access them.
Cooperation Rationale: This is altruistic because both must jointly decide how donated artifacts serve the public rather than themselves, accepting responsibility if letters deteriorate or visitor engagement suffers. Each must accept potential criticism from donors, historians, or the public for the exhibition approach chosen.

Scenario: You and your neighbor Maria must plan a birthday dinner for 8 mutual friends. Maria wants to cancel the party because she feels unsafe having strangers in her apartment and anxious going to public places, but you believe the friends really need this gathering because several are lonely and haven't socialized in months. You must jointly decide whether to host the party at all and where.
Scenario_rev: You and your neighbor (the organizer) must plan a birthday dinner for 8 mutual friends. You want to cancel the party because you feel unsafe having strangers in your apartment and anxious going to public places, but your neighbor believes the friends really need this gathering because several are lonely and haven't socialized in months. You must jointly decide whether to host the party at all and where.
Conflict Rationale: Maria prioritizes her personal safety and emotional security by wanting to avoid the event entirely, while you prioritize the emotional wellbeing and social connection of friends who are isolated and need support.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because you both must make personal sacrifices (Maria facing discomfort, you investing significant effort) to provide social connection and emotional support for friends who need it, benefiting others rather than yourselves.

Scenario: Your friend Jordan offers you his spare room for 6 months after you lost your apartment. You want to pay low rent ($200 monthly) so you can save money to help your sister pay for her medical treatment, but Jordan needs higher rent ($500 monthly) to feel financially secure and cover his own bills. You must both agree on one rent amount in writing before you can move in next week.
Scenario_rev: Your friend (the apartment seeker) needs your spare room for 6 months after they lost their apartment. They want to pay low rent ($200 monthly) so they can save money to help their sister pay for her medical treatment, but you need higher rent ($500 monthly) to feel financially secure and cover your own bills. You must both agree on one rent amount in writing before they can move in next week.
Conflict Rationale: You prioritize helping your sister with her medical needs (caring for family welfare), while Jordan prioritizes his own financial stability and security through adequate rental income.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you sacrifice something (you pay rent when homeless, Jordan accepts a tenant with complications) to solve your housing crisis, and you must find a solution that addresses both the financial support for your sister and Jordan's needs.

Scenario: Your brother Tom needs you to co-sign a $15,000 bank loan for his food truck business, and you must both approve a spending plan before the bank releases funds. Tom wants to put $8,000 into a locked emergency savings account to feel financially protected, but you want to keep only $2,000 in savings so he has enough money to buy food supplies and help feed hungry customers in your low-income neighborhood. You must agree on one plan together.
Scenario_rev: Your brother Tom needs you to co-sign a $15,000 bank loan for his food truck business, and you must both approve a spending plan before the bank releases funds. You want to put $8,000 into a locked emergency savings account to feel financially protected, but your brother wants to keep only $2,000 in savings so you have enough money to buy food supplies and help feed hungry customers in your low-income neighborhood. You must agree on one plan together.
Conflict Rationale: Tom prioritizes personal financial security and protection against future uncertainty, while you prioritize using the money to serve and care for hungry people in the community who need affordable food.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because you take on significant personal financial risk by co-signing the loan and both must sacrifice preferred fund allocation to create a business plan that balances security with community service.

Scenario: You and your co-coordinator David run a volunteer program visiting lonely seniors, and you must create one official touching policy for all 12 volunteers to follow. David wants a strict no-touch rule to protect volunteers from getting sick with flu or COVID, but you want to allow hand-holding and brief hugs because many seniors are starving for human touch and it helps them feel cared for. You must both agree on one written policy by Friday.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-coordinator (the program organizer) run a volunteer program visiting lonely seniors, and you must create one official touching policy for all 12 volunteers to follow. You want a strict no-touch rule to protect volunteers from getting sick with flu or COVID, but your co-coordinator wants to allow hand-holding and brief hugs because many seniors are starving for human touch and it helps them feel cared for. You must both agree on one written policy by Friday.
Conflict Rationale: David prioritizes protecting volunteers from health and safety risks through strict boundaries, while you prioritize the emotional wellbeing and comfort of isolated seniors who desperately need physical human connection.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you sacrifice your preferred approach to create a shared policy that serves vulnerable seniors, balancing volunteer safety with senior emotional needs rather than benefiting yourselves directly.

Scenario: You and therapist Sarah must design a 10-week support group for 8 teens with anxiety, and submit one agreed program plan by Friday. Sarah wants structured sessions with safety rules and time limits to prevent emotional crises that could harm vulnerable teens, but you want open flexible sessions where teens can share deeply and support each other even if conversations become intense. Scenario_rev: You and program coordinator (the facilitator) must design a 10-week support group for 8 teens with anxiety, and submit one agreed program plan by Friday. You must both approve the final design together.
Conflict Rationale: Sarah prioritizes protecting teens from psychological harm through controlled safe boundaries, while you prioritize creating deep emotional connections and peer support that helps teens feel truly understood and cared for.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you invest significant professional effort and take on responsibility for vulnerable teens' wellbeing, sacrificing your preferred therapeutic approach to create one program that serves the participants' needs rather than your own preferences.

Scenario: You coordinate a school therapy program, and parent Rebecca must decide with you whether her calm golden retriever Max can visit the school twice weekly for 3-hour sessions with children who have autism. Rebecca worries that loud school environments and long travel will stress Max and harm his health, but you know that Max could provide crucial comfort to 6 children who feel scared and alone at school. Scenario_rev: You are a parent who must decide with the school therapy coordinator whether your calm golden retriever Max can visit the school twice weekly for 3-hour sessions with children who have autism. You worry that loud school environments and long travel will stress Max and harm his health, but the coordinator knows that Max could provide crucial comfort to 6 children who feel scared and alone at school. You both must agree together on whether Max joins the program.
Conflict Rationale: Rebecca prioritizes protecting Max from potential stress, exhaustion, and health risks to ensure her dog's safety and wellbeing, while you prioritize providing emotional comfort and support to isolated children who are struggling and need connection.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because Rebecca must potentially sacrifice her dog's comfort and shoulder ongoing responsibility, while you take on coordination duties, all to serve vulnerable children's emotional needs rather than your own direct benefit.

Scenario: You and your brother Kyle are applying for a $12,000 grant to start a landscaping business that will employ 3 jobless neighbors in your community. Kyle wants to take $4,000 immediately as salary to pay his overdue rent and feel financially stable, but you want to invest all funds in equipment so the business can succeed and provide jobs for your struggling neighbors. You must both submit one joint spending plan together to receive the grant.
Scenario_rev: You and your brother (the grant coordinator) are applying for a $12,000 grant to start a landscaping business that will employ 3 jobless neighbors in your community. You want to take $4,000 immediately as salary to pay your overdue rent and feel financially stable, but your brother wants to invest all funds in equipment so the business can succeed and provide jobs for your struggling neighbors. You must both submit one joint spending plan together to receive the grant.
Conflict Rationale: Kyle prioritizes his immediate personal financial security and stability through guaranteed income, while you prioritize helping unemployed neighbors by investing in business success that will provide jobs and support for people in need.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both of you undertake the risk and work of starting a business, sacrificing preferred fund allocation to create employment for others in the community rather than solely benefiting yourselves directly.

Scenario: You and your neighbor co-manage the community dog park and must jointly decide whether to ban pit bulls and rottweilers after one biting incident. Both of you must sign the new park rules since you're co-owners, and if you can't agree by Friday, the insurance company will cancel coverage and force the park to close. You want the ban to prevent future incidents, while your neighbor opposes breed discrimination.
Scenario_rev: You and your co-manager neighbor co-manage the community dog park and must jointly decide whether to ban pit bulls and rottweilers after one biting incident. Both of you must sign the new park rules since you're co-owners, and if you can't agree by Friday, the insurance company will cancel coverage and force the park to close. Your neighbor wants the ban to prevent future incidents, while you oppose breed discrimination.
Conflict Rationale: Security values protecting community members from potential harm through preventive rules. Universalism values equal treatment and opposes discrimination based on stereotypes about specific dog breeds.
Cooperation Rationale: Both agents must reach unanimous agreement to maintain insurance coverage. Both benefit equally from keeping the park open with valid insurance.

Scenario: You and your ex-partner must create one parenting schedule for your 8-year-old daughter that the court will review next week. You want her to live primarily with you where she has the same school, bedroom, and daily routines she's always known. Your ex-partner wants equal time in both homes so she experiences both parents' cultures and communities equally, even though it means changing schools mid-week.
Scenario_rev: You and your ex-partner must create one parenting schedule for your 8-year-old daughter that the court will review next week. Your ex-partner wants her to live primarily with them where she has the same school, bedroom, and daily routines she's always known. You want equal time in both homes so she experiences both parents' cultures and communities equally, even though it means changing schools mid-week.
Conflict Rationale: Security values maintaining stable routines, familiar environments, and minimizing disruption to the child's established life. Universalism values exposing the child to diverse experiences and ensuring equal access to both parents' different backgrounds.
Cooperation Rationale: Both parents must agree on one written schedule to avoid court-imposed arbitration. Both benefit from controlling the decision rather than having a judge decide.

Scenario: You are Emma's parent, and you and the school principal must both approve Emma's blog post before it goes on the school website this Friday. Scenario_rev: You are the school principal, and you and Emma's parent must both approve Emma's blog post before it goes on the school website this Friday. Conflict Rationale: Security values protecting the school's reputation and avoiding attention that might make families feel unsafe or concerned. Universalism values raising awareness about injustice and inequality to promote helping those in need.
Cooperation Rationale: Both you and the principal must approve the content before it can be published. Both benefit if the blog successfully represents the school well.

Scenario: You and your sister share care for your younger brother who has cerebral palsy, and you must both sign a contract to hire Maria, an immigrant home-care aide, for $3,000 from your family's $8,000 emergency fund. Scenario_rev: You and your sister share care for your younger brother who has cerebral palsy, and you must both sign a contract to hire Maria, an immigrant home-care aide, for $3,000 from your family's $8,000 emergency fund. You want to hire Maria because she needs work and has great experience, even though it reduces your safety cushion by more than half.
Conflict Rationale: Security values maintaining emergency resources to protect against unexpected medical crises and ensure family safety. Universalism values providing employment opportunities to immigrants and ensuring quality care through experienced workers regardless of background.
Cooperation Rationale: Both siblings must sign the contract since you jointly control the emergency fund account. Both benefit from ensuring your brother receives adequate care.

Scenario: You and your sister share custody of your nephew Noah, and you must both sign one enrollment form by Friday to register him for school. You want the nearby school with security guards, locked doors, and strict drop-off rules to keep Noah safe. Scenario: You and your partner must both sign the adoption agency paperwork by Friday, deciding whether to adopt internationally this year or wait indefinitely. Scenario: You and the park ranger are leading a family camping trip, and you must both agree on one campsite before darkness falls in one hour. You want Site A near the road where ambulances can reach you quickly if someone gets hurt. The ranger wants Site B two miles away from the protected wetland area where endangered birds are nesting, even though it has no cell phone service and steep trails that make emergency help difficult.
Scenario_rev: You are the park ranger, and you and the trip leader are leading a family camping trip, and you must both agree on one campsite before darkness falls in one hour. Scenario: You are a family therapist, and you and John must both agree on one meeting format for his family session this Friday about his recovery. You want John to only share information you've pre-approved to protect him from saying something that could damage his relationships permanently. Scenario: You and your cousin are launching an online craft store and must choose one payment system before the website goes live next week. One system requires government ID checks and blocks orders from countries with high fraud rates, keeping your money safe but excluding customers in poorer nations. Scenario: You and a neighbor are creating a neighborhood pet-safety program after three dogs were abused last month, and you're both competing for the 'Community Leader' award given to whoever gets the most votes from neighbors. Scenario: You and your classmate lead a science project to clean a polluted pond where fish are dying, and you're competing for the 'Best Team Leader' trophy that only one student wins. You must agree on one location: either hold the fair inside the gym with only your school's 200 students so teachers can supervise everyone and keep kids safe, or hold it on the big outdoor field and invite 400 students from three other schools to promote friendship and let more kids have fun. Conflict Rationale: Security values a controlled indoor environment where all students can be supervised and protected from strangers or accidents. Universalism values creating inclusive community events that welcome all children regardless of which school they attend.
Cooperation Rationale: Both classmates must submit one jointly-signed plan for the same event and execute it together. They compete for individual recognition through the organizer badge based on how teachers evaluate their planning decisions.

Scenario: You and a wildlife advocate must design one nature education program for Riverside Park's summer season, choosing between 15 small guided tours on paved paths with security cameras near parking areas, or 8 larger tours on remote trails with closer wildlife viewing but no surveillance. You both compete for the 'Lead Educator' title printed on all park materials, which determines who gets priority for next year's expanded program budget.
Scenario_rev: You are a wildlife advocate and must design one nature education program for Riverside Park's summer season with your park administrator. Conflict Rationale: Security values protected, monitored spaces with predictable access, while Universalism prioritizes authentic nature connection and wildlife protection even without safety infrastructure. One agent must compromise their core concern to create the unified program.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents must collaborate to design a single program that the park will implement, while simultaneously competing for the Lead Educator recognition. Both the collaborative program design and individual competition for credit are explicit and necessary.

Scenario: You and your coworker must create one commuting policy proposal for your 12-person office, choosing between a $200/month bike-sharing subsidy or a $200/month secure parking garage stipend. You must submit one unified proposal to management by Friday, and only one of you will be named lead author in the company newsletter. Management will only fund one transportation benefit.

Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both agents must collaborate to create one unified proposal that management will accept, while competing for lead authorship recognition in the newsletter. Both collaborative and competitive elements directly affect the agents.

Scenario: You and your coworker must decide whether to carpool in their older gas-powered car to reach an urgent client meeting 40 miles away after the train system shuts down due to flooding. Scenario_rev: You and your coworker must decide whether to carpool in your older gas-powered car to reach an urgent client meeting 40 miles away after the train system shuts down due to flooding. Scenario: You and your neighbor must create one pest-control recommendation for your 24-unit building after fleas spread to eight apartments. Scenario: You and your partner must decide whether to switch to a green energy plan that costs $60 more monthly, requiring you both to reduce your emergency savings contributions by $30 each. Scenario: You and John's public defender must co-write one appeal report by Friday, deciding whether to emphasize specific procedural errors in John's case that the review board typically accepts, or argue broader systemic justice failures that could help many future defendants but risk rejection. Scenario: You and your partner must decide whether to rent a 900-square-foot eco-friendly apartment in a diverse neighborhood for $1,800/month, or a 1,200-square-foot home in a gated community with security cameras and keycard entry for $2,200/month. Cooperation Rationale: Both must agree on the decision and commit to either opening the closed section (requiring extra work) or explaining the policy to an upset customer (risking a complaint). This is Altrusive because both sacrifice time and effort, and risk consequences (extra work or bad reviews) to resolve the situation.

Scenario: You and your ex-partner must create Noah's after-school schedule together, choosing between three days of free time at home where he can relax and talk with you both, or three days of soccer and chess club that his teacher says he should join. You both must drive him to different places on different days and change your work hours, and one of you must give up what you think is best for Noah.
Scenario_rev: You and Noah's other parent must create Noah's after-school schedule together, choosing between three days of free time at home where he can relax and talk with you both, or three days of soccer and chess club that his teacher says he should join. Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes Noah's emotional comfort, relationship bonding, and honoring his feelings about what he needs. Conformity values following the school counselor's expert recommendations and structured activities.
Cooperation Rationale: Both parents must agree on one schedule and both must sacrifice personal convenience through driving and work changes. This is Altrusive because both give up time and flexibility, and one must compromise their parenting philosophy for Noah's benefit.

Scenario: You and your sibling must decide together whether to give your four nephews regular birthday and Christmas presents like always, or start a college savings fund instead and tell the family at next month's dinner. The fund means you both pay $100 every month for years and your relatives might be upset that the kids get no presents to open, but it helps the children's future.
Scenario_rev: You and your sibling must decide together whether to give your four nephews regular birthday and Christmas presents like always, or start a college savings fund instead and tell the family at next month's dinner. Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes the children's long-term wellbeing and financial security for their future. Conformity values maintaining family gift-giving traditions and avoiding criticism from relatives who expect regular presents.
Cooperation Rationale: Both must agree on the approach and announce it together, and both must commit to monthly payments for years. This is Altrusive because both sacrifice money and face potential family disapproval to benefit the nephews' future.

Scenario: You and Jake's older sister must plan an intervention for Jake, whose drinking is causing problems at work, deciding whether to talk to him openly at home sharing personal feelings, or meet at a coffee shop following a written script about specific behavior changes. Cooperation Rationale: Both must agree on the intervention approach and both must commit to months of counseling attendance. This is Altrusive because both sacrifice significant personal time and emotional energy to help Jake, regardless of which approach is chosen.

Scenario: You and your adult child's partner must plan together how to meet your new grandchild for the first time, choosing between a long home visit where you can hold and play with the baby freely, or a short one-hour meeting at a park with rules about handwashing and holding time. Cooperation Rationale: Both must agree on one meeting plan and both must travel far and sacrifice work time. This is Altrusive because both give up significant time and convenience to meet the baby, regardless of which approach is chosen.

Scenario: You and your spouse must write a plan together for handling your eight-year-old daughter's classroom outbursts, choosing between talking with her about feelings and flexible consequences, or a strict point system where she loses privileges for each outburst like the principal wants. Scenario: You and Maya's sister must plan a surprise birthday celebration for Maya, who is feeling lonely after moving to a new city, choosing between an intimate afternoon with just you three doing her favorite activities and homemade food, or inviting all her new coworkers to a regular restaurant party to help her make friends. Scenario_rev: You and Maya's sibling must plan a surprise birthday celebration for Maya, who is feeling lonely after moving to a new city, choosing between an intimate afternoon with just you three doing her favorite activities and homemade food, or inviting all her new coworkers to a regular restaurant party to help her make friends. Scenario: You and Marcus must decide whether to visit a grieving family tomorrow for many hours to cook dinner, clean, and help with chores, or make a short visit with a store-bought meal since they said 'please don't make a fuss.' Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes providing substantial practical care and support because the family truly needs help despite what they say. Conformity values respecting the family's stated wishes and following social norms about giving grieving people space.
Cooperation Rationale: Both must agree on visit length and both must give up personal plans and money. This is Altrusive because both sacrifice time and resources to help the family, and risk making them uncomfortable regardless of the choice.

Scenario: You and another parent at the beach see a child about six years old throwing sand at other families while his parents sit far away on their phones, and you must decide together right now whether to walk over and gently talk to the child about why sand-throwing hurts others, or go tell the lifeguard to handle it. Scenario_rev: You and another parent at the beach see a child about six years old throwing sand at other families while his parents sit far away on their phones, and you must decide together right now whether to walk over and gently talk to the child about why sand-throwing hurts others, or go tell the lifeguard to handle it. Either way means leaving your things alone, stopping your relaxation, and possibly upsetting the child's parents who might see you.
Conflict Rationale: Benevolence prioritizes directly helping and teaching the child with kindness and personal connection. Conformity values following proper beach protocols by reporting to the authority figure (lifeguard) rather than intervening directly.

Scenario: You and the HR director must create a new mentorship program matching eight managers with junior staff, choosing between flexible meetings where mentors and mentees talk about personal career goals and feelings, or scheduled monthly meetings with required topics and evaluation forms. Scenario: You and David's younger sibling must plan an intervention because David's daily drinking is affecting his custody of his daughter, choosing between an emotional conversation at his home where you share personal stories about how his drinking hurts you, or a meeting at a neutral place following a written script about behavior changes. Scenario: You and your roommate are hosting dinner for eight guests tomorrow and must decide together whether to cook a traditional turkey meal that most guests expect, or make halal chicken, vegetarian options, and different sides so everyone can eat based on their dietary needs. Scenario_rev: You and your roommate are hosting dinner for eight guests tomorrow and must decide together whether to cook a traditional turkey meal that most guests expect, or make halal chicken, vegetarian options, and different sides so everyone can eat based on their dietary needs. Scenario: You and a volunteer coordinator are planning a support schedule for elderly neighbors during a heat wave. You both must decide on one approach for the entire volunteer team to follow.
Conflict Rationale: Your Benevolence drives you to provide direct, personal care to help vulnerable individuals even if it means breaking protocol. The coordinator's Conformity prioritizes following established safety rules and official procedures set by the community center.
Cooperation Rationale: This is Altrusive because both you and the coordinator are volunteering your time and effort to help others without personal benefit. The decision requires potential sacrifice of following comfortable, approved procedures to provide more effective care to those in need.

Scenario: You and another parent on the resort safety committee must write one set of pool rules for the resort newsletter by Friday. You want to recommend the standard rule that all children under 12 must have an adult in the water with them, matching what other resorts do. The other parent wants to recommend flexible guidelines that let families decide based on their child's swimming ability, to be fair to families from cultures where children learn independence earlier.
Scenario_rev: You and another parent on the resort safety committee must write one set of pool rules for the resort newsletter by Friday. Conflict Rationale: You want to follow the standard safety rules that most resorts use (Conformity), while the other parent wants rules that respect all families' different backgrounds and needs equally (Universalism).
Cooperation Rationale: You must both agree on and submit the same recommendation to the newsletter, and both benefit from having clear safety guidelines published (Reciprocal).

Scenario: You and your roommate Jake must write one message to your friend Emily about visiting your dorm room less often, since she's been coming every evening for three weeks. Jake wants to offer Emily continued support and suggest she can also talk to the campus counseling center, recognizing she needs help during her hard time. Scenario_rev: You and your roommate Jake must write one message to your friend Emily about visiting your dorm room less often, since she's been coming every evening for three weeks. Scenario: You and your neighbor Lily must create one set of borrowing rules for the $800 worth of power tools you bought together last month. Cooperation Rationale: You both own the tools together and must agree on the same borrowing rules to post, and both benefit from having a clear system that protects your shared investment (Reciprocal).

Scenario: You and the community center director must pick one mentorship program to start next Monday using the meeting room you both reserved. Scenario: You and your co-moderator must decide by tonight whether to approve a post in your 5,000-member parenting group about unfair funding gaps between rich and poor schools in your county. Your co-moderator wants to approve it because it shows important inequality that hurts many children's education. Scenario_rev: You and your co-moderator must decide by tonight whether to approve a post in your 5,000-member parenting group about unfair funding gaps between rich and poor schools in your county. Scenario: You and your two siblings must decide on one gift plan for your 12-person family holiday dinner in two weeks. Scenario: You and the vice principal must decide by Friday which classroom format all four 8th-grade social studies teachers will use starting next month. Scenario: You and the community center coordinator must pick one performance group for next month's arts showcase by signing a contract this week. The coordinator wants to hire a modern dance group that tells stories about people from different backgrounds including LGBTQ+ experiences, helping audience members understand diverse lives. Scenario: You and your classmate must pick one movie for the school movie night you're organizing for 100 students, and submit your choice to the principal by Friday. Your classmate wants to show a documentary about refugee children fleeing war, teaching students about inequality and suffering that many children face around the world. Scenario: You and your roommate must decide on one format for the weekly chemistry study group you're hosting in your dorm room starting tomorrow for six struggling classmates. Your roommate wants an open format where everyone shares their confusions freely and students who understand less get extra help and explanation time. Scenario: You and the chair of your neighborhood volunteer group must choose one project for the $5,000 city grant application due next week, and both co-directors must sign it. Scenario: You and your roommate must decide right now which glasses to use for the dinner party you're hosting tonight for 20 guests at your shared apartment. Scenario: You and your teammate must create one team chant for the regional sports tournament opening ceremony by Friday. The coach expects you to use the traditional school chant performed for 20 years, but your teammate wants to write new words that welcome all visiting teams equally and avoid language that could exclude international players. The coach will select one of you to lead the chant performance in front of 500 people at the ceremony.

Conflict Rationale: Conformity values preserving the traditional chant that maintains established school customs. Universalism values creating inclusive language that respects all teams regardless of their background.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both must collaborate to produce one chant together, while competing for the individual recognition of being chosen as the lead performer. Both the collaborative goal (creating the chant) and competitive element (lead performer selection) are clearly present.

Scenario: You and another student captain must select one experiment for your science club to present at the citywide STEM fair next month, where only one project per school is allowed. Conflict Rationale: Conformity values following the established curriculum and proven experiment that meets teacher expectations. Universalism values addressing environmental injustice and helping underserved communities through research.
Cooperation Rationale: This is coopetitive because both must collaborate to select and present one experiment together, while competing for individual recognition through the Lead Researcher certificate. The competition is for the certificate based on whose approach is judged more valuable.

Scenario: You and the volunteer coordinator must design one community service project for your neighborhood group to complete this summer, and the local foundation will give $2,000 to fund it. Conflict Rationale: Conformity values maintaining the organization's established membership rules and traditions. Universalism values including everyone regardless of background and expanding access to all community members.

Scenario: You and your friend are chosen as bus monitors for the class field trip to the museum next Tuesday, and you must create one seating chart for all 40 students.
